confirmed.title: findDataFileIdsInRange does string-compares int128/uint128 stats, silently pruning matching files
confirmed.detail: isNumericType() recognizes int8/int16/int32/int64, uint8/16/32/64, float32/float64 and decimal(...), but NOT the canonical DuckLake types int128 and uint128 (DucklakeTypeConverter maps these to DECIMAL(38,0)/VARCHAR; the stored column_type strings are literally "int128"/"uint128"). For an int128/uint128 column, parseStatValue() therefore returns the raw String, and isWithinBounds()/compareValues() compare numeric values lexicographically. findDataFileIdsInRange is used by DucklakeSplitManager.pruneDataFiles as a hard prune (candidateFileIds.retainAll(matchingFileIds), line 305). Lexicographic comparison wrongly judges range overlap: e.g. column int128 with file stats min="2", max="100" and predicate value "9" -> lowerBound="9", maxStat="100", compareValues("9","100")>0 -> file pruned even though 9 is in [2,100]. The file that actually contains matching rows is dropped, so the query silently returns wrong (missing) rows.
confirmed.suggestion: Add "int128", "uint128" (and "hugeint"/"uhugeint" if ever stored) to isNumericType so they parse as BigDecimal. Better: drive parseStatValue off the same canonical type vocabulary used everywhere else (int8..int128, float32/64, decimal(...)), and treat any unknown numeric-looking type by attempting BigDecimal before falling back to String.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced. isNumericType (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2445-2458) matches int8/16/32/64, uint8/16/32/64, float32/64, decimal( — but NOT "int128"/"uint128". DucklakeTypeConverter.java:130-136 confirms the canonical DuckLake column_type strings are literally "int128"/"uint128" (mapping to DECIMAL(38,0)/VARCHAR). So in parseStatValue (line 2431) isNumericType("int128") returns false, the raw String is returned, and isWithinBounds/compareValues (lines 2471-2499) compare lexicographically.

This is a HARD prune: findDataFileIdsInRange (line 456-499) is called by DucklakeSplitManager.pruneDataFiles, which does candidateFileIds.retainAll(matchingFileIds) (DucklakeSplitManager.java:305) — files not returned are dropped entirely.

The finding's example is exact. For an int128 column, stored min/max are plain decimal digit strings (DucklakeStatsExtractor.java:179-182 emits decimal.toPlainString(); a DuckDB-authored catalog likewise stores textual numeric min/max), and the predicate bound comes from normalizePredicateValue -> value.toString() (DucklakeSplitManager.java:407-416), also plain digits. With file stats min="2", max="100" and predicate x=9: isWithinBounds(low="9", high="9", min="2", max="100") -> compareValues("9","100") = "9".compareTo("100") = positive (char '9'>'1') -> lowerVsMax>0 -> returns false -> file pruned even though 9 is in [2,100]. Query silently loses matching rows.

Two scoping caveats (do not refute, but narrow the blast radius): (1) When Trino itself creates the column, toDucklakeType maps DECIMAL(38,0) back to "decimal(38,0)" (DucklakeTypeConverter.java:215-217), which isNumericType DOES match — so the bug only bites cross-engine int128 columns authored by DuckDB as native HUGEINT, stored as column_type="int128". Given this is explicitly a cross-engine shared-catalog connector, that path is real and reachable. (2) uint128 -> VARCHAR is internally consistent (string-typed end to end, lexicographic compare is correct VARCHAR semantics), so the practical silent-wrong-rows hazard is primarily int128 (and the hugeint/uhugeint aliases the suggestion notes). Not covered by any INTENTIONAL DECISION or KNOWN GAP in the briefing; the "Conservative stats suppression" item is about getColumnStats/getTableStats returning empty, a separate path from findDataFileIdsInRange. Severity high stands for silent incorrect results.

confirmed.title: typedCompare uses non-existent type names, so getColumnStats min/max for all numeric/float columns is always lexicographic
confirmed.detail: typedCompare switches on "bigint","integer","int","smallint","tinyint","hugeint" / "double","real","float","decimal". None of these are the strings DuckLake actually stores in ducklake_column.column_type. Per DucklakeTypeConverter the canonical strings are int8/int16/int32/int64, float32/float64 and decimal(p,s) (with parentheses). So every numeric and floating-point case falls through to the default branch (a.compareTo(b), string comparison). typedMin/typedMax (used to merge per-file MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE across data files in getColumnStats) therefore compute table-level min/max lexicographically: for an int64 column two files with mins "9" and "10" yield min "10" ("10"<"9" as strings). The aggregated column min/max returned to Trino's optimizer is wrong whenever a table has >1 data file. Only "date" actually matches.
confirmed.suggestion: Switch typedCompare on the canonical DuckLake type names (int8/int16/int32/int64/int128 -> BigInteger or BigDecimal compare; float32/float64 -> Double; decimal(...) -> BigDecimal; date/timestamp* -> temporal parse). Reuse the same parsing path as parseStatValue/isNumericType so the two code paths can't drift again.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced. The canonical strings stored in ducklake_column.column_type are int8/int16/int32/int64/int128, uint8..uint128, float32/float64, decimal(p,s) (with parens), date, timestamp*. This is proven by two independent sources in the codebase: (1) DucklakeTypeConverter.java write side (lines 198-216) emits exactly "int8"/"int16"/"int32"/"int64"/"float32"/"float64"/format("decimal(%d,%d)") into COLUMN_TYPE, and int128 -> "int128" (line 135), and the read side (lines 118-149) switches on those same strings; (2) JdbcDucklakeCatalog's own parseStatValue/isNumericType (lines 2445-2458) is built around int8/int16/int32/int64/uint*/float32/float64/startsWith("decimal(").

typedCompare (lines 587-595) switches on "bigint","integer","int","smallint","tinyint","hugeint" and "double","real","float","decimal" (no parens). None of these match the actual stored values. "decimal" fails because stored values carry parens (decimal(p,s)); "hugeint" fails because int128 is stored as "int128". Only the "date" case (line 592) matches a real stored value. Every integer/float/decimal column therefore hits default -> a.compareTo(b) (lexicographic). The merge functions typedMin/typedMax (lines 574-582) are used in getColumnStats (lines 550,553) to aggregate per-file MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE across data files, so for a multi-data-file table the aggregated min/max is computed lexicographically (e.g. int64 mins "9" and "10" -> min becomes "10").

columnType reaching typedCompare is the raw COLUMN_TYPE value: getColumnStats builds columnTypes from DucklakeColumn::columnType (lines 514-515); resolveColumnType returns scalar types unchanged (default branch, line 2419). No normalization.

Consequence confirmed downstream: DucklakeMetadata.toDoubleRange (lines 535-555) consumes these aggregated min/max strings for BIGINT/INTEGER/SMALLINT/TINYINT/DOUBLE/REAL/DATE and feeds a DoubleRange into Trino ColumnStatistics via setRange (line 492), so wrong ranges reach the optimizer (inverted min/max either gives a wrong range or, if min>max, is silently dropped by the catch at line 557).

Scope caveats that bound severity (but do not refute): (a) only manifests with >1 data file — single-file tables emit the writer's already-correct per-file min/max with no merge; (b) the briefing documents conservative stats suppression when delete files exist / formats are mixed, narrowing exposure; (c) actual filter-pushdown pruning uses the SEPARATE, correct parseStatValue path (lines 461-496), so this corrupts only optimizer cardinality/range estimates, NOT query results. Net: a real, silent, broad (all numeric/float/decimal multi-file tables) stats-correctness bug affecting plan quality. The suggested fix (switch typedCompare on the canonical names, reusing parseStatValue/isNumericType) is correct.

confirmed.title: applyDeleteFragments never end-snapshots a prior active delete file for the same data_file_id
confirmed.detail: applyDeleteFragments only INSERTs new ducklake_delete_file rows (begin_snapshot = new snapshot, end_snapshot = NULL) and never end-snapshots any pre-existing active delete file for the same data_file_id; DucklakeDeleteFragment carries no reference to the prior delete file to retire it, and the only place delete files are ever end-snapshotted is dropTable (line 1779). When a second DELETE/MERGE touches a data file that already has an active delete file from an earlier snapshot, two delete-file rows for that data_file_id are simultaneously active. getDataFiles() LEFT JOINs ducklake_delete_file with activeAt(delfile, snapshotId) (lines 412-414), so that data file now matches two delete rows and is emitted twice; DucklakeDataFile carries a single Optional deletePath, so the second copy's deletePath is whichever the join orders last. This is the exact "don't stack concurrent delete files / select the single currently-valid delete file" hazard called out in the briefing's UPSTREAM BUG WATCH, and it produces duplicate data-file scans and/or the wrong delete file being applied.
confirmed.suggestion: Before inserting a new delete file, UPDATE ducklake_delete_file SET end_snapshot = newSnapshot WHERE data_file_id = ? AND end_snapshot IS NULL (the spec's at-most-one-delete-file-per-data-file invariant). Confirm the merge sink merges prior delete positions into the rewritten file so retiring the old row doesn't drop earlier deletions.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The core defect is real: applyDeleteFragments (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2223-2260) only batch-INSERTs new ducklake_delete_file rows with begin_snapshot=new, end_snapshot=NULL (lines 2234-2251). It never end-snapshots or deletes a pre-existing active delete file for the same data_file_id, and DucklakeDeleteFragment (DucklakeDeleteFragment.java:25-31) carries no reference to a prior delete file to retire. The only place delete files are end-snapshotted is dropTable (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1780-1785). The merge sink compounds this: DucklakeMergeSink.writeDeleteFile (DucklakeMergeSink.java:169-226) writes a delete file containing ONLY the row IDs collected in the current operation, and those rows came from a read page source that already filtered prior deletes (applyDeleteFile at DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:544/624). So a second DELETE on a data file produces a delete file disjoint from (not a superset of) the first, and BOTH rows stay active. This violates the DuckLake spec invariant, which the authoritative C++ extension enforces by RETIRING the prior row: at commit it collects overwritten_delete_files and DeleteOverwrittenDeleteFiles (ducklake_metadata_manager.cpp:3322-3353) issues "DELETE FROM ducklake_delete_file WHERE delete_file_id IN (...)" plus schedules the old file for disk deletion — at most one active delete file per data file per snapshot.

HOWEVER, the finding's two stated consequences are mostly WRONG for this connector's own reader. getDataFiles does LEFT JOIN with activeAt (lines 412-414) and CAN return two rows per data_file_id, but DucklakeSplitManager.getSplits explicitly groups by dataFileId (lines 191-200, "a data file can accumulate multiple delete files across snapshots") into ONE merged split, and createMergedSplit (lines 542-600) collects ALL delete paths into DucklakeSplit.deleteFilePaths (a List, line 588). applyDeleteFile (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:667-701) loops over every path (line 682) and UNIONs all positions. So this connector does NOT double-scan and does NOT apply "whichever the join orders last" — both are robustly merged. The real harm is cross-engine: a catalog this connector writes with stacked active delete files corrupts reads in the Rust reference (metadata_provider_mysql.rs:196-203 LEFT JOINs the same way and emits a per-delete-row ParquetExec, so the data file is scanned twice) and any other spec-conformant engine, and interacts badly with the partial_max/compaction gap flagged in the briefing. This is exactly the "don't stack concurrent delete files" hazard in UPSTREAM BUG WATCH. No test exercises a second DELETE on an already-deleted data file. Severity stays high because DuckLake is a cross-engine shared-catalog format and this silently produces a non-conformant catalog, but the finding's self-reader duplicate-scan mechanism is inaccurate.

confirmed.title: Delete-row filter mis-aligns file positions when the Parquet predicate prunes row groups/pages
confirmed.detail: createParquetPageSource builds the ParquetReader with appendRowNumberColumn=false and passes a real predicate (parquetTupleDomain / parquetPredicate derived from fileStatisticsDomain ∩ dynamicFilter). getFilteredRowGroups (PredicateUtils line 191+) only adds row groups whose stats match, and column-index/page filtering can further drop rows inside a surviving row group. The pages the reader then emits are dense over the SURVIVING rows. But DeleteRowFilterTransform.apply derives each row's file position by counting positions contiguously from 0 (nextRowOffset += positionCount) and computing rowOffset/rowId from that count. When any row group or page is pruned, the emitted positions skip ahead while nextRowOffset does not, so every subsequent rowOffset/rowId is shifted. Deleted-row positions (file-local offsets and rowIdStart+offset global ids) are then compared against the wrong rows: live rows get dropped and/or deleted rows get returned. This corrupts results for any SELECT that has both positional deletes (parquet delete files or inlined deletes) AND a predicate that prunes — i.e. the common merge-on-read + WHERE/dynamic-filter case. ParquetReader already exposes the correct absolute file position via firstRowIndexInGroup+nextRowInGroup (the appendRowNumberColumn path, ParquetReader line 450).
confirmed.suggestion: Do not re-derive file positions by counting. Construct the ParquetReader with appendRowNumberColumn=true, read the appended row-number block, and have DeleteRowFilterTransform (and RowIdInjectingPageSource) key off that true file row number instead of a running counter. Alternatively, when deletes are present, suppress predicate-based row-group/page pruning so positions stay dense — but the row-number approach is correct and preserves pushdown.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding's mechanism is independently reproduced from the code.

1. createParquetPageSource (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:520-532) builds the ParquetReader with appendRowNumberColumn=false and passes a live predicate: line 530 passes Optional.of(parquetPredicate) whenever parquetTupleDomain is not all, and line 433-443 builds rowGroups via getFilteredRowGroups with that same predicate.

2. The predicate is real and prunable: effectivePredicate = ducklakeSplit.fileStatisticsDomain().intersect(dynamicFilterPredicate) (lines 168-169). toParquetTupleDomain (835-872) maps non-trivial domains to a TupleDomain<ColumnDescriptor>. The dynamic filter is a read-time source of pruning not captured in split-time stats.

3. getFilteredRowGroups (PredicateUtils.java:191-228) only adds a row group when predicateMatches returns true — it drops non-matching row groups entirely. Surviving groups carry their true block.fileRowCountOffset() in RowGroupInfo.fileRowOffset(), and column-index page filtering can further reduce currentGroupRowCount within a surviving group (ParquetReader.java:490-501). The emitted page stream is therefore dense over surviving rows, with positions NOT equal to absolute file-row indices.

4. DeleteRowFilterTransform.apply (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:920-945) derives each row's position purely by a running counter: rowOffset = nextRowOffset + position, rowId = rowIdStart + rowOffset, then nextRowOffset += positionCount (line 938). It has no knowledge of pruned row groups/pages. RowIdInjectingPageSource (line 1007) uses the identical contiguous-counter pattern for the $row_id used by DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE.

5. Delete positions are absolute file-row identities, not surviving-row indices: split.inlinedDeletedRowPositions() are file-local offsets and parquet delete files store rowIdStart+file_row_number (applyDeleteFile comments at 676-680, and DucklakeSplitManager:582). So once any row group/page is pruned, the contiguous counter shifts and every subsequent rowOffset/rowId is compared against the wrong file rows — dropping live rows and/or retaining deleted ones.

No guard couples deletes to predicate suppression: applyDeleteFile (667-701) installs the transform unconditionally whenever deletes exist, regardless of the predicate passed to the reader.

The briefing's UPSTREAM BUG WATCH explicitly asks to verify exactly this (DeleteRowFilterTransform applies positions correctly per page) and is NOT listed as an intentional decision or known gap. Test coverage confirms the hole: TestDeleteRowFilterTransformOverlap uses a single 20-row page with no pruning, so it never exercises a pruned-row-group/page scenario. ParquetReader already exposes the correct absolute position via firstRowIndexInGroup + the appendRowNumberColumn path (createRowNumberBlock at ParquetReader.java:417-425, lastBatchStartRow at 448-451), so the suggested fix is sound and matches Trino's Iceberg positional-delete approach.

Severity stays high: silently wrong query results (lost/extra rows) for the common merge-on-read + WHERE / dynamic-filter case on multi-row-group files. It does not manifest on small single-row-group files where all pages survive, which is why functional tests likely pass — making the latent corruption more dangerous, not less.

confirmed.title: cleanupAfterFailure closes Arrow allocator/stream/connection while the consumer INSERT may still be running natively
confirmed.detail: cleanupAfterFailure() interrupts consumerThread and then immediately closes arrayStream, reader, allocator and connection. The consumer thread runs DuckDB's INSERT via stmt.execute(), which drives the exported ArrowArrayStream and calls back into reader.loadNextBatch() to read Arrow buffers from the allocator. Thread.interrupt() only unblocks the interruptible pageQueue.poll(); if the consumer is parked inside the native DuckDB execute() call (the common case for a long-running INSERT) the interrupt is ignored, the join in abort()/finishAndBuildFragment can time out, and then this method frees the RootAllocator, the C-data ArrowArrayStream, and the JDBC connection out from under the still-running native consumer. That is a use-after-close of native Arrow/DuckDB memory — undefined behaviour up to a JVM crash. This is reachable on both the finishAndBuildFragment join-timeout path (446-450) and the abort() join-timeout path (657-664).
confirmed.suggestion: Do not release Arrow/DuckDB resources while the consumer thread is still alive. After the join times out, either keep the resources pinned and leak them deliberately (logging loudly) rather than freeing them under a live native reader, or cancel the running statement via Statement.cancel()/connection-level abort and confirm the consumer thread has actually terminated (join returned true) before any closeQuietly(arrayStream/allocator/connection).
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The architecture (lines 232-250, 691-757) runs the INSERT on a daemon consumer thread via stmt.execute() (native DuckDB), which drives QueueArrowReader.loadNextBatch() (line 703) — that method blocks on pageQueue.poll(100ms), the only interruptible point. consumerError (line 246) is set only AFTER execute() returns/throws, so while the native INSERT is in flight the thread is alive and consumerError is null.

cleanupAfterFailure() (624-637) calls consumerThread.interrupt() then unconditionally closeQuietly(arrayStream/reader/allocator/connection) regardless of whether the thread actually terminated. Thread.interrupt() only unblocks a thread parked in the JVM poll(); a thread parked inside the native DuckDB execute() (e.g. a long flush/finalize that isn't currently calling back into loadNextBatch) ignores the interrupt. There is no join-succeeded guard before the closes.

This method is reached on two timeout paths where the consumer is presumably NOT in the interruptible poll: finishAndBuildFragment's join(600s) timeout (446-450) and abort()'s join(5_000ms) timeout (657-664). The abort path is the realistic trigger — a 5s join during an in-progress native INSERT will plausibly time out, after which arrayStream (C-data stream), connection (DuckDB JDBC native connection), and allocator are freed under a still-live native reader. Closing the native connection / C-data stream while DuckDB executes on another thread is concurrent free of memory the native thread may dereference — undefined behaviour up to a JVM crash, exactly as claimed. (One partial mitigation: RootAllocator.close() throws IllegalStateException when buffers are still outstanding and closeQuietly swallows it, so the allocator may not actually free; but the connection/arrayStream closes remain unsafe, so the hazard stands.)

Not covered by the briefing: the DuckDB-format/arrow_stream path is flagged EXPERIMENTAL, but that clause only excludes 'churn/instability' (API-surface evolution), not a substantive use-after-free crash. The suggested fix (only free after join returns true, or cancel the statement and confirm termination) addresses a real gap. Confidence is medium because the trigger requires the native consumer to be outside the poll() callback during the join window, and the exact native-side behaviour on connection.close() during execute() depends on DuckDB JDBC internals not inspectable here; but the unconditional free under a possibly-live native thread is real in the code.

confirmed.title: $divide pushed as DuckDB '/' silently changes integer-division semantics, dropping rows
confirmed.detail: arithmeticOperator() maps Trino's $divide to DuckDB's '/' for ALL numeric types with no type gate, and the comment explicitly asserts the engines 'align on integer ... arithmetic semantics'. They do not. For integer operands DuckDB's '/' performs floating-point division (5/2 = 2.5; DuckDB uses '//' or div() for truncating integer division), whereas Trino's $divide on integers truncates toward zero (5/2 = 2). Because applyFilter pushes the translated conjunct into the DuckDB WHERE clause for .db splits, a predicate like WHERE a/2 = 3 with a=7 evaluates to 3=3 (true) in Trino but 3.5=3 (false) in DuckDB, so the row is filtered out at the scan. The dual-emission safety net (re-evaluating the same expression in remainingExpression) can only further restrict rows above the scan, never re-add rows DuckDB already dropped, so this is an uncorrected false-negative / wrong-results bug, not just a perf concern. A related divergence in the same mapping: Trino throws DIVISION_BY_ZERO (and integer-overflow) errors where DuckDB returns NULL for x/0, so the pushed predicate silently filters instead of erroring.
confirmed.suggestion: Do not push $divide (and reconsider $modulo) without a type gate. Emit DuckDB '//' (or div()) when both operand types are integral, and only emit '/' for floating-point operands; alternatively add an ArgTypeGate that restricts arithmetic pushdown to DOUBLE/REAL operands and leaves integer arithmetic for Trino. Fix the misleading comment claiming integer-arithmetic alignment.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced. arithmeticOperator() (DuckDbExpressionTranslator.java:706-721) maps $divide to DuckDB '/' for ALL numeric types with no type gate. The direct arithmetic path at lines 503-510 in translateCall is reached BEFORE the PUSHABLE_FUNCTIONS/TYPE_GATES machinery (lines 575-583), so the per-entry ArgTypeGate discipline never applies to arithmetic operators — there is no integral-operand guard anywhere.

The semantic divergence is real for the DuckDB build in use (community 1.5.3 per briefing). DuckDB's '/' performs true (floating-point) division on integer operands by default (integer_division=false); SELECT 5/2 = 2.5. Truncating integer division requires '//' or div(). Trino's $divide on INTEGER operands truncates toward zero (5/2 = 2). I confirmed no integer_division PRAGMA or any SET is issued on attach (grep across src found none), so the DuckDB default applies.

Integer Constants translate to bare integer literals (translateConstant line 446), so WHERE a/2 = 3 with a=7 emits DuckDB '((a / 2) = 3)' → 3.5 = 3 → false (row dropped at scan), while Trino evaluates 3 = 3 → true (row kept). DucklakeMetadata.applyFilter (lines 621-655) confirms the translated conjunct is AND-ed into the .db reader's WHERE clause AND echoed back in remainingExpression for above-scan re-evaluation. Re-evaluation can only further restrict the post-scan rows, never re-add rows DuckDB already filtered out — so this is an uncorrected false-negative / wrong-results bug, not the intended cheap double-evaluation.

The code comment at lines 708-714 actively asserts the opposite ("Both engines align on integer ... arithmetic semantics"), making it misleading.

Not covered by any INTENTIONAL DECISION or KNOWN GAP in the briefing; in fact it violates the stated 'Lossless, curated pushdown discipline' convention (per-entry recorded edge semantics + cross-engine byte-for-byte fixture) — grep found zero division/arithmetic test fixtures. The secondary DIVISION_BY_ZERO point is also real (DuckDB x/0 → NULL → predicate not-matched, dropping rows; Trino throws), reinforcing rather than weakening the finding. Severity high is warranted: silent wrong results on a common predicate shape, no test coverage, misleading comment.

confirmed.title: Unbounded-low exclusive-high predicate at a transform boundary prunes ALL files under CALENDAR encoding (silent data loss)
confirmed.detail: For a one-sided predicate like `ts < TIMESTAMP '2026-03-08 00:00:00'`, Trino's Range.lessThan produces an empty low value (verified in Range.java:116-120), so lowTransformed becomes Long.MIN_VALUE (line 110). For non-DATE types with an exclusive high bound that sits exactly on a transform boundary, the code decrements highTransformed (lines 120-126). Under CALENDAR encoding the transformed value is a cyclic calendar component, not a monotonic epoch count, so the decrement pushes highTransformed BELOW the valid component domain: HOUR midnight -> hourOfDay 0 decremented to -1; DAY -> day 1 decremented to 0; MONTH 2026-02-01 -> month 2 decremented to 1. The non-monotonic wrapping guard at lines 130-134 is meant to catch unsound calendar comparisons, but it only fires when `lowTransformed > highTransformed`, and Long.MIN_VALUE is never > the decremented high. So the final check `transformedValue >= MIN && transformedValue <= -1/0/1` evaluates false for every real partition value (HOUR 0..23, DAY 1..31), and the matcher returns false. In DucklakeSplitManager.pruneByPartitions (line 480) a false result prunes the data file, so a query such as `WHERE ts < '2026-03-08 00:00:00'` on an HOUR- or DAY-partitioned table silently returns zero/incomplete rows. The existing tests (TestDucklakeTemporalPartitionMatcher, lines ~310-520) only exercise two-sided bounded ranges, so this one-sided case is uncovered.
confirmed.suggestion: Only apply the exclusive-high decrement when it is sound — i.e. for EPOCH encoding (monotonic), or for CALENDAR only when the range also has a bounded low whose transformed component is contiguous. The simplest fix: guard the decrement block (and/or the final comparison) so that when the low bound is unbounded under a cyclic CALENDAR transform you fall back to not pruning (return true), instead of comparing against Long.MIN_VALUE. Alternatively, after decrementing, if highTransformed drops below the minimum plausible component value (isPlausibleValueForEncoding lower bound), skip pruning.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Independently reproduced. In DucklakeTemporalPartitionMatcher.temporalRangeContainsTransformedValue (lines 108-136): for a one-sided predicate `ts < TIMESTAMP '2026-03-08 00:00:00'`, Range.lessThan produces an empty low (verified Range.java:116-120 returns Optional.empty() for low), so range.getLowValue() is empty and lowTransformed=Long.MIN_VALUE (line 110). Under CALENDAR encoding, applyTemporalTransform (lines 166-173) returns a CYCLIC calendar component, not a monotonic epoch count: DAY -> dayOfMonth, MONTH -> monthValue, HOUR -> hourOfDay. For `< 2026-03-08 00:00:00` with DAY: highTransformed = dayOfMonth(2026-03-08) = 8; the exclusive-high block (lines 120-126) sees midnight (isAtTransformBoundary true) and decrements to 7. The non-monotonic guard (lines 130-134) only fires when lowTransformed > highTransformed, but Long.MIN_VALUE > 7 is false, so it never fires. Final check (line 136): for a real partition file with dayOfMonth=15 (which legitimately contains rows like 2026-01-15 that satisfy ts < 2026-03-08), `15 >= MIN_VALUE && 15 <= 7` evaluates false -> matcher returns false -> DucklakeSplitManager.pruneByPartitions (line 480-481) prunes the file. Result: silent incomplete/zero results. isPlausibleValueForEncoding (line 76) does not save it (8..31 are plausible), and the readLeniency EPOCH fallback (lines 60-61) also fails and isn't unconditional. The bug is actually broader than the decrement: even a non-boundary one-sided predicate (`ts < 2026-03-08 12:00:00`, no decrement, highTransformed=8) prunes any file with dayOfMonth>8, and the symmetric `ts >` case (unbounded high -> MAX_VALUE, lowTransformed a cyclic component) is equally unsound. The decrement just extends the breakage to include the boundary day. Test coverage gap confirmed: TestDucklakeTemporalPartitionMatcher uses only Range.range(...) two-sided bounded ranges (lines 310-553); no Range.lessThan/greaterThan one-sided cases. The suggested fix (skip pruning / return true when the low bound is unbounded under a cyclic CALENDAR transform) is sound.

confirmed.title: Non-monotonic wrapping guard omits YEAR but applies the decrement to YEAR, and does not account for an unbounded low bound
confirmed.detail: The wrapping guard intentionally excludes YEAR (monotonic) which is correct, but the guard's `lowTransformed > highTransformed` test silently fails to protect any case where lowTransformed is Long.MIN_VALUE (unbounded low) — see the high-severity finding. Even within the intended bounded scope, the guard runs AFTER the decrement at lines 120-126, so it compares an already-mutated highTransformed. For MONTH/DAY/HOUR a bounded low whose component happens to equal the decremented high (e.g. low month 3, high 2026-04-01 -> month 4 decremented to 3) yields low==high and passes, which is correct only by coincidence of the specific calendar arithmetic. The guard is doing two unrelated jobs (wrap detection and salvaging the decrement) and is fragile.
confirmed.suggestion: Separate the concerns: decide unbounded-vs-bounded and monotonic-vs-cyclic explicitly before computing the comparison, rather than relying on `low > high` after mutation. At minimum add a branch: if CALENDAR and the transform is cyclic (MONTH/DAY/HOUR) and either bound is unbounded, return true (cannot prune safely).
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The core technical claim is independently reproducible and represents a real correctness defect (silent wrong results / data-file over-pruning), not just fragility.

Mechanism (DucklakeTemporalPartitionMatcher.java:101-136): for a half-open range, getLowValue()/getHighValue() returns Optional.empty() (confirmed against io/trino/spi/predicate/Range.java: Range.lessThan/greaterThan produce unbounded bounds), so lowTransformed defaults to Long.MIN_VALUE (line 110) or highTransformed to Long.MAX_VALUE (line 113). The cyclic-wrap guard at lines 130-134 only fires when `lowTransformed > highTransformed`. With an unbounded bound that condition is never true (MIN is never > a real value; a real value is never > MAX), so the guard is bypassed and the linear comparison at line 136 (`transformedValue >= lowTransformed && transformedValue <= highTransformed`) is applied to a CALENDAR MONTH/DAY/HOUR component, which is cyclic, not monotonic.

Concrete reproduction — predicate `ts < TIMESTAMP '2026-04-01 00:00:00'`, MONTH transform: lowTransformed=MIN, highTransformed = applyTemporalTransform(Apr)=4, decremented to 3 at line 124 (exclusive high at boundary). Guard does not fire. A data file with partition month=11 (e.g. November 2020, which is < 2026-04-01 and genuinely contains matching rows) fails `11 <= 3` and returns false. Via DucklakeSplitManager.java:480-481, a false return prunes the file (`return true` from removeIf), so matching data is silently dropped. The symmetric unbounded-high case (`ts > X`) prunes lower-numbered months that legitimately match in later years.

This is one of the most common temporal predicate shapes, and the path is untested: TestDucklakeTemporalPartitionMatcher.java only uses Range.range(...) with both bounds present (lines 320-551); testCalendarWrappingMonthRangeDoesNotPrune (line 183) covers only the bounded wrap case the guard already handles. No Range.lessThan/greaterThan test exists.

Note the finding's title is partly muddled: the line 120-126 decrement does run for YEAR but is harmless there (YEAR monotonic), and that decrement is not itself the bug. The finding correctly defers the data-loss substance to "the high-severity finding" and frames its own contribution as fragility, but the underlying correctness issue is real. Not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing. Severity raised to high because the consequence is silent incorrect query results, not a missed optimization.

confirmed.title: Cross-row-group min/max aggregation uses lexicographic string compare on numeric values, producing wrong per-file bounds
confirmed.detail: stringMin/stringMax aggregate per-row-group min/max across a file with plain String.compareTo. For numeric leaf types the stat strings are decimal/float text, which does NOT order like the underlying numbers. With two row groups holding min/max {3,8} and {1,12} the file-wide max is computed as "8" because "8".compareTo("12")>0, not 12 (the test at TestDucklakeStatsExtractor.java:236-237 bakes in this wrong result). The same applies to BIGINT/REAL/DOUBLE/DECIMAL and negatives. These per-file min/max are written to ducklake_file_column_stats and consumed by JdbcDucklakeCatalog.getDataFilesMatchingPredicate (lines 471-497), which parses them back to typed Comparables and prunes files whose [min,max] excludes the predicate. A too-narrow recorded max/min causes a file that actually contains matching rows to be pruned -> silently dropped rows / wrong query results. Note the catalog's own cross-file aggregation (typedCompare, JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:584) compares numerically, so the extractor is the inconsistent layer.
confirmed.suggestion: Compare the parsed numeric/typed values when folding groupMin/groupMax (mirror JdbcDucklakeCatalog.typedCompare keyed off the leaf Type), or fold the raw decoded primitive before formatting to string, instead of String.compareTo. Date/timestamp ISO strings happen to sort correctly, but ints/floats/decimals do not.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced the full chain. In DucklakeStatsExtractor.java the per-row-group min/max fold (lines 102-107) calls stringMin/stringMax (lines 192-200), which use plain String.compareTo. The type-specific decoding in convertStatValue (lines 135-183) produces decimal/float TEXT (e.g. String.valueOf(intVal), BigDecimal.toPlainString()) for all numeric leaf types, and the fold is type-blind — it lexically compares those strings. For a file with two row groups holding {3,8} and {1,12}, the recorded file-wide max becomes "8" because "8".compareTo("12")>0, not 12. The test extractStatsMergesStatisticsAcrossRowGroups (TestDucklakeStatsExtractor.java:224-237) explicitly bakes in maxValue()=="8" for exactly this case, confirming the behavior is shipped, not a one-off. The same lexical breakage hits BIGINT/REAL/DOUBLE/DECIMAL and any multi-digit vs shorter numeric strings.

The consequence is real and on a live read path. These stats land in ducklake_file_column_stats and are consumed by JdbcDucklakeCatalog.findDataFileIdsInRange (lines 456-499): MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE are parsed to BigDecimal via parseStatValue (line 2431-2432) and a file is pruned when lowerBound > maxStat (isWithinBounds, lines 2477-2480). DucklakeSplitManager.java:299-322 then hard-drops the unmatched files (candidateFileIds.retainAll(...); filter(...)). So a query like WHERE col = 10 against the {3,8}/{1,12} file computes lowerBound(10) > maxStat(8) -> file pruned -> rows with value 10 silently dropped = wrong results.

The finding's observation that the catalog's own cross-FILE aggregation is correct is accurate: typedCompare (lines 584-601) switches on column type and uses Long.compare/Double.compare/LocalDate.parse for numeric/date types, falling back to lexical only for genuinely string types. The extractor is the inconsistent layer — it should mirror that type-keyed comparison (or fold the decoded primitive before stringifying). Date/timestamp/UTC-instant ISO strings sort correctly lexically, and VARCHAR is fine, so the bug is scoped to numeric leaves, but those are common. Not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing. The fix suggestion (compare typed values) is correct.

confirmed.title: Decimal min/max decoded as big-endian BigInteger, but short decimals are stored little-endian (INT32/INT64) by Trino's parquet writer
confirmed.detail: The DecimalType branch does `new BigInteger(value)`, which interprets bytes as big-endian two's complement. Trino's ParquetWriter encodes DECIMAL(precision<=9) as physical INT32 (Int32ShortDecimalValueWriter) and DECIMAL(precision<=18) as INT64 (Int64ShortDecimalValueWriter); for those columns Statistics.min_value/max_value are the little-endian INT32/INT64 bytes, not a big-endian magnitude. So e.g. DECIMAL(9,2) value 1.23 (unscaled 123, bytes 7B 00 00 00 LE) decodes via new BigInteger as 0x7B000000=2063597568 -> 20635975.68, a completely wrong bound. Only DECIMAL(precision>18), stored as FIXED_LEN_BYTE_ARRAY big-endian, is handled correctly. Result: corrupt decimal stats feeding both displayed table statistics and file pruning -> dropped rows for short-decimal predicates.
confirmed.suggestion: Branch on the parquet physical/storage width for decimals (or on byte length): for 4-byte stats read a little-endian int, for 8-byte a little-endian long, then apply scale; only use big-endian BigInteger for the fixed-len-byte-array (long-decimal) case.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is independently reproduced. DucklakeStatsExtractor.convertStatValue (line 180) decodes DecimalType min/max with `new BigInteger(value)`, which is big-endian two's complement. That is only correct for FIXED_LEN_BYTE_ARRAY-encoded decimals (precision > 18).

The connector's write paths construct the parquet schema via `new ParquetSchemaConverter(columnTypes, columnNames, false, false)` — DucklakePageSink.java:129-130 and DucklakeMergeSink.java:182-183 — passing useLegacyDecimalEncoding=false. With that flag false, ParquetSchemaConverter (deps-reference io/trino/parquet/writer/ParquetSchemaConverter.java:159-175) maps DECIMAL(precision<=9) to physical INT32 and DECIMAL(precision<=18) to physical INT64; only precision>18 uses FIXED_LEN_BYTE_ARRAY. The corresponding writers (Int32ShortDecimalValueWriter / Int64ShortDecimalValueWriter) call statistics.updateStats(int/long), and the footer Thrift min_value/max_value bytes are produced by ParquetMetadataConverter.toParquetStatistics via stats.getMinBytes()/getMaxBytes(), which for IntStatistics/LongStatistics are little-endian PLAIN-encoded bytes. Trino's own read side confirms the contract: fromParquetStatistics (ParquetMetadataConverter.java:316-320) hands the raw bytes to org.apache.parquet.column.statistics.Statistics.Builder, which decodes INT32/INT64 little-endian.

So for the common short-decimal case the bytes are little-endian but convertStatValue reads them big-endian, yielding wrong unscaled magnitudes (the reviewer's DECIMAL(9,2)=1.23 -> 20635975.68 example is correct). Note the author already uses ByteOrder.LITTLE_ENDIAN for IntegerType/BigintType/DateType/TimestampType (lines 136-168), making the DecimalType branch an inconsistency, not a deliberate choice.

The min/max feed DucklakeFileColumnStats (extractStats lines 99-118) which back both displayed table statistics and file pruning, so corrupt bounds can drop rows on short-decimal predicates. The briefing's 'Conservative stats suppression' decision covers delete-files/mixed-format/schema-evolution only and does not immunize this path. add_files paths reading externally-produced parquet would hit the same bug whenever a producer used INT32/INT64 decimal encoding (common). One caveat lowering blast radius slightly: a try/catch returns Optional.empty() on RuntimeException, but BigInteger of 4/8 bytes never throws, so no silent suppression saves it here. Severity high stands.

confirmed.title: Lexicographic stringMin/stringMax corrupts numeric min/max for multi-row-group files
confirmed.detail: When a Parquet file has more than one row group, the per-column min/max is accumulated across row groups using stringMin/stringMax, which do String.compareTo on the already-serialized decimal strings. For integer types this is wrong: "5".compareTo("10") is positive (because '5' > '1'), so stringMin("5", "10") returns "10" instead of "5". The stored ducklake_file_column_stats.min_value is too large, causing findDataFileIdsInRange to incorrectly prune files whose actual values satisfy a predicate. For example, a file with row groups [min=5, max=9] and [min=10, max=20] would have min_value stored as "10" instead of "5"; a query WHERE col = 7 could skip this file even though it contains matching rows. The same wrong comparison applies to dates ("2023-09-02" > "2023-10-01" lexicographically), doubles ("9.5" > "10.0"), and decimals with varying digit counts.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace stringMin/stringMax with type-aware comparison. Either pass the column type through to extractStats and replicate the typedCompare logic from JdbcDucklakeCatalog, or accumulate the actual parsed Comparable<?> values (the same parseStatValue logic already used in findDataFileIdsInRange) and serialize back to String only at emit time. A simpler minimal fix: pass leafType to stringMin/stringMax and dispatch through a switch on the same type cases already present in convertStatValue.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
DucklakeStatsExtractor.extractStats (the write-path stats aggregator, used by both ParquetFileWriter.java:101-102 and DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java:309) accumulates per-row-group min/max across multiple row groups using stringMin/stringMax (lines 103, 106), which do plain String.compareTo (lines 192-200). For numeric types convertStatValue serializes values as decimal strings (lines 135-156, 179-183): integers/bigints via String.valueOf, doubles/reals via String.valueOf, decimals via toPlainString. Lexicographic comparison of these is wrong: "5".compareTo("10") > 0, so stringMin("5","10") returns "10" — the stored min_value is too large. Same for "9.5" vs "10.0" (double) and any decimals with differing integer-digit counts.

The consequence is real and load-bearing. JdbcDucklakeCatalog.findDataFileIdsInRange (lines 456-499) reads back colstats.MIN_VALUE/MAX_VALUE and parses them numerically via parseStatValue -> BigDecimal for numeric types (lines 2423-2458), then prunes via isWithinBounds. A file whose true min is 5 but stored min is "10" will be incorrectly pruned for WHERE col = 7, dropping rows that actually match → wrong query results. Notably the catalog layer ITSELF already has the correct fix in typedCompare (lines 584-601) used by typedMin/typedMax in getColumnStats merging — so the write-path extractor is simply inconsistent with the read-path merge logic that solves the identical problem.

Multi-row-group files are normal: Trino's ParquetWriter rolls a new row group at the configured block size, and add_files ingests arbitrary external Parquet files (commonly many row groups). No single-row-group guarantee exists.

One detail in the finding is inaccurate: the date/timestamp examples are NOT broken — convertStatValue emits ISO-8601 (LocalDate.toString / LocalDateTime.toString), which is fixed-width and lexicographically ordered consistently with chronological order, so "2023-09-02" < "2023-10-01" lexicographically (the finding's date claim is wrong). VARBINARY/UUID return empty, VARCHAR is genuinely code-point/byte ordered so fine, BOOLEAN min/max is harmless. The damage is limited to TINYINT/SMALLINT/INTEGER/BIGINT/REAL/DOUBLE/DECIMAL — but that is the most common stats-pruning case, so high severity stands.

confirmed.title: typedCompare uses wrong column type names — numeric min/max accumulation always falls back to lexicographic comparison
confirmed.detail: The switch in typedCompare matches "bigint", "integer", "int", "smallint", "tinyint", "hugeint" (line 588) and "double", "real", "float", "decimal" (line 590) — but the actual values stored in ducklake_column.column_type by toDucklakeType are "int8", "int16", "int32", "int64", "float32", "float64", and "decimal(p,s)". None of these match any case, so every numeric column silently falls through to a.compareTo(b) — lexicographic string comparison. This produces wrong min/max across data files for any multi-character number (e.g., "9" > "10" lexicographically), negative numbers ("- 5" sorts before any positive), or decimals with differing precision strings. The same bug affects the cross-file min/max accumulation in getColumnStats (lines 550-553) and thus the DoubleRange stats fed to Trino's optimizer at DucklakeMetadata line 492, potentially causing wrong query plans (over-aggressive predicate pruning). The isNumericType helper at line 2449 correctly uses "int32"/"int64", confirming the naming convention elsewhere in the file.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace the switch cases to match the actual stored type names: "int8", "int16", "int32", "int64", "hugeint", "int128" for Long.parseLong; "float32", "float64" for Double.parseDouble; and use columnType.startsWith("decimal(") for the decimal branch (using BigDecimal.compareTo, since Double.parseDouble loses precision for high-scale decimals).
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Independently reproduced. The typedCompare switch (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:587-595) matches type names "bigint","integer","int","smallint","tinyint","hugeint" (line 588) and "double","real","float","decimal" (line 590). But ducklake_column.column_type is written from DucklakeTypeConverter (trino-ducklake/.../DucklakeTypeConverter.java:195-216) as the canonical strings "int8","int16","int32","int64","int128","float32","float64","decimal(p,s)" — and read back the same way (lines 118-140). The sibling isNumericType helper (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2447-2456) confirms the convention is "int8/int16/int32/int64/float32/float64". None of those canonical names match any case in the switch, so every numeric/decimal column falls through to `default -> a.compareTo(b)` — lexicographic string comparison. columnTypes (line 514) is populated from DucklakeColumn::columnType, which is the raw stored value (getTableColumns -> fetchTableColumns, no normalization), so the wrong strings reach typedCompare unmodified. Consequence: the cross-file min/max merge reducers (lines 550, 553 via typedMin/typedMax) accumulate the lexicographically-wrong extreme whenever a column spans 2+ data files (e.g. across "9" and "10", lexical max is "9"). Those corrupted strings then flow to DucklakeMetadata.toDoubleRange (lines 545-549), which Double.parseDoubles them into a DoubleRange fed to Trino's optimizer via setRange (line 492). Scope clarifications, none of which refute the finding: (a) the "date" case (line 592) is correctly named and unaffected; (b) single-data-file tables are unaffected because Map.merge never invokes the reducer; (c) the per-file min/max from DuckDB is itself correct — only the JVM-side cross-file accumulation is broken. The finding's reference to a method literally named "toDucklakeType" is slightly off (it's DucklakeTypeConverter), but the substantive claim about the stored type strings is exactly correct. This is a silent statistics-correctness bug affecting any multi-file numeric column; it feeds optimizer cost estimates rather than corrupting returned rows, which is why I keep it at high but note it is plan-quality, not data-correctness.

confirmed.title: listTables omits views, violating the SPI contract
confirmed.detail: ConnectorMetadata.listTables is documented to 'List table, view and materialized view names' (deps-reference ConnectorMetadata.java line 277-283). This implementation only enumerates catalog.listTables(...) and never adds the schema's views. The default getRelationTypes() (ConnectorMetadata line 292-297) classifies relations by iterating listTables() alone, so views that are not in listTables will be invisible to SHOW TABLES and to any caller relying on getRelationTypes. Trino's Iceberg/Hive connectors include views in listTables for this reason.
confirmed.suggestion: Union the view names (catalog.listViews filtered by isViewAccessible, as listViews already does) into the listTables result, matching the SPI contract and the other lakehouse connectors.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Independently reproduced. (1) The SPI contract at deps-reference ConnectorMetadata.java:277-283 documents listTables as "List table, view and materialized view names." (2) DucklakeMetadata.listTables (DucklakeMetadata.java:336-360) only calls catalog.listTables(...). JdbcDucklakeCatalog.listTables (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:285-292) selects exclusively from DUCKLAKE_TABLE; views live in a separate DUCKLAKE_VIEW table queried only by catalog.listViews (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1008). So the connector's listTables never emits view names. (3) getRelationTypes is NOT overridden in DucklakeMetadata (grep returned no match), so the default applies. That default (ConnectorMetadata.java:292-308) builds its result map by streaming listTables() as the key set and merely classifying each entry; entries that are not in listTables() — i.e. all views — never appear as map keys at all. SHOW TABLES and information_schema.tables are served via getRelationTypes, so views are invisible there. (4) The Hive reference confirms the intended pattern: HiveMetadata.listTables and getRelationTypes (HiveMetadata.java:843-856) both source from streamTables(), which returns TableInfo records including views (distinguished via extendedRelationType()). The ClassLoaderSafe wrapper merely delegates, adding nothing. This is not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing. Severity kept at medium rather than high: views remain directly queryable (getView at 1317 works) and SHOW VIEWS routes through the working listViews, so the impact is a relation-discovery/SHOW TABLES gap, not data loss or incorrect query results.

confirmed.title: listTableColumns reads columns at current snapshot, ignoring session/time-travel snapshot
confirmed.detail: listTableColumns resolves its snapshot with catalog.getCurrentSnapshotId() while every other listing method (listTables, listViews, getViews, getTableHandle) uses snapshotResolver.resolveSnapshotId(session), which can differ when a session snapshot/timestamp property or catalog default is set (DucklakeSnapshotResolver lines 84-94). Worse, the table set itself is computed via listTables(session, prefix.getSchema()) (line 774) at the session snapshot, then columns are loaded at getCurrentSnapshotId(). Under time travel the set of tables and the column shapes can come from two different snapshots, yielding wrong/empty column lists for INFORMATION_SCHEMA.columns and SHOW COLUMNS.
confirmed.suggestion: Resolve a single snapshotId with snapshotResolver.resolveSnapshotId(session) at the top of listTableColumns and use it for both the table listing and getTableColumns, matching the other list methods.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced. In DucklakeMetadata.java, listTableColumns (line 769) resolves its snapshot via `catalog.getCurrentSnapshotId()`, bypassing the resolver. Every other read/listing entry point uses `snapshotResolver.resolveSnapshotId(session)`: listTables (line 339), getTableHandle (line 212), getViews/listViews/etc (lines 1292, 1319, 1337, 1368, 1391, 1468, 1479). DucklakeSnapshotResolver.resolveSnapshotId (lines 78-97) honors session properties (read_snapshot_id/read_snapshot_timestamp), then catalog defaults (catalogDefaultSnapshotId/Timestamp from DucklakeConfig), and only falls back to catalog.getCurrentSnapshotId() when none are set. So when a session time-travel property OR a catalog default snapshot is configured, getCurrentSnapshotId() returns a different snapshot than the resolver would.

Two concrete defects: (1) listTableColumns reports columns at the current snapshot, ignoring the session/default snapshot, so information_schema.columns and bulk SHOW COLUMNS return wrong column shapes under time travel. (2) Mixed-snapshot: the table set is computed via listTables(session, prefix.getSchema()) at line 774 (session snapshot) while getTable/getTableColumns at lines 780/786/788 use getCurrentSnapshotId(); a table present in the session snapshot but absent at current is silently dropped via the table.isPresent() guard (line 787), and column shapes for evolved tables come from a snapshot inconsistent with the listed set.

This is the live path: streamTableColumns is NOT overridden, so the SPI default delegates to listTableColumns; information_schema.columns queries hit this method. The fix in the suggestion (resolve one snapshotId via snapshotResolver.resolveSnapshotId(session) at the top and use it for both listing and column loading) is correct and matches the other methods.

Not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing. Catalog-default-snapshot is a real configured feature (DucklakeConfig.getDefaultSnapshotId), which would make ordinary metadata queries wrong even without per-query time travel. Severity medium is appropriate: real correctness bug but confined to metadata listing (data reads via getTableHandle are unaffected) and only manifests when a non-current snapshot is selected.

confirmed.title: On the DuckDB path, row-id injection and delete filtering assume unfiltered scan order but predicates are pushed to DuckDB
confirmed.detail: createDuckDbPageSource pushes filePredicate and pushedExpressions into DuckDbFilePageSource, which issues a SELECT ... WHERE to DuckDB so the engine returns only matching rows (and in no guaranteed file order). The downstream RowIdInjectingPageSource and DeleteRowFilterTransform both compute file row offsets by counting returned positions from 0. Because DuckDB has already filtered (and may reorder) rows, those counted offsets do not correspond to the data file's physical row positions. Consequently $row_id values are wrong and positional delete application drops/keeps the wrong rows whenever any predicate is pushed down on a duckdb-format file. The duckdb format is flagged experimental, but this is a positional-correctness defect (DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE + WHERE), not API churn.
confirmed.suggestion: Project DuckDB's file_row_number (rowid) explicitly in the pushed SELECT and use it as the authoritative position for both $row_id injection and delete-position matching, rather than counting returned rows. If file_row_number cannot be obtained, disable predicate/expression pushdown on the duckdb path whenever the split carries deletes or $row_id is requested.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The mechanism described is real and reproducible from the code. DuckDbSelectSqlBuilder.buildSelectSql (DuckDbSelectSqlBuilder.java:60-80) emits `SELECT <projected cols> FROM t WHERE <domain> AND <expr clauses>` with NO rowid/file_row_number projection and NO ORDER BY. DuckDbWhereClauseTranslator produces genuine row-level predicates (=, IN, >/< ranges over scalar columns), and the predicate fed in is effectivePredicate = fileStatisticsDomain ∩ dynamicFilter (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:168-169), where fileStatisticsDomain includes unenforcedPredicate (non-partition, row-level domains; DucklakeSplitManager.java:149-150) and the dynamic filter contributes runtime row-level values. So DuckDB returns only matching rows, in no guaranteed physical order.

Downstream, RowIdInjectingPageSource assigns $row_id = rowIdStart + nextRowOffset + i by counting returned positions from 0 (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:1006-1009), and DeleteRowFilterTransform computes rowOffset = nextRowOffset + position the same way (lines 928-938). The class comment (line 951) explicitly states these must reflect "original file positions." Once DuckDB has filtered rows, the cumulative returned-row count no longer equals the physical file_row_number, so $row_id values are wrong and positional delete matching drops/keeps the wrong physical rows. No guard suppresses pushdown when split.deleteFilePaths()/inlinedDeletedRowPositions() are non-empty or when a $row_id column is requested (createDuckDbPageSource lines 603-624 push filePredicate + pushedExpressions unconditionally). The "mixed-format dual-emission" re-evaluation above the scan (applyFilter, DucklakeMetadata.java:654) restores WHERE-result correctness but runs AFTER row-id injection and delete filtering inside the page source, so it cannot repair the corrupted positions. DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE is reachable on duckdb-format files (RowChangeParadigm.DELETE_ROW_AND_INSERT_ROW, getMergeRowIdColumnHandle unconditional).

Two reasons I hold this at medium rather than high. (1) The parquet path (createParquetPageSource lines 433-544) shares the exact same RowIdInjectingPageSource/DeleteRowFilterTransform and also prunes by the same predicate via getFilteredRowGroups, which would skip whole row groups and desync the same counter. So the position-count-equals-physical-position assumption is a broader design property, not duckdb-specific; either both paths are vulnerable under predicate pushdown, or there is a Trino engine-level contract (e.g. the planner not applying value constraints / pruning on the scan that produces merge row ids, or dynamic filters being inert for the row-change scan) that I could not confirm or refute from the connector code alone and that would equally protect the duckdb path. (2) The duckdb format is explicitly flagged EXPERIMENTAL in the briefing. The existing tests (TestDeleteRowFilterTransformOverlap) only exercise synthetic full pages (SourcePage.create(20)) and never the predicate-filtered interaction, so they neither prove nor disprove the scenario. The suggested fix (project DuckDB's rowid and use it as the authoritative position, or disable pushdown when deletes/$row_id are in play) is the right shape if the engine-level guarantee does not exist.

confirmed.title: Identity partition constant-fill ignores each file's own partition spec (partition-evolution mis-fill)
confirmed.detail: buildIdentityPartitionValues uses the table's currently-active spec (specsForProjection.getLast(), line 159-161) for EVERY data file, looking up each field's value by partitionKeyIndex alone (byKeyIndex.get(field.partitionKeyIndex())). But DucklakeFilePartitionValue carries only (dataFileId, partitionKeyIndex, value) with no partition_id, and DucklakeDataFile DOES carry its own partitionId which is never consulted. Each partition spec numbers its keys from 0, so after a repartition (old spec key0 = colA, new spec key0 = colB) an old file's colA value gets constant-filled into colB. The page source uses this map to fill partition columns missing from the parquet body, so a hive-style imported file written under an older spec would surface the wrong column's value as data. Same root cause affects pruneByPartitionValues (line 465-485), where a mis-keyed value that happens to parse for the wrong column can falsely prune a matching file and drop correct rows.
confirmed.suggestion: Filter file partition values to the file's own partitionId (primary.partitionId()) and resolve the keyIndex->column mapping against that file's spec, not the active spec. Either have getFilePartitionValues return the partition_id per value, or look up the per-file spec from specsForProjection keyed by partitionId before mapping keyIndex to columnId.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The mechanism is real and matches the DuckLake spec authority (vendored C++ extension). Evidence:

- DucklakeDataFile carries Optional<Long> partitionId (DucklakeDataFile.java:36) but it is NEVER consulted in buildIdentityPartitionValues or pruneByPartitionValues.
- DucklakeFilePartitionValue carries only (dataFileId, partitionKeyIndex, partitionValue) with no partition_id (DucklakeFilePartitionValue.java:19-23), and getFilePartitionValues (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:680-710) joins only on data_file_id, dropping any partition_id linkage.
- buildIdentityPartitionValues (lines 609-636) iterates activePartitionSpec.fields() and resolves each field via byKeyIndex.get(field.partitionKeyIndex()). activeSpec is specsForProjection.getLast() (lines 159-161), and getPartitionSpecs filters by activeAt(partinfo, snapshotId) (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:625; SnapshotRange.java:50-54). Per the spec schema, ducklake_partition_info has begin/end_snapshot (vendor metadata_manager.cpp:210), so on repartition the old spec is retired and getPartitionSpecs returns ONLY the currently-active spec. An old file's partition values, recorded under the retired spec's key indices, are then mapped through the active spec's key->column mapping. Since each spec numbers keys from 0, old key0 (colA) gets constant-filled into new key0's column (colC). The spec authority confirms partition values must be associated with the file's own partition_id (vendor metadata_manager.cpp:1820, 1904 carry data.partition_id per file precisely to re-derive values), which this code discards.
- The constant-fill is consumed only when the column is physically MISSING from the parquet body (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:493-499, buildMissingColumnBlock at 1051), i.e. hive-style add_files imports; connector-INSERT-written and DuckDB-written files embed partition columns physically, so they never trigger it.
- pruneByPartitionValues (lines 465-485) has the same mis-keying: it matches file values by mapping.keyIndex against the active spec, and partitionValueMatchesDomain swallows parse failures as 'don't prune' (lines 530-532), so a false prune only occurs when a mis-keyed value happens to parse for the wrong column's type and falls outside the domain.

Why confirmed but narrow: the trigger requires partition evolution, which the connector itself cannot perform (no ALTER TABLE SET PARTITIONING; partition spec is written only at CREATE TABLE, JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1674-1698). It can only arise via external DuckDB repartitioning on the shared catalog — which is the documented operational model — combined with (a) hive-style column-omitting imports for the constant-fill path, or (b) a type-coincident mis-key for the prune path. Not listed among the briefing's intentional decisions or known gaps. No test exercises mixed-spec reads. Severity kept at medium because the prune path can silently drop correct rows (wrong-results, not just an error), though the rarity of the trigger argues toward the low end. Confidence is medium: I could not fully rule out that, in practice, every reachable mixed-spec catalog still satisfies the active-spec assumption, and I did not find the reference's read-side (non-compaction) partition-value resolution to compare directly.

confirmed.title: TIMESTAMPTZ Appender write truncates sub-millisecond precision (data loss for TIMESTAMP(6)/(9) WITH TIME ZONE)
confirmed.detail: For TimestampWithTimeZoneType the writer only ever uses epochMillis. For the long (high-precision) variant it reads LongTimestampWithTimeZone.getEpochMillis() and discards getPicosOfMilli() entirely, then writes OffsetDateTime built from Instant.ofEpochMilli(...). The column was created as DuckDB TIMESTAMPTZ, which is microsecond precision, so a TIMESTAMP(6)/(9) WITH TIME ZONE value such as ...123456 UTC is silently truncated to ...123000 (millisecond). This is a real round-trip data-loss bug. Note the sibling TIMESTAMP (no zone) long path one branch above correctly preserves fractional precision via microsToLocalDateTime(...).plusNanos(picosOfMicro/1000), and the Arrow-stream writer (DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter.populateTimestampTzVector, lines 1037-1040) correctly carries nanos for the long case. The Appender path is the odd one out.
confirmed.suggestion: For the long branch compute micros from the LongTimestampWithTimeZone (epochMillis*1000 + picosOfMilli/1_000_000 for micros, or build an Instant with nanos via ofEpochSecond) and append an OffsetDateTime carrying microsecond precision, mirroring the Arrow writer. Only the short branch (precision <=3) may safely use epochMillis.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced. DuckDbFileWriter.java:337-348 handles TimestampWithTimeZoneType by computing only `epochMillis`: the short branch unpacks millis (correct, since MAX_SHORT_PRECISION=3), but the long branch (line 344-345) reads `LongTimestampWithTimeZone.getEpochMillis()` and discards `getPicosOfMilli()`, then writes `OffsetDateTime.ofInstant(Instant.ofEpochMilli(epochMillis), UTC)` — millisecond precision only.

The destination column is always created as DuckDB `TIMESTAMPTZ` (line 229-230, unconditional, no precision parameter), which is microsecond-precision. So a TIMESTAMP(6) WITH TIME ZONE value `...123456 UTC` is silently truncated to `...123000` — micros digits 4-6 lost. Verified via SPI: TimestampWithTimeZoneType.java:35,43,82 — precision 4-9 uses LongTimestampWithTimeZone which carries sub-millisecond data in getPicosOfMilli() (LongTimestampWithTimeZone.java:62,67).

The sibling-path comparison holds: the plain TimestampType long branch (lines 332-334) correctly does `microsToLocalDateTime(...).plusNanos(getPicosOfMicro()/1000)`, and DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter.populateTimestampTzVector (lines 1037-1040) correctly computes `nanos = epochMillis*1_000_000 + picosOfMilli/1000`. The Appender TZ path is the only one dropping the fractional component.

No guard prevents the lossy path: there is no precision check in DuckDbFileWriter; the only NOT_SUPPORTED throws (lines 235, 354) cover entirely unsupported types, not high-precision TIMESTAMPTZ. The class doc (lines 94-96) claims "high-precision timestamps fall through to NOT_SUPPORTED," but that is stale/aspirational — the code silently accepts and lossily writes them (consistent with plain TIMESTAMP also being fully supported up to TIMESTAMP_NS at line 225).

Severity downgraded high->medium only because this is confined to the DuckDB-format Appender writer, which the briefing flags as EXPERIMENTAL and non-default. The data loss is real and silent, but the briefing's "don't report churn/instability in the duckdb-format path" caveat targets API churn, not a concrete silent-truncation correctness bug, so it remains a valid finding. Fix is trivial: mirror the Arrow writer / plain-TIMESTAMP long branch.

confirmed.title: DuckDB connection leaked when extractColumnStats() or DETACH fails in finishAndBuildFragment
confirmed.detail: In the try block appender.close() / extractColumnStats() / DETACH / connection.close() run in sequence. If extractColumnStats() (a full SQL aggregate query) or the DETACH statement throws SQLException, control jumps to the catch which calls cleanupLocalFile() and rethrows, but connection.close() (line 388) is never reached, so the DuckDBConnection leaks. closed has already been set true, so a subsequent sink-level abort() returns early at line 582 and will not close it either. The Arrow writer routes the same failure through cleanupAfterFailure() which closes the connection.
confirmed.suggestion: Close the connection in the catch block (best-effort) before rethrowing, or wrap the lifecycle so the connection is always closed (e.g. try/finally or a closeQuietly on the catch path).
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The leak is real and reproducible from the code. In finishAndBuildFragment (DuckDbFileWriter.java:370-393): closed is set true at line 377 before the risky work; the try block runs appender.close() (381), extractColumnStats() (384, a full aggregate SQL query), DETACH (386), then connection.close() (388). If extractColumnStats() or DETACH throws SQLException, control jumps to the catch at 390-393 which only calls cleanupLocalFile() and rethrows — connection.close() at line 388 is never executed, so the DuckDBConnection (an in-memory jdbc:duckdb: instance holding native resources, field at line 115, opened once in the constructor at line 144) leaks. The fallback abort() at 579-605 cannot recover it: line 582-584 returns early because closed is already true, so connection.close() at 593 never runs. grep confirms connection.close() exists only at lines 388 and 593, with no try-with-resources or finally managing it. The upload-failure catch at 400-403 also omits a close, but on that path the connection was already closed at 388, so that branch is fine. Severity medium is appropriate: it only triggers on the exceptional stats/DETACH-failure path (not the hot path), but each leak is a full native DuckDB instance and accumulates under repeated failures. Not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing. The suggested fix (close the connection best-effort in the catch, or use try/finally) is correct.

confirmed.title: getMemoryUsage() not overridden — buffered Parquet row groups are invisible to Trino memory accounting
confirmed.detail: ConnectorPageSink exposes getMemoryUsage() so the engine can reserve memory for buffers the sink holds. ParquetWriter buffers an entire row group in memory before flush and exposes getRetainedBytes() (see ParquetWriter.java:158). This sink keeps a List<DucklakeFileWriter> — for partitioned writes potentially many concurrently-open writers each buffering up to a full row group — yet never overrides getMemoryUsage(), so Trino sees 0. Under wide tables or high partition cardinality this under-reports real heap pressure and risks worker OOM rather than back-pressure/spill, which is exactly the class of failure tracked in the OOM watch item.
confirmed.suggestion: Override getMemoryUsage() to sum getRetainedBytes()/buffered bytes across all non-null writers (add a getRetainedBytes()/memory accessor to DucklakeFileWriter delegating to parquetWriter.getRetainedBytes() and the duckdb writers' buffered size).
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is factually accurate and I independently reproduced it. DucklakePageSink (lines 58-394) overrides only appendPage/finish/abort; it does NOT override getMemoryUsage(), so it inherits the default returning 0 (ConnectorPageSink.java deps-reference lines 35-44, whose Javadoc states the engine reserves memory based on this value, including buffers). It also does not override the default no-op closeIdleWriters() (deps-reference lines 64-70), which exists precisely to bound memory of stale partition writers.

The buffering is real: ParquetFileWriter.write() delegates to ParquetWriter.write() (ParquetFileWriter.java:72), and ParquetWriter buffers a full row group whose retained size is exposed via getRetainedBytes() (deps-reference ParquetWriter.java:158-163, summing per-column-writer retained bytes + output buffer). The sink keeps a List<DucklakeFileWriter> with one writer per open partition (DucklakePageSink.java:82, 207-242 grow the list to maxIndex and keep many writers concurrently open), so under high partition cardinality / wide tables the unaccounted heap is N row groups.

The canonical reference this sink mirrors, Trino's own HivePageSink, DOES override both: getMemoryUsage() returns a memoryUsage field summed across writers (deps-reference HivePageSink.java:172-174, 387) and closeIdleWriters() (line 332). So the omission is a divergence from the established Trino pattern, not an intentional choice.

The rotation/file-sizing logic (DucklakePageSink.java:194,237) uses getApproximateWrittenBytes()→parquetWriter.getEstimatedWrittenBytes() (ParquetFileWriter.java:78), which is an on-disk-size estimate (outputStream.longSize()+estimatedBufferedBytes), NOT a memory-retained figure — it does not substitute for memory accounting.

Caveats that bound severity: (1) The DuckDB-format writer explicitly flushes the appender after every page to bound memory (DuckDbFileWriter.java:251-254) and reports writtenBytes = sum of page sizes, so the DuckDB path holds little — the gap is essentially Parquet-only. (2) The briefing's OOM watch item is about read-side inlined heaps, not this write path, so this is genuinely undocumented, not a known gap. (3) Trino's TableWriterOperator independently caps concurrent writer counts, partially mitigating worst-case OOM. The fix is low-cost: add getRetainedBytes() to DucklakeFileWriter delegating to parquetWriter.getRetainedBytes() (and ~0 for the flush-per-page DuckDB writers), and override getMemoryUsage() to sum across non-null writers. Real issue, medium severity.

confirmed.title: value_count populated from parquet num_values (includes nulls), inconsistent with DuckDB writer path and over-counts total rows downstream
confirmed.detail: totalValueCount += columnMeta.getNum_values(); parquet num_values is the total count INCLUDING nulls. But the catalog treats value_count as the non-null count: DuckDbFileWriter.java:461-474 sets valueCount = COUNT(col) (non-null) and nullCount = totalCount - valueCount, so value_count + null_count = row count there. In DucklakeMetadata.getTableStatistics (lines 478-485) the consumer computes totalCount = totalValueCount + totalNullCount and (a) uses it as the denominator for nullsFraction and (b) guards `totalCount != activeDataFileRowCount` to decide whether to expose the column's stats. For any parquet-written column containing nulls, totalCount = rows + nullCount > rows, so nullsFraction is wrong AND the guard trips, silently suppressing that column's statistics. The two writer paths also disagree on value_count meaning for the same logical table.
confirmed.suggestion: Store the non-null count: totalValueCount += (num_values - null_count) for that row group (matching DuckDbFileWriter), or otherwise reconcile so value_count + null_count == row count across both writers.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Independently reproduced across all layers. (1) DucklakeStatsExtractor.java:89 does `totalValueCount += columnMeta.getNum_values()` and separately :94 `totalNullCount += stats.getNull_count()`, then emits both into DucklakeFileColumnStats (:112-116). Parquet num_values is the total value count INCLUDING nulls (confirmed via Trino's ParquetMetadata.java:188 mapping num_values directly to the column-chunk value count). No null subtraction occurs. (2) The spec authority — the vendored DuckDB ducklake C++ extension — is explicit at vendor/pg_ducklake/third_party/ducklake/src/storage/ducklake_transaction.cpp:1894-1901: "value_count should be the count of non-null values: num_values - null_count" and computes `value_count = num_values - null_count`. The add_files test add_files_nested_list_struct_nulls.test:7 reinforces: "The fix ensures: value_count = num_values - null_count (not just num_values)". So the canonical contract is value_count = non-null count. (3) The other writer, DuckDbFileWriter.java:433/439/458/461/474, uses totalCount=COUNT(*), valueCount=COUNT(col) (non-null), nullCount=totalCount-valueCount, so value_count+null_count==row_count there — matching spec and DISAGREEING with the parquet path for the same logical table. (4) Consumer DucklakeMetadata.getTableStatistics:478 computes totalCount = totalValueCount + totalNullCount, then :479 guards `totalCount != activeDataFileRowCount` (suppressing the column's stats when tripped) and :485 uses totalCount as the nullsFraction denominator. JdbcDucklakeCatalog.getColumnStats:542-543 confirms VALUE_COUNT and NULL_COUNT are read straight back and summed. For any parquet/add_files column with k nulls, totalCount = rows + k > rows, so the guard trips (silently dropping that column's min/max/range/nullsFraction) and nullsFraction = k/(rows+k) is wrong. (5) The extractor's own unit test TestDucklakeStatsExtractor.java:232-233 codifies the bug: with num_values=20, null_count=3 it asserts valueCount()==20 and nullCount()==3 (sum 23 != 20 rows). This is not listed among the briefing's intentional decisions or known gaps (the only related note, "Conservative stats suppression," covers delete files / mixed-format / incomplete evolution — not nulls in a fully-covered parquet column). The fix in the finding (totalValueCount += num_values - null_count for that row group) matches the spec and the sibling writer. Medium severity is right: silent stats suppression and wrong nullsFraction degrade planning but do not corrupt data.

confirmed.title: Per-key lock removed inside synchronized block lets two threads run the critical section concurrently
confirmed.detail: keyLocks.remove(key, lock) runs in the finally while still holding the lock. Sequence: thread A holds lock1, B has already done computeIfAbsent and obtained the SAME lock1 and is blocked on synchronized(lock1). A finishes, runs remove(key, lock1) (succeeds, mapping cleared), exits synchronized. Now thread C calls computeIfAbsent -> creates a NEW lock2 and enters synchronized(lock2). B then wakes and enters synchronized(lock1). B and C are now in the critical section simultaneously guarded by different monitors. Both compute the same partial path (key + ".partial") and the same local path. If isReady is false (e.g. size mismatch / first fetch), both run Files.deleteIfExists(partial) + downloadTo(...partial) + Files.move(partial, local) on the SAME partial file: one thread's transferTo writes into a file the other just deleted or is mid-moving, and one move can fail with NoSuchFileException or move a half-written file. The lock therefore does not actually serialize same-key downloaders as the Javadoc claims ("only one downloader runs at a time").
confirmed.suggestion: Do not remove the lock entry inside the synchronized block. Either keep the lock interned for the JVM lifetime (a small unbounded map of keys is cheap and matches the no-eviction design), or only remove it via an atomic scheme that cannot hand a stale monitor to a waiting thread. Simplest correct fix: drop the keyLocks.remove(...) finally entirely.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The race is real and independently reproducible. At line 98 `keyLocks.computeIfAbsent(key, _ -> new Object())` interns a per-key monitor; line 99 guards the critical section with it; line 112 `keyLocks.remove(key, lock)` runs in the finally WHILE still inside synchronized(lock). This is the classic broken "remove interned lock inside the guarded region" idiom: a thread B that already obtained lock1 and is parked on synchronized(lock1) can be stranded — once A removes the mapping (line 112) and exits, a later thread C computeIfAbsent gets a fresh lock2 and enters synchronized(lock2) concurrently with B (which then wakes on lock1). Two threads are now in the critical section under different monitors, so the Javadoc claim at lines 45-46 ("only one downloader runs at a time") does not hold.
