Same-key concurrency is realistic: the cache is a Guice @Singleton (DucklakeModule.java:138) and materialize() is invoked from DucklakePageSourceProvider.resolveDuckDbAttachTarget (line 662), called per-split across Trino's concurrent driver threads on a worker; multiple splits over the same duckdb-format .db file produce concurrent calls with an identical key.

The partial path at line 104 is purely key-derived (`key + ".partial"`, no thread/UUID component) and the key is SHA-256 of (remotePath, fileSize) — so two concurrent same-key critical sections collide on the exact same partial file. downloadTo (line 137) truncates it with Files.newOutputStream while the other thread may be mid-transferTo or mid-move; Files.move at line 107 can then fail with NoSuchFileException (partial already moved by the peer) and propagate as a query error, or move a half-written file. The lock-removal-after-exception path makes this concretely reachable: if A's download/move throws, its finally still removes the lock with local absent/invalid, so a waiting B and a new C both proceed into the colliding critical section with isReady(local) false at line 101.

Severity downgraded from high to medium: the read-back size guard in isReady (lines 123-128) deletes torn `local` files on the next read, so the failure mode is a transient/spurious query error under concurrent cold same-key fetches that self-heals on retry, not silent wrong-result data corruption in steady state. The EXPERIMENTAL-path briefing note covers API churn, not concurrency correctness, so it does not excuse this. The reviewer's suggested fix (drop the keyLocks.remove(...) finally and keep keys interned for JVM lifetime, matching the no-eviction design) is correct.

confirmed.title: Unconditional cast to GroupColumnIO for ArrayType throws ClassCastException on 2-level Parquet lists
confirmed.detail: Line 91 casts `columnIO` to `GroupColumnIO` without first checking whether it is a `PrimitiveColumnIO`. Parquet's backward-compatibility rules allow a 2-level list encoding where the repeated field is a primitive (not a group). Trino's own `ParquetTypeUtils.constructField` (lines 351-356 of the reference copy) handles this with an explicit `if (columnIO instanceof PrimitiveColumnIO)` branch. `add_files` registers external Parquet files written by any tool (Spark, legacy Hive writers, etc.) — those files may carry 2-level LIST schemas. Hitting this path throws `ClassCastException` at query time, crashing the page-source for any split over such a file.
confirmed.suggestion: Mirror Trino's handling: before line 91, add `if (columnIO instanceof PrimitiveColumnIO primitiveColumnIO)` and, after validating repetition level and definition level are non-zero, build a `PrimitiveField` with adjusted levels and wrap it in a `GroupField` — exactly as the reference does. Only fall through to the `(GroupColumnIO)` cast when `columnIO` is not a `PrimitiveColumnIO`.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is fundamentally correct. In DucklakeParquetTypeUtils.java line 91, `(GroupColumnIO) columnIO` is an unconditional cast in the ArrayType branch with no prior `instanceof PrimitiveColumnIO` check. Trino's reference ParquetTypeUtils.constructField (deps-reference copy lines 338-357) explicitly handles the Parquet backward-compat 2-level list encoding — where a `repeated <primitive> colors` field surfaces as a PrimitiveColumnIO rather than a GroupColumnIO — by branching on `columnIO instanceof PrimitiveColumnIO`, validating REPEATED/levels, and synthesizing a GroupField over a PrimitiveField with `repetitionLevel-1`/`definitionLevel-1`. The Ducklake copy omits that branch entirely, so a 2-level list element column reaches line 91 and a `PrimitiveColumnIO` is cast to `GroupColumnIO` → ClassCastException.

Reachability is real, not theoretical: constructField is called on the main Parquet read path (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:503) for every column, and the surrounding code (lines 481-491) explicitly supports files registered via `add_files` from external writers. DucklakeAddFilesTypeChecker compatibility for ArrayType relies on `target.equals(source)` (line 69) where the source ArrayType is derived from the Parquet schema by Trino's type machinery — both 2-level and 3-level encodings deserialize to the same ArrayType, so the add_files gate does NOT reject 2-level lists. Thus an externally-added Parquet file (e.g. legacy Hive/Avro-derived writer) with a 2-level LIST column passes registration and then crashes at scan time.

Two corrections to the finding's literal claims: (1) the exception is not a raw ClassCastException at query time — it is caught by the `catch (IOException | RuntimeException e)` at DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:551 and rethrown wrapped as `RuntimeException("Failed to create Parquet page source...")`, so the split/query still fails but with a wrapped message. (2) Severity is overstated as high: the crash only affects array columns physically encoded as the legacy 2-level form in externally-added files; connector-INSERT-written files and modern 3-level external files (the default for current Spark/parquet-mr) are unaffected. That is a genuine robustness/correctness gap on a legacy-encoding edge case rather than a broad failure, so medium is the right severity. The same omission also applies to the MapType branch (line 80) by analogy, though the finding scopes only the array case.

confirmed.title: getSnapshotAtOrBefore materializes the entire snapshot table to find one row
confirmed.detail: getSnapshotAtOrBefore fetches and maps every row of ducklake_snapshot ordered by snapshot_id DESC, then streams + filters in Java for the first snapshot whose time is <= timestamp. For a catalog with many snapshots this transfers and allocates the whole table on every time-travel-by-timestamp resolution, when the database can return the single row directly.
confirmed.suggestion: Push the predicate into SQL: WHERE snapshot_time <= ? ORDER BY snapshot_id DESC LIMIT 1, fetchOptional. This returns at most one row and lets the DB use the snapshot ordering.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The code is exactly as described. JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:238-243 does selectFrom(snap).orderBy(SNAPSHOT_ID.desc()).fetch(toDucklakeSnapshot) — fetching and mapping every row of ducklake_snapshot — then .stream().filter(s -> !s.snapshotTime().isAfter(timestamp)).findFirst() in Java. SNAPSHOT_TIME is a real column (set at line 1211 via DSL.currentOffsetDateTime()), so the predicate is fully SQL-expressible. The Java filter !snapshotTime().isAfter(timestamp) is exactly snapshot_time <= ?, so the suggested rewrite WHERE SNAPSHOT_TIME <= ? ORDER BY SNAPSHOT_ID DESC LIMIT 1 + fetchOptional is semantically identical regardless of whether snapshot_time is monotonic with snapshot_id (ordering is still by snapshot_id DESC). ducklake_snapshot grows one row per write transaction (every INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE/DDL commit), so on a long-lived catalog it can be large, and the current approach transfers/allocates the whole table plus a DucklakeSnapshot object per row.\n\nMitigating context lowers severity from medium to low: this is NOT a hot/inner-loop path. The only production caller is DucklakeSnapshotResolver.resolveSnapshotIdAtOrBefore (line 113), invoked at most once per query-plan, and only for timestamp-based time travel (FOR TIMESTAMP AS OF). The common current-snapshot path already uses MAX(SNAPSHOT_ID) (line 215) and is unaffected. So it is a real, easily-fixed inefficiency but bounded to per-plan cost of one query variant, not a per-row or per-split cost. Not listed in the briefing's intentional decisions or known gaps. The fix is correct and worth making, but the medium severity overstates the practical impact.

confirmed.title: Locale-sensitive toLowerCase on view dialect
confirmed.detail: isViewAccessible does view.dialect().toLowerCase() with the default JVM locale. If the JVM runs in the Turkish locale, an uppercase 'I' in a dialect string lowercases to a dotless 'ı', so a dialect that should match a Trino marker could fail to match (or vice versa). The same default-locale toLowerCase pattern appears in getTableProperties for column names (line 317).
confirmed.suggestion: Use toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT) for all internal case-folding comparisons, consistent with the explicit Locale.ROOT already used at line 317's column map only partially.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Line 1495 does `view.dialect().toLowerCase()` with the default JVM locale, and the comparison target is `TRINO_VIEW_DIALECT = "trino/brikk"` (line 134). So the locale-sensitive toLowerCase is real and matches the finding's description. Line 317 also uses toLowerCase, but it explicitly passes `java.util.Locale.ROOT`, so that call is actually correct — the suggestion's claim that 317 is "only partially" using ROOT is wrong; ROOT is the sole toLowerCase in that block and is applied correctly.

The asserted correctness consequence at 1495 is essentially unreachable, which is why severity stays low: (1) Every view this connector writes stores the dialect as the literal constant `"trino/brikk"` (lines 1381, 1422, 1461), all lowercase — toLowerCase is a no-op for self-written views. (2) For the Turkish-I trap to drop a should-match view, some *external* writer would have to store this brikk-specific marker in uppercase (e.g. "TRINO/BRIKK"), which under tr_TR lowercases to "trıno/brıkk" (dotless ı) and fails .equals. No other engine writes the "brikk" dialect string, and per the project briefing cross-dialect views are intentionally filtered out anyway. (3) The marker contains no uppercase chars, so even the canonical value is immune. 

Net: the locale-omission is a genuine consistency/hygiene nit worth fixing for defensiveness (use Locale.ROOT to match line 317), but the described data-loss scenario cannot realistically be triggered. Confirmed as a real but very low-impact code-quality issue, severity low.

confirmed.title: RowIdInjectingPageSource does not propagate getMetrics() (or isBlocked()) from delegate
confirmed.detail: RowIdInjectingPageSource overrides most ConnectorPageSource methods but omits getMetrics() and isBlocked(), so the SPI defaults apply. Since this wrapper is inserted on every DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE read (whenever $row_id is projected), the underlying ParquetPageSource/DuckDbFilePageSource metrics are silently lost from query stats. isBlocked() defaulting to a completed future is currently harmless because neither delegate blocks, but it is a latent hazard if a blocking delegate is added.
confirmed.suggestion: Forward getMetrics() and isBlocked() to the delegate, matching what TransformConnectorPageSource does.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced. RowIdInjectingPageSource (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:953-1041) overrides getCompletedBytes/getCompletedPositions/getReadTimeNanos/isFinished/getMemoryUsage/getNextSourcePage/close but does NOT override getMetrics() or isBlocked(), so the ConnectorPageSource SPI defaults apply: getMetrics() returns Metrics.EMPTY and isBlocked() returns NOT_BLOCKED (verified in deps-reference ConnectorPageSource.java:83-97).

The wrapper is inserted on the Parquet path at line 541 and the DuckDB path at line 621, conditional only on rowIdOutputPosition >= 0 (i.e. whenever $row_id is projected — DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE reads). On the Parquet path the delegate is ParquetPageSource, whose getMetrics() (deps-reference ParquetPageSource.java:115-118) returns parquetReader.getMetrics(), which carries real metrics: codecMetrics, COLUMN_INDEX_ROWS_FILTERED, and dataSource.getMetrics() (ParquetReader.java:735-745). Those are silently dropped by the wrapper.

The further wrapping by TransformConnectorPageSource in applyDeleteFile (line 701) does NOT save it: TransformConnectorPageSource forwards getMetrics()/isBlocked() faithfully to its delegate (deps-reference TransformConnectorPageSource.java:114-123), and that delegate is the RowIdInjectingPageSource, so the drop propagates to the outermost source. So the loss occurs both with and without delete files on the Parquet path.

Scope/severity: impact is observability only (EXPLAIN ANALYZE / OperatorStats lose Parquet codec + column-index-filtered metrics on $row_id reads); no query-result correctness impact. The DuckDB delegate (DuckDbFilePageSource) does not override getMetrics() itself, so nothing is lost there, and neither delegate has a custom isBlocked(), so the isBlocked() concern is purely latent — as the finding itself states. Not listed in the briefing as intentional or a known gap. Low severity is correct (arguably 'observability' rather than 'correctness', but the finding does not overstate it). Suggestion to forward both to the delegate is valid and matches TransformConnectorPageSource.

confirmed.title: effectivePredicate.filter uses O(n) List.contains per domain entry
confirmed.detail: filePredicate is computed with effectivePredicate.filter((col, _) -> fileColumns.contains(col)) where fileColumns is an ArrayList. For each predicate column this does a linear scan of fileColumns, giving O(predicateColumns * fileColumns) per split. Negligible for small projections but needless.
confirmed.suggestion: Build a Set<DucklakeColumnHandle> (or Set of column ids) from fileColumns once and test membership against it.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The mechanism is real. At DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:603-604, `effectivePredicate.filter((col, _) -> fileColumns.contains(col))` is applied. TupleDomain.filter (deps-reference TupleDomain.java:614-623) delegates to transformDomains, invoking the BiPredicate once per predicate domain entry. fileColumns is an ArrayList (built at line 580-588), so each `contains(col)` is a linear scan with record-based equals on DucklakeColumnHandle (it's a `record`, line 30 of DucklakeColumnHandle.java, so equals compares all components including columnType). Net cost is O(predicateColumns x fileColumns) with non-trivial per-comparison cost. The suggestion to build a Set once is correct and would make it O(predicateColumns). However the practical impact is what the finding itself states: negligible. This code runs once per split in createDuckDbPageSource (not per page or per row — line 616 constructs the page source after this), and both collections are small in practice (projected columns and pushed predicate columns, typically single to low-double digits). There is no framework guard that makes the Set version necessary, and nothing in the briefing covers this. So it is a genuine but minor micro-inefficiency: real, correctly described, and accurately self-rated as low. I would not raise it above the lowest severity.

confirmed.title: DATE constant outside 4-digit-year range emits a literal DuckDB cannot parse
confirmed.detail: translateConstant() formats a DATE constant via LocalDate.ofEpochDay(days).toString(). For years <1000 or BC dates LocalDate emits zero-padded/negative forms, and for years >9999 it emits a leading-'+' form like '+10000-01-01'. DuckDB's DATE literal parser does not accept the '+'-prefixed form, so the generated fragment (DATE '+10000-01-01') makes the whole pushed WHERE clause fail to parse at the DuckDB side rather than the translator failing the conjunct cleanly. Trino's DATE range is bounded to int days so extreme values are reachable.
confirmed.suggestion: Range-check the epoch-day value (reject years outside DuckDB's accepted 4-digit literal range, or format with an explicit zero-padded/era-safe pattern that DuckDB accepts) and return null when out of range so the conjunct stays unpushed.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The cited code at DuckDbExpressionTranslator.java:463-467 formats a DATE constant as `DATE '" + LocalDate.ofEpochDay(days) + "'`. I verified empirically that LocalDate.toString() produces year-out-of-4-digit forms across the reachable input range: Integer.MAX_VALUE days -> '+5881580-07-11' (leading '+'), Integer.MIN_VALUE -> '-5877641-06-23', BC dates -> '-0768-02-04'/'-0005-01-01'. DateType.java:57-59 confirms Trino DATE is int days (range Integer.MIN..MAX), so years far beyond 9999 and negative/BC years are representable, not just theoretical.

LocalDate.ofEpochDay does NOT throw for any int-range day (LocalDate supports years to 999,999,999), so translate()'s RuntimeException guard (lines 396-402) does not catch this — the malformed literal IS produced and AND-ed into the pushed WHERE for .db splits (DucklakeMetadata.java:627-630, DuckDbSelectSqlBuilder.java:73-78). InProcessDuckDbExecutor.java:93-109 shows the SELECT is executed directly and any SQLException is rethrown with no fallback to an unpushed query, so a DuckDB parse rejection of the '+'/'-'-prefixed literal would fail the whole query rather than degrade cleanly. This violates the translator's documented 'fail the conjunct cleanly / never push something unparseable' contract. The identical defective pattern also exists in DuckDbWhereClauseTranslator.java:241-243 (the more common domain path for date_col = DATE '...'), so the surface is broader than the one finding cites.

Not listed in the briefing as intentional or a known gap. Severity correctly low: extreme/BC date constants in predicates are rare in practice. Confidence is medium rather than high because I could not run DuckDB to confirm its date parser actually rejects the '+'-prefixed extended form (the finding's core external assumption), and whether Trino's planner routes such extreme constants through the expression path vs domain path — though both paths carry the same bug.

confirmed.title: N+1 fetchExists probe per inlined-data table during split planning
confirmed.detail: The stream filter calls catalog.hasInlinedRows(...) once per DucklakeInlinedDataInfo, and each call issues a separate JDBC fetchExists (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.hasInlinedRows line 786-799). getInlinedDataInfos already returned one row per schema_version, so this is a per-element round trip. Usually few schema versions so impact is small, but it is an avoidable per-element query at planning time.
confirmed.suggestion: Have the catalog batch the live-rows check across all (tableId, schemaVersion) pairs in a single query, or fold the EXISTS check into getInlinedDataInfos so the planner makes one round trip.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding accurately describes a real per-element JDBC round trip. DucklakeSplitManager.java:132-133 streams over the inlinedDataInfos returned by getInlinedDataInfos and calls catalog.hasInlinedRows(...) once per element. In JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:786-799, hasInlinedRows issues an independent dsl.fetchExists (a SELECT 1 ... LIMIT-style EXISTS) against the physical table ducklake_inlined_data_<tableId>_<schemaVersion>. Since getInlinedDataInfos (lines 741-776) returns one row per schema version, this is genuinely N separate EXISTS queries at split-planning time — the claimed consequence holds.

Two factors keep severity at low (consistent with the reporter's own framing): (1) The path is cold. For tables that never used DATA_INLINING_ROW_LIMIT, ducklake_inlined_data_tables is empty or absent (DataAccessException caught at line 778-782), so getInlinedDataInfos returns an empty list and the stream filter runs zero times. (2) When inlined data does exist, N is bounded by the number of schema versions carrying inlined data — a small handful even under schema evolution.

I'd also note the reporter understated the pattern: getInlinedDataInfos itself ALREADY performs a per-element probe via inlinedTable.existsAsTable(dsl) (line 764, definition at 977-986, which runs SELECT 1 FROM <table> WHERE false per row). So the hasInlinedRows probe is the second per-element round trip in this code path, not the first. The suggested 'batch in one query' fix is non-trivial because each schema version is a distinct physical table (line 964), so batching would require a dynamically built UNION ALL of EXISTS clauses rather than a simple IN-list — the same N table reads folded into one statement. The cleaner suggestion (fold the EXISTS into getInlinedDataInfos) is reasonable and would also subsume the existing existsAsTable probe.

Real but negligible-in-practice efficiency nitpick; not listed in the briefing as intentional or a known gap. Confirmed at low severity.

confirmed.title: DuckDB connection leaked when ATTACH/CREATE TABLE/createAppender fails in constructor
confirmed.detail: On the SQLException path the constructor deletes the local temp file but never closes the already-opened DuckDBConnection. If stmt.execute(ATTACH/CREATE TABLE) or connection.createAppender(...) throws, the in-memory DuckDB connection (and its native engine handle) is leaked for the lifetime of the JVM/pool. The sibling DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter constructor explicitly closeQuietly(conn) on the same failure path (line 218), confirming this is an oversight here.
confirmed.suggestion: In the catch block, close the connection (best-effort, swallowing any secondary SQLException) before deleting the temp file and rethrowing as IOException.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced. In DuckDbFileWriter.java the constructor opens a native DuckDB connection at line 144 (this.connection = (DuckDBConnection) DriverManager.getConnection("jdbc:duckdb:")). The subsequent ATTACH (146-149), CREATE TABLE (150), and connection.createAppender(...) (152) can each throw SQLException. The catch block at lines 154-162 only does Files.deleteIfExists(localTempFile) and rethrows as IOException — it never closes the already-open connection. Because the constructor throws, no instance is published and no later close() can run.

The sibling DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter (lines 194-226) handles the identical init sequence with a nullable local `conn` and on the SQLException path explicitly calls closeQuietly(conn) at line 218 (plus stream/reader/allocator). This confirms the omission in DuckDbFileWriter is an oversight, not an intentional pattern.

No external guard compensates: the only caller, DucklakePageSink.openDuckDbWriter (line 352), returns the constructed writer; closeWriter (384-393) operates on writers.get(index), which is never populated when the constructor throws. So the open DuckDB connection and its native engine handle become unreachable and are leaked until GC/finalization (if any), not deterministically closed.

Severity downgraded from medium to low: the leak only occurs on the rare init-failure path (ATTACH/CREATE on a fresh local temp .db with controlled SQL, then createAppender), so accumulation requires repeated failures. The fix is a one-line best-effort close in the catch, matching the sibling. Not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing.

confirmed.title: Unguarded asGroupType() on MAP key_value child can escape as ClassCastException
confirmed.detail: mapMap calls group.getType(0).asGroupType() with no isPrimitive() guard, unlike mapList (line 348) which explicitly checks !middle.isPrimitive() before recursing. A parquet MAP whose single child is a primitive (a malformed or non-standard 2-level encoding) makes asGroupType() throw a parquet ClassCastException ("... is not a group"). The caller at DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java:296 only catches DucklakeAddFilesException, so this propagates as a raw internal error rather than a clean INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT with a useful message. Every other malformed-shape case in this class is reported as a DucklakeAddFilesException.
confirmed.suggestion: Guard before casting: if (group.getType(0).isPrimitive()) throw new DucklakeAddFilesException("Column \"%s\" in file \"%s\" has unexpected MAP shape (key_value child must be a group)"). Then call asGroupType().
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The finding is accurate. At DucklakeAddFilesNameMapper.java:377 mapMap calls group.getType(0).asGroupType() with no isPrimitive() guard. The parallel mapList at line 348 explicitly guards `!middle.isPrimitive()` before its asGroupType() call — the author's own code proves they know asGroupType() throws ClassCastException on primitives (org.apache.parquet.schema.Type.asGroupType() returns this for groups, otherwise throws "... is not a group"). The bad shape is reachable: mapMap is dispatched solely on the Trino target type being a MapType (line 289); the parquet side is verified only to be a non-primitive group at the top level (line 282) and to have exactly one child field (line 372). Nothing checks that the single key_value child (group.getType(0)) is itself a group before line 377, so a malformed/non-standard 2-level MAP with a primitive child reaches the cast. The consequence is real: the only caller, DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java:296, catches only DucklakeAddFilesException and rethrows as INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT, so a raw ClassCastException escapes as an internal error instead of the clean, message-bearing error every other malformed-shape branch in this class produces. The suggested guard matches the existing mapList idiom and the surrounding error-reporting convention. Downgraded to low: this only affects error-message quality for structurally invalid hand-crafted parquet input on the user-invoked add_files procedure — no data-correctness or wrong-results impact, and standard parquet writers always emit a key_value group child.

confirmed.title: Footer is read from the data source twice per file (extra tail round-trip)
confirmed.detail: MetadataReader.readFooter(dataSource, ...) already performs a tail read of the file footer (a blind ~48KB tail read, or a sized read), and that tail necessarily includes the 8-byte post-script (4-byte LE footer length + magic). readFooterLengthFromPostScript() then issues a SECOND, independent dataSource.readTail(8). ParquetDataSource.readTail is uncached (AbstractParquetDataSource.readTail -> readFully every call), so on object stores (S3/GCS) this is an additional network round trip for every single file in the add_files batch, purely to recover 4 bytes that were already on the wire during readFooter.
confirmed.suggestion: Wrap the input in FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource (or a small caching wrapper) before readFooter so the post-script bytes are reused, or derive the footer length from the bytes readFooter already fetched, instead of issuing a separate readTail(8) per file.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The double-read is real. In MetadataReader.readFooter (deps-reference io/trino/parquet/reader/MetadataReader.java) line 100 does `buffer = dataSource.readTail(expectedReadSize)` (the blind/sized tail read that necessarily includes the 8-byte post-script, validated >= POST_SCRIPT_SIZE at line 98), and line 106 extracts `metadataLength = buffer.getInt(buffer.length() - POST_SCRIPT_SIZE)` — i.e. the exact footer-length value the procedure wants is already computed from bytes already on the wire. Then DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java:318 calls readFooterLengthFromPostScript(dataSource), which at line 385 issues a SECOND `dataSource.readTail(8)`. readTail is uncached: AbstractParquetDataSource.readTail (line 96) -> readTailInternal (line 61) -> readFully -> abstract readInternal (real IO every call); TrinoParquetDataSource.readTailInternal (line 51) delegates straight to input.readTail with no caching. So on object stores this is a genuine extra network round trip per file to recover 4 bytes already fetched.

Two mitigations bound the impact, hence I downgrade to low: (1) createDataSource (ParquetPageSourceFactory.java:325-328) returns the uncached TrinoParquetDataSource only when fileSize > smallFileThreshold; for files at/below threshold it returns MemoryParquetDataSource which buffers the whole file, so the second readTail(8) is served from memory with no extra round trip. add_files always passes a known size (DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java:274), so large warehouse parquet files do hit the uncached path and incur the round trip, but small files do not. (2) This is add_files — a one-shot bulk registration procedure, not a per-query hot path; the cost is one extra ~8-byte tail read per file at registration time only, not per scan. The fix is real and cheap (the suggested FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource wrapper would cache the post-script), but note ParquetMetadata returned by readFooter does not expose the footer length, so the alternative "derive from the bytes readFooter fetched" isn't directly available without the wrapper. Technically correct finding, modest real-world impact.

confirmed.title: Malformed parquet MAP shape throws ClassCastException instead of DucklakeAddFilesException
confirmed.detail: mapMap() calls group.getType(0).asGroupType() with no isPrimitive() guard. mapList() at line 348 explicitly guards primitiveness, but mapMap does not. If a column whose catalog type is MAP is backed by a parquet group whose single child is a primitive (malformed/foreign LIST/MAP encoding), asGroupType() throws a raw ClassCastException. That escapes the surrounding try that only catches DucklakeAddFilesException (procedure line 296-298), so instead of a clean INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT the user gets an unmapped 500/internal error.
confirmed.suggestion: Before group.getType(0).asGroupType(), check group.getType(0).isPrimitive() and throw a DucklakeAddFilesException with the MAP-shape message, mirroring the guard in mapList().
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The finding reproduces independently. In DucklakeAddFilesNameMapper.java, mapMap() at line 377 calls `group.getType(0).asGroupType()` with no isPrimitive() guard, whereas the analogous mapList() at line 348 explicitly checks `!middle.isPrimitive()` before calling asGroupType(). org.apache.parquet.schema.Type.asGroupType() throws a raw ClassCastException when invoked on a PrimitiveType (GroupType overrides it to return this; PrimitiveType inherits the throwing base impl). Reachability: mapMap is entered purely because the catalog/Trino target type is MapType (mapField line 289), independent of the parquet file's logical annotation — the parquet schema comes straight from fileMetadata.getSchema() (footer-read, unvalidated). So a malformed/foreign parquet file whose MAP-targeted group has a single primitive child reaches line 377 and throws CCE. The caller wraps the map() call in a try that catches ONLY DucklakeAddFilesException (DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java lines 293-298, converting to INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT); a CCE escapes that catch and surfaces as an unmapped internal error instead of a clean INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT. The mapList guard at line 348 is direct evidence the author intended this defense and missed it for maps. Not listed as an intentional decision or known gap; the "trusted catalog on read paths" convention does not cover add_files on user-supplied parquet files. Severity correctly low: only reachable on adversarial/malformed input, no data corruption or wrong results — the operation fails either way; only error classification/quality degrades. The suggested fix (guard isPrimitive() and throw DucklakeAddFilesException mirroring mapList) is appropriate.

confirmed.title: Unquoted 'NULL' token collides with blob/varchar payloads and non-finite floats
confirmed.detail: The unquoted-token branch maps any token equal to "NULL" to a SQL null (line 214). Unquoted tokens cover numbers, booleans, and (per the cross-engine blob test) bare \xNN blob text. A list<blob> element whose bytes spell exactly N,U,L,L is serialized unquoted as the token 'NULL' and is therefore mis-read as a NULL element instead of a 4-byte blob. Separately, for list<double>/list<real>, DuckDB renders non-finite values as 'inf', '-inf', and 'nan' as bare tokens; these reach toDouble/toFloat which call Double.parseDouble("inf")/Float.parseFloat("nan"), and Java's parser expects "Infinity"/"NaN", so the read throws NumberFormatException. Neither case is covered by the audit (only finite values [1.5,-2.25,0.0,NULL] are tested).
confirmed.suggestion: For the float/double element path, map DuckDB's inf/-inf/nan tokens to Double.POSITIVE_INFINITY/NEGATIVE_INFINITY/NaN before delegating to the numeric parsers. For the NULL-vs-blob collision, distinguish a genuine null sentinel from blob/varchar payload by element type (only treat the bare NULL token as null for non-binary element types, decoding it as blob bytes otherwise).
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Both sub-claims hold up against the actual code in /Users/jminard/DEV/brikk/repos/ducklake-integrations/jvm/trino-ducklake/src/dev/brikk/ducklake/trino/plugin/DucklakeInlinedValueConverter.java.

NULL-vs-blob collision: parseDucklakeListText (lines 208-215) maps ANY unquoted token equal to "NULL" to Java null (line 214), before any element-type dispatch. convertArray (lines 127-131) then calls convertJdbcValue(null, elementType), which returns null at line 69 — never reaching the VarbinaryType blob-decode branch (lines 102-108). The converter's own test confirms blobs are serialized UNQUOTED in list literals: TestDucklakeInlinedValueConverter.java lines 31-32 state "BLOB '\x00\x01\xFF' is serialized as plain \x00\x01\xFF (no quoting — the list-cast LOOKUP_TABLE doesn't flag '\\' so the element stays unquoted)". A blob whose bytes are 0x4E 0x55 0x4C 0x4C ("NULL") are all regular printable ASCII, so DuckDB emits the bare token NULL, and line 214 silently turns a 4-byte blob into a SQL null. Mechanically real.

Non-finite floats: list<double>/list<real> arrive as a single VARCHAR (per comment lines 135-136) parsed into String tokens by parseDucklakeListText (token is a String, line 213). For non-finite values DuckDB renders bare 'inf'/'-inf'/'nan' (confirmed by dev-docs/RESEARCH-function-mapping.md:109 and TODO-READ-MODE). These Strings reach toFloat (lines 319-325) / toDouble (lines 327-333), which call Float.parseFloat / Double.parseDouble — Java requires "Infinity"/"NaN", so these throw NumberFormatException. Real robustness gap (loud failure, no corruption).

Coverage gap confirmed: TestDucklakeCrossEngineTypeAudit.java lines 308-331 test only finite list double/real values [1.5,-2.25,0.0,NULL]; the blob test (lines 440-457) uses '\x00\x01\xFF', never a blob spelling NULL. Neither corner case is exercised. The finding is not in the intentional-decisions or known-gaps lists.

Severity correctly low: both are narrow corner cases (a blob whose bytes are exactly the 4 chars NULL; non-finite floats inside an inlined list) on a path the docs already mark as partial. The float case fails loudly (exception); the blob case mis-reads silently but needs a very specific payload. The non-finite parsing fix is trivially safe and worth taking; the NULL/blob distinction-by-element-type fix is also reasonable.

confirmed.title: Decimal conversion silently rounds (HALF_UP) instead of preserving stored scale
confirmed.detail: toDecimal unconditionally calls setScale(decimalType.getScale(), HALF_UP) (line 460). If a driver/jOOQ returns a BigDecimal with more fractional digits than the column scale, the value is silently rounded rather than reflecting what DuckDB stored, producing a value that diverges from a Parquet read of the same column. Inlined DuckDB decimals normally already carry the column scale so this is usually a no-op, but the silent HALF_UP masks any scale mismatch (including from the String fallback path, new BigDecimal(text)) instead of surfacing it.
confirmed.suggestion: Use setScale without rounding (default UNNECESSARY) so a genuine scale mismatch throws loudly, or document/justify why HALF_UP rounding on inlined reads is acceptable and aligned with the Parquet read path.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The code at DucklakeInlinedValueConverter.java:451-466 does exactly what the finding describes: toDecimal unconditionally calls decimal.setScale(decimalType.getScale(), RoundingMode.HALF_UP) (line 460) for both the BigDecimal input path and the String-fallback path (new BigDecimal(toStringValue(value)), line 458). The setScale itself is mandatory — Trino's InMemoryRecordSet representation (long for short decimals via longValueExact(), Int128 otherwise) requires the unscaled value at exactly the column scale. So the issue is purely the rounding mode.

The finding is corroborated by Trino's own SPI conventions in io/trino/spi/type/Decimals.java: encodeShortScaledValue(BigDecimal,scale) and encodeScaledValue(BigDecimal,scale) both delegate with RoundingMode.UNNECESSARY (lines 142, 155), and writeBigDecimal does value.setScale(type.getScale(), UNNECESSARY) (line 247). Decimals.valueOf even documents 'It is caller responsibility to ensure that value.scale() equals DecimalType.getScale()' (line 278). The idiomatic Trino approach therefore throws loudly on a genuine scale mismatch; this code instead silently rounds, which masks the mismatch — exactly the finding's claim. The suggestion (use UNNECESSARY) aligns with the SPI's own pattern.

I could not fully refute it. However, the practical consequence is bounded: correctly-stored DuckDB inlined decimals already carry the column's declared scale, so setScale is a no-op and there is no divergence from a Parquet read in normal operation. A divergence requires catalog corruption or a driver returning unexpected precision, and the project briefing states read paths assume a trusted catalog with intentionally minimal catalog-value validation. So the finding is a legitimate code-quality nitpick (deviation from Trino's loud-on-mismatch convention) rather than a bug that produces wrong user-visible data under normal, trusted-catalog conditions. 'low' severity is correct and arguably generous.

confirmed.title: NPE when SET TimeZone fails with a null-message SQLException masks the real error
confirmed.detail: In applyServerSideTimeZone, the one-shot WARN calls e.getMessage().lines().findFirst().orElse(e.getMessage()). SQLException.getMessage() can return null (e.g. exceptions chained from a driver that only sets a SQLState or a cause). When the message is null, e.getMessage().lines() throws NullPointerException. That NPE is thrown from inside the catch (SQLException) block, so it propagates out of applyServerSideTimeZone and out of execute() instead of the intended graceful degradation (proceed without SET TimeZone). The actual SET-TimeZone failure is then masked by a confusing NPE, and the split fails entirely rather than falling back to Trino-side evaluation as designed. InProcessDuckDbExecutor.applySessionTimeZone (line 199) has the identical latent bug.
confirmed.suggestion: Capture the message once into a local (String msg = e.getMessage();) and guard for null before calling .lines(), e.g. (msg == null ? e.toString() : msg.lines().findFirst().orElse(msg)).
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The mechanism is a real Java defect. At QuackDuckDbExecutor.java:299 (and the identical InProcessDuckDbExecutor.java:199), the catch(SQLException e) block evaluates `e.getMessage().lines().findFirst().orElse(e.getMessage())`. SQLException.getMessage() is contractually nullable (e.g. new SQLException() no-arg, or new SQLException((Throwable)null)). If null, the `.lines()` call NPEs inside the catch block. That NPE propagates out of applyServerSideTimeZone (invoked at execute() line 135 during attach/init), gets caught by the `catch (SQLException | RuntimeException e)` at line 145, which closes resources and rethrows — so the split fails entirely instead of the intended graceful degradation. The briefing explicitly documents this WARN path as an intentional graceful-degradation design ("fractional offsets fail cleanly and pushdown silently falls back to Trino-side eval with a one-shot WARN"), so a code defect that defeats it is a legitimate correctness concern, not a documented gap. The NPE occurs during argument evaluation before log.warn, so airlift's null-tolerant formatting does not save it.

However, the realistic trigger probability is very low, which is why I keep severity at low (and would not argue against even lower). The SQLException here originates from a DuckDB `SET TimeZone` failure. In the reference driver sources (org/duckdb), every SQLException construction passes a non-null string (createErrMsg(...)) or wraps a non-null cause via `new SQLException(e)` — which yields a non-null message whenever the cause is non-null, as it always is at those sites. DuckDB itself always emits a descriptive error string (e.g. "Conversion Error: Unknown TimeZone ...") for SET TimeZone failures. I found no path that produces a null-message SQLException for query execution. The Quack RPC boundary marginally raises the chance of an odd error shape, but I could not confirm a concrete null-message source. So: real latent defensive-coding bug, mechanism verified at both cited lines, but unlikely to fire in practice. The suggested fix (capture msg once, guard for null) is correct and cheap.

confirmed.title: Documented quack-token min-length (4 chars) is never enforced
confirmed.detail: The @ConfigDescription on setQuackToken states 'Min 4 characters (Quack server-side requirement)', but there is no bean-validation constraint enforcing it. The only check anywhere is the blank check in DucklakeDuckDbExecutorFactory.create() (lines 69-72) and the isQuackEngineConfigComplete() validator (line 385-386), both of which accept any non-blank token. A 1-3 character token therefore passes Trino's config validation cleanly and only fails later at the Quack server during the first split's RPC handshake, surfacing as an opaque per-query runtime error instead of a clear catalog-load-time configuration error.
confirmed.suggestion: Enforce the documented requirement at config validation time, e.g. extend isQuackEngineConfigComplete() to also require quackToken.length() >= 4 (with a message reflecting the 4-char minimum), or add a dedicated @AssertTrue validator so a too-short token is rejected when the catalog is loaded.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The code matches the finding exactly. DucklakeConfig.java:282 documents "Min 4 characters (Quack server-side requirement)" in the @ConfigDescription on setQuackToken, but no bean-validation constraint enforces a minimum length. The only validation present is blank-only: isQuackEngineConfigComplete() (lines 379-387) checks quackToken != null && !quackToken.isBlank(), and DucklakeDuckDbExecutorFactory.create() (lines 69-72) likewise only rejects null/blank tokens with CONFIGURATION_INVALID. A token of length 1-3 passes both checks. So the documented 4-char minimum is genuinely unenforced, and a too-short token would only fail later at the Quack server handshake rather than at catalog load. The technical claim is correct.

Severity is correctly low and should stay low (not upgraded): the Quack engine is explicitly experimental, test-gated behind -Dducklake.test.catalog-backend=DUCKDB_QUACK and "not production" per the PROJECT BRIEFING, so the failure mode affects a non-production code path. It is a real but minor documentation/enforcement mismatch, not listed among intentional decisions or known gaps. The suggested fix (extend isQuackEngineConfigComplete to require quackToken.length() >= 4, or an @AssertTrue) is reasonable.

confirmed.title: Rollover eagerly opens a successor writer that may stay empty, producing a zero-row data file
confirmed.detail: After a writer crosses targetMaxFileSize it is closed and a brand-new writer is opened immediately (writers.set(0, openNewWriter(...)) / writers.set(writerIndex, openNewWriter(...))). openNewWriter creates the destination output stream and registers the path in writtenFilePaths right away. If the rollover happens on the last page (or the last page for that partition), no further write() ever lands on the new writer. finish() then calls closeWriter on it, which runs parquetWriter.close()/finishAndBuildFragment() and emits a DucklakeWriteFragment with recordCount=0. The result is a real, empty (header+footer only) data file written to storage AND a catalog row registering a zero-row file for every rollover boundary that lands on the final page. This bloats the metadata, wastes a remote object per rollover, and pollutes table stats/file listings. The same pattern exists in both appendUnpartitioned (194-197) and appendPartitioned (237-241).
confirmed.suggestion: Open the successor writer lazily on the next write instead of eagerly at rollover: leave the slot null after closeWriter and let appendUnpartitioned/appendPartitioned re-create it on the next page (they already handle the null/empty case). Alternatively, in finish()/closeWriter skip writers whose recordCount is 0 and delete their files.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The mechanism is real and I traced it end-to-end. In DucklakePageSink.appendUnpartitioned (194-197) and appendPartitioned (237-241), a rollover closes the current writer then *immediately* opens a successor via openNewWriter. openParquetWriter/openDuckDbWriter eagerly create the output stream and register the path in writtenFilePaths (lines 311-314 for parquet, 336-337 for duckdb). If the rollover crosses targetMaxFileSize on the final page (or the final page for a given partition), no further write lands on the successor. finish() (249-253) then unconditionally closeWriter()s every non-null slot, and closeWriter (384-393) calls finishAndBuildFragment(). ParquetFileWriter.finishAndBuildFragment (82-113) calls parquetWriter.close() and emits a DucklakeWriteFragment with recordCount = fileMetaData.getNum_rows() = 0 plus a real header+footer file. DuckDbFileWriter.finishAndBuildFragment (371-415) even uploads the empty .db file to remote storage with rowCount=0.

No layer filters the zero-row fragment: DucklakeMetadata.finishInsert (1026-1029) only checks writeFragments.isEmpty() on the list; JdbcDucklakeCatalog.commitInsert (1887-1891) only checks the list; applyInsertFragments (1957-1996) writes a ducklake_data_file row for *every* fragment, including recordCount=0. So an empty object is persisted to storage AND a zero-row catalog row is registered. The claimed consequence is genuine.

I downgrade severity from medium to low. It is not a correctness defect — query results are unaffected (a zero-row file contributes nothing) and conservative stats suppression isn't triggered by it. Impact is bounded: only the *final* successor writer per page-sink per partition can stay empty (intermediate successors always receive the next page), so the finding's "for every rollover boundary" slightly overstates it — at most one empty file per sink instance per partition. It is real metadata bloat / a wasted remote object and minor file-listing noise, but not a medium-severity issue. The suggested lazy-open fix (leave the slot null after closeWriter; both append paths already handle the null case) is sound and cheap.

confirmed.title: getCompletedBytes() not overridden — reported written bytes always 0
confirmed.detail: getCompletedBytes() is left at the default 0 even though every writer tracks getApproximateWrittenBytes() and finished fragments carry fileSize. Operator stats / EXPLAIN ANALYZE write-byte metrics for this connector will always read zero, which is misleading for monitoring large writes. Lower severity than memory accounting since it is purely observational.
confirmed.suggestion: Override getCompletedBytes() to sum completed-fragment fileSize plus each live writer's getApproximateWrittenBytes().
confirmed.verifierReasoning: All factual premises of the finding hold. DucklakePageSink (lines 58-394) does not override getCompletedBytes(); the SPI default in ConnectorPageSink.java:30-33 returns 0. The data needed to report real values exists: DucklakeFileWriter.getApproximateWrittenBytes() (DucklakeFileWriter.java:39) gives live-writer bytes, and DucklakeWriteFragment carries fileSizeBytes (DucklakeWriteFragment.java:30), which the sink accumulates in completedFragments (DucklakePageSink.java:83, 391). The peer Hive connector overrides it (HivePageSink.java:165-169 returns writtenBytes), confirming the override is the expected pattern for write-byte operator stats / EXPLAIN ANALYZE. Consequence is real but strictly observational: write-byte metrics read zero for this connector. The SPI Javadoc explicitly permits returning 0 ('If size is not available, this method should return zero'), so this is not a correctness bug and not data-affecting — hence low severity. It is not listed among the briefing's intentional decisions or known gaps, so it stands as a legitimate low-severity nitpick. The suggested fix (sum completed-fragment fileSize plus each live writer's getApproximateWrittenBytes()) is directly implementable from the available fields, though approximate bytes and on-disk fileSize use slightly different accounting (logical vs on-disk), which is acceptable for an observational metric.

confirmed.title: Partition values recomputed twice on rollover
confirmed.detail: In appendPartitioned, when a writer rotates, getPartitionValues(page, posArray[0]) is invoked a second time at line 239 to seed the replacement writer, having already been computed at line 227 (or being trivially re-derivable). For an IDENTITY/temporal partition this re-walks the partition computer over the source block for that row. The values for a given writerIndex are invariant within the page (that is the whole point of grouping by writer index), so the result is identical to the value used to open the original writer.
confirmed.suggestion: Hoist the partitionValues for this writerIndex into a local computed once per entry and reuse it both for the initial open and the post-rollover open.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The redundancy is real. In DucklakePageSink.appendPartitioned, line 227 computes partitionValues = partitioner.getPartitionValues(page, posArray[0]) when first opening a writer, and line 239 recomputes partitioner.getPartitionValues(page, posArray[0]) after a size-triggered rollover. All positions in posArray share the same writerIndex (positionsByWriter groups by writerIndexes[position], lines 212-215), and PageIndexer guarantees equal partition values for equal writer index, so the line-239 map is identical to the one the writer was opened with. When a writer is opened at 227 and immediately rolls over at 237 within the same entry iteration, getPartitionValues runs twice on the same row for the same result.

However the efficiency impact is negligible, so severity stays at the bottom of low: (1) getPartitionValues (DucklakePagePartitioner.java:104-119) and computePartitionValue (DucklakePartitionComputer.java:75-99) operate on a SINGLE position, not the whole block — cost is O(numPartitionFields) single-row decodes, not a block walk as the finding's wording implies. (2) Line 239 only executes on rollover, i.e. once per targetMaxFileSize (hundreds of MB) written — vanishingly rare relative to per-page work. (3) The suggested hoist only removes the duplicate in the rare same-iteration open-then-rollover case; for a writer opened in a prior page there is no local to reuse, so a recompute (or caching the values on the writer) is still needed. Genuine but trivial cleanliness nit; correctly classified as low.

confirmed.title: Per-page boxing into HashMap<Integer,List<Integer>> then re-unboxing to int[]
confirmed.detail: appendPartitioned builds a HashMap<Integer,List<Integer>> of boxed Integer positions for every page, appending one boxed Integer per row, then immediately re-materializes each list to an int[] via a stream (positions.stream().mapToInt(...)). For wide partitioned writes this is per-row autoboxing plus a second full pass and intermediate stream allocation on the hot append path. The grouping could be done directly into int[] buckets (e.g. count per index, then fill), avoiding boxing entirely.
confirmed.suggestion: Pre-size int[] buckets per writer index from a single counting pass over writerIndexes (max index is known via getMaxIndex), then fill in a second pass — no Integer boxing, no per-entry stream.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The code at DucklakePageSink.java:212-221 matches the finding exactly. appendPartitioned (called once per page via appendPage→line 174) builds Map<Integer,List<Integer>> positionsByWriter, and for every position (page.getPositionCount(), typically up to ~1024-8192 rows) does computeIfAbsent(...).add(position) — autoboxing each int position into an Integer and appending to an ArrayList (line 213-215). Then for each writer-index entry it re-materializes via positions.stream().mapToInt(Integer::intValue).toArray() (line 221), a second full pass plus a Stream allocation. This runs on the hot write path for partitioned writes, once per page.

partitioner.partitionPage returns int[] writerIndexes (DucklakePagePartitioner:90-93, backed by Trino's PageIndexer), and getMaxIndex() is already available (line 204), so the suggested approach — one counting pass to size int[] buckets per writer index, then a fill pass — is directly implementable with no Integer boxing and no per-entry stream. This is the same idiom Trino's own Hive/Iceberg page sinks use for exactly this grouping.

The consequence is real (per-row boxing + intermediate ArrayList + Stream per page), not guarded away by any framework contract, and is not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing. It is, however, a micro-optimization on bounded per-page row counts — a genuine but minor efficiency nit, so "low" severity is correct.

confirmed.title: NPE on null SQLException message masks the real TimeZone-rejection error
confirmed.detail: applySessionTimeZone logs the tz-rejection via e.getMessage().lines().findFirst().orElse(e.getMessage()). If e.getMessage() returns null (legal for SQLException, and some driver-wrapped causes surface a null message), the .lines() call throws NullPointerException. Because applySessionTimeZone runs inside the try block of execute() (line 91), that NPE is caught by the catch (SQLException | RuntimeException) on line 101, the half-built resources are closed, and the NPE is rethrown in place of the original SQLException. The 'best-effort, proceed on tz failure' contract documented in the Javadoc is silently broken: a tz failure that should have been a one-shot WARN + continue instead aborts the whole split with a confusing NullPointerException. Low likelihood for DuckDB SQLExceptions (message is normally non-null) but the failure mode is exactly the path this method exists to tolerate.
confirmed.suggestion: Guard the message before calling .lines(), e.g. String msg = e.getMessage(); ... msg == null ? e.toString() : msg.lines().findFirst().orElse(msg); so a null message degrades to a usable string instead of throwing, preserving the documented continue-on-tz-failure behaviour.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The code at InProcessDuckDbExecutor.java:194-200 calls e.getMessage().lines().findFirst().orElse(e.getMessage()) inside catch (SQLException e). SQLException.getMessage() is contractually nullable, so a null message would NPE on .lines(). That NPE is thrown from within the SQLException catch block (lines 190-201 catch only SQLException, not RuntimeException), so it propagates out of applySessionTimeZone, up through the try-with-resources at lines 88-92, and is caught by catch (SQLException | RuntimeException e) at line 101, which closes the connection and rethrows (line 109). This does break the documented best-effort/one-shot-WARN-and-continue contract in the Javadoc (lines 168-178): a tz rejection that should degrade gracefully would instead abort the whole execute() with a confusing NPE. The mechanism is real and independently reproduced. However, reachability is very low: a SET TimeZone failure surfaces from the native DuckDB JDBC layer (DuckDBNative.duckdb_jdbc_execute / execute_pending) or from driver-level throws that are either explicit non-null strings or new SQLException(cause) (message = cause.toString(), non-null) — see DuckDBPreparedStatement.java:97,114,188,317,333. None of these produce a null message for a SET TimeZone rejection in practice. So this is a legitimate defensive-coding nit on a near-theoretical path, not a practically-triggered bug. Severity low is correct; the suggested guard (msg == null ? e.toString() : msg.lines()...) is a reasonable, cheap hardening of exactly the failure path this method exists to tolerate.

confirmed.title: Per-split DETACH on a fresh in-memory connection is pure overhead immediately before connection close
confirmed.detail: close() opens a throwaway Statement (connection.createStatement()) and runs 'DETACH ducklake_in' on line 275-277, then closes the connection on line 284. The connection is a fresh per-split jdbc:duckdb: in-memory instance (line 81) that is never reused — closing it tears down the attached database anyway. The interface Javadoc (DucklakeDuckDbExecutor.ExecutionContext) explicitly notes that DETACH-on-close is meaningful only for engines whose server-side ATTACH state persists across queries; the in-process per-split path is not such an engine. The extra createStatement + DETACH round trip runs once per split for no behavioural benefit, and its failure is folded into 'suppressed' and logged as an error, occasionally producing spurious close-time WARN noise.
confirmed.suggestion: Drop the DETACH block for the in-process executor and rely on connection.close() to release the attached DB; if a DETACH is kept for symmetry it should not be logged as an error when it fails on an already-tearing-down connection.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding reproduces independently. The connection is a fresh per-call in-memory instance: InProcessDuckDbExecutor.execute() creates `jdbc:duckdb:` at line 81 on every invocation, and DuckDbFilePageSource calls executor.execute(request) per split (line 119) and executionContext.close() per split (line 169). There is no pooling or connection reuse. On close (lines 274-291) the code opens a throwaway Statement to run `DETACH ducklake_in` (line 276), then immediately calls connection.close() (line 284). Closing an in-memory DuckDB connection tears down the entire database, including any attached DBs, so the prior DETACH has no behavioural effect — it is purely an extra createStatement + round trip per split.

The interface contract supports the finding: DucklakeDuckDbExecutor.ExecutionContext Javadoc (lines 128-134) states DETACH-on-close is meaningful for "engines whose server-side state persists across queries," which the in-process per-split connection is explicitly not. Confirming evidence comes from the sibling QuackDuckDbExecutor (lines 388-396) — the executor most likely to hold persistent state — which deliberately does NOT issue a client-side DETACH on context close, commenting that explicit DETACH is driven by cache-row invalidation, not per-context close. So the in-process DETACH is inconsistent with both the documented contract and its sibling implementation.

A failed DETACH is folded into `suppressed` and logged as a WARN (line 303), so it can produce spurious close-time noise on an already-tearing-down connection, exactly as claimed. Nothing in the PROJECT BRIEFING marks this as intentional.

Severity is correctly low: a DETACH on an in-process (no-network) connection is cheap and splits are not created at pathological frequency, so the cost and noise are minor. It is a legitimate cleanup/efficiency nit, not a correctness issue.

confirmed.title: toLowerCase() uses default locale (Turkish-i hazard)
confirmed.detail: normalizedType = ducklakeType.trim().toLowerCase() uses the JVM default locale. Under a Turkish locale, 'INT32'.toLowerCase() yields 'ınt32' (dotless i) and 'TIME'/'TIMESTAMP' etc. mis-fold, so the switch on line 113 falls through to default and throws NOT_SUPPORTED for perfectly valid types. The sibling DucklakeAddFilesNameMapper deliberately uses Locale.ROOT for exactly this reason; this converter does not. Both the case-folding here and the prefix matching on lines 79/84/100 (which depend on the result) are affected.
confirmed.suggestion: Use ducklakeType.trim().toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT) (import java.util.Locale).
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Confirmed at line 77: `String normalizedType = ducklakeType.trim().toLowerCase();` uses the no-arg, default-locale form. The lowercased result feeds both the prefix checks (lines 79 `list<`, 84 `struct<`, 100 `map<`) and the exhaustive switch at line 113, whose cases include many tokens containing the letter i: int8/int16/int32/int64 (lines 118-121), uint8/16/32/64 (125-128), int128/uint128 (135-136), plus timestamp/time/bit families further down. Under a Turkish locale (tr-TR), `'I'.toLowerCase()` yields dotless 'ı', so an input like "INT32" folds to "ınt32" and fails to match case "int32", falling through to the default branch and throwing NOT_SUPPORTED for a valid type. The sibling DucklakeAddFilesNameMapper.java deliberately and consistently uses toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT) at lines 150,155,159,168,203,308 — confirming the author's own convention and that this converter is the inconsistent outlier. The fix (Locale.ROOT) is correct and matches the suggestion. Two caveats lower the practical severity: (1) DuckLake catalog type strings are conventionally stored lowercase (the code comments reference 'int32','timestamp' etc.), so uppercase 'I' input is uncommon on the normal read path; (2) it only manifests on a JVM running under a Turkish-family locale. So it is a real latent correctness bug, but low-probability in practice; medium is slightly overstated, low is more accurate.

confirmed.title: timestamp precision round-trips silently for unsupported precisions
confirmed.detail: toTrinoType maps timestamp_s/ms/(micros)/ns to precisions 0/3/6/9. toDucklakeType inverts only those four; the default branch on line 227 maps ANY other TimestampType precision (1,2,4,5,7,8) to 'timestamp' (micros) silently, losing precision on CREATE TABLE / add column with e.g. TIMESTAMP(2) or TIMESTAMP(7). A user-declared TIMESTAMP(7) column becomes TIMESTAMP(6) on read-back with no error or warning.
confirmed.suggestion: Either reject unsupported timestamp precisions with NOT_SUPPORTED, or round to the nearest representable precision with an explicit, documented rule rather than a silent fall-through to micros.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is accurate and the consequence is reachable. In DucklakeTypeConverter.java, toTrinoType only ever yields TimestampType precisions 0/3/6/9 (lines 146,148,149,150 map timestamp_s/ms/timestamp/timestamp_ns; there is no encoding for precision 1,2,4,5,7,8). The inverse toDucklakeType (lines 221-228) switches on timestampType.getPrecision() with cases 0/3/6/9 and a catch-all `default -> "timestamp"` (line 227), so any other Trino timestamp precision silently collapses to micros.

The write path is genuinely reachable with no upstream guard: DucklakeMetadata.createTable (line 905) and addColumn (line 960) feed the user-declared column type straight into toColumnSpec → toDucklakeType (line 921) with no precision validation or normalization. Trino's TimestampType supports precisions 0–12, so `CREATE TABLE t (c TIMESTAMP(7))` or `ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c TIMESTAMP(2)` reaches this code with a precision the converter cannot represent; it is stored as `timestamp` (micros) and reads back as TIMESTAMP(6) with no error or warning. Tests (TestDucklakeTypeConverter.java) only cover the four canonical precisions; no fixture exercises 1/2/4/5/7/8.

This is NOT in the PROJECT BRIEFING's intentional-decisions or known-gaps lists (those cover calendar partition encoding, decimal p>38 hard error, unsigned widening, 128-bit degradation, etc. — nothing about timestamp sub-precision rounding).

Severity correctly stays low: DuckLake/Parquet physically support only the second/milli/micro/nano tiers, so some mapping of in-between precisions is unavoidable, and the data round-trips at micros without corruption. The defect is purely the silent, undocumented rounding (loss of the declared precision metadata) rather than a wrong-data bug. The author's own pattern elsewhere (e.g. decimal p>38 throwing NOT_SUPPORTED) suggests an explicit reject-or-document rule would be the consistent fix, supporting the suggestion as written.

confirmed.title: TIME/TIME WITH TIME ZONE precision dropped on write
confirmed.detail: toTrinoType('time') yields TIME_MICROS (precision 6) and 'timetz' yields TIME_TZ_MICROS, but toDucklakeType maps every TimeType to 'time' and every TimeWithTimeZoneType to 'timetz' regardless of declared precision. A column declared TIME(9) or TIME(0) in CREATE TABLE is persisted as micros and reads back as TIME(6) with no error, an asymmetric round trip. Same class of silent precision loss as the timestamp case.
confirmed.suggestion: Validate that the TimeType/TimeWithTimeZoneType precision is 6 (the only precision DuckLake's time/timetz represents) and throw NOT_SUPPORTED otherwise, instead of silently coercing.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The asymmetry is real and reachable. In DucklakeTypeConverter.java the write path collapses precision: line 233-234 maps any `TimeType` to "time" and line 236-237 maps any `TimeWithTimeZoneType` to "timetz", with no precision inspection. The read path (lines 144-145) fixes these at TIME_MICROS / TIME_TZ_MICROS (precision 6). DuckLake/DuckDB time/timetz are micros-only, so a column created as TIME(0)..TIME(5) or TIME(7)..TIME(9) is persisted as "time" and reads back as TIME(6) with no error — an asymmetric round trip (declared precision silently changed; for >6 it is genuine precision loss).

The path is reachable: DucklakeMetadata.createTable -> toColumnSpec (DucklakeMetadata.java:921) -> typeConverter.toDucklakeType(trinoType) with the user-declared column type, so `CREATE TABLE t (c TIME(9))` flows straight through. No guard or framework contract intercepts the precision before this point.

Contrast with the TimestampType branch (lines 221-228), which the finding cites as analogous: that branch at least maps 0/3/6/9 to distinct DuckLake types and round-trips those correctly (only non-{0,3,6,9} precisions hit the silent `default -> "timestamp"` fallback). The TIME/TIMETZ branches have no such switch at all, so they are actually worse — every non-6 precision is silently coerced, including the common default TIME (Trino default precision 3).

Not listed as an INTENTIONAL DECISION or KNOWN GAP in the project briefing (briefing's temporal notes concern partition encoding and timezone behavior, not TIME write precision). The suggestion to reject precision != 6 with NOT_SUPPORTED (matching the converter's existing throw style at lines 182/261) is reasonable. Severity correctly low: lossless for declared-precision < 6 (widening), and TIME(7-9) data loss is an edge case, but it is a genuine silent-coercion correctness/consistency gap.

confirmed.title: DATE/TIMESTAMP literals mis-render for years <1000, BC, or >9999
confirmed.detail: DATE uses `LocalDate.ofEpochDay(days).toString()` and TIMESTAMP uses `DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSSSSS")`. LocalDate.toString() renders years >9999 with a leading '+' (e.g. `+10000-01-01`) and BC years with '-', neither of which DuckDB's DATE literal parser accepts; the pattern `yyyy` is year-of-era (unsigned) so a pre-0001 (negative epoch day far in the past) TIMESTAMP renders the wrong year with no era marker. A pushed predicate on such a value would either fail to parse (query error) or compare against the wrong instant. These are out-of-normal-range dates so impact is low, but the failure is silent mis-comparison rather than a clean skip.
confirmed.suggestion: Either clamp/skip out-of-range temporal values (return Optional.empty() so the predicate falls back to Trino-side eval), or use a sign-aware formatter (pattern `uuuu` for proleptic year, and explicitly handle the >9999 '+' case) and verify DuckDB accepts the produced literal.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
Independently reproduced all three rendering claims by compiling and running the exact code paths.

DATE path (line 243, `"DATE '" + LocalDate.ofEpochDay(days) + "'"`): LocalDate.toString() emits a leading sign for out-of-range years — `+10000-01-01` for years >9999 and `-0001-01-01` / `-0100-06-15` for proleptic/BC years. These signed forms are not accepted by DuckDB's DATE literal parser, producing a query error rather than a clean fallback.

TIMESTAMP path (lines 245-250, TIMESTAMP_MICROS = `DateTimeFormatter.ofPattern("yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSSSSS")`): `yyyy` is year-of-era and is the worst case. Confirmed output: proleptic year 0 renders `0001-01-01`, year -1 renders `0002-01-01`, year -44 (45 BC) renders `0045-03-15` — a syntactically valid literal carrying the WRONG year with no era marker. That is a silent mis-comparison against the wrong instant, not an error. Year 10000 renders `+10000-...` (formatter still emits a sign past 9999).

Reachability: values flow straight from a user WHERE-clause TupleDomain on DATE/TIMESTAMP columns through translateDomain -> translateValues/formatRange -> formatLiteral with no clamping anywhere upstream (grep found no normalization). The output is the authoritative WHERE clause the embedded DuckDB engine runs against the data (DuckDbSelectSqlBuilder lines 62-80), not a hint Trino re-evaluates, so a wrong/unparseable literal genuinely affects results. The same DATE pattern is duplicated at DuckDbExpressionTranslator.java:466, confirming a deliberate-but-flawed rendering style rather than a typo.

Not covered by any INTENTIONAL DECISION or KNOWN GAP in the briefing; if anything the 'lossless, curated, recorded edge semantics' pushdown convention argues these edge years should clamp/skip to Trino-side eval.

Severity correctly stated as low: real and silent (especially the BC year-of-era case), but only triggered by year-0/BC or >9999 temporal predicates, which are far outside normal data and rarely queried.

confirmed.title: Insert fragments are deserialized then re-serialized to identical bytes for no effect
confirmed.detail: Each insert fragment Slice is fed through writeFragmentCodec.fromJson(...) and immediately back through writeFragmentCodec.toJsonBytes(...). This is a JSON parse + object alloc + re-serialize that produces bytes equivalent to the original Slice — no transformation, no marker, no tag is actually added. The comment 'we wrap them with a type tag' is misleading: nothing is wrapped or tagged. finishMerge distinguishes delete vs write fragments purely by attempting deleteFragmentCodec.fromJson and checking the path prefix (DucklakeMetadata.java:1257-1268), so the round-trip serves no purpose. It is pure CPU/allocation overhead per inserted file and an outright incorrect comment.
confirmed.suggestion: Add the insert Slice unchanged: fragments.add(insertFragment); delete the parse/re-serialize and the misleading comment.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Independently reproduced. DucklakeMergeSink.java:158-159 does `writeFragmentCodec.toJsonBytes(writeFragmentCodec.fromJson(insertFragment.getBytes()))` where both calls use the same `JsonCodec<DucklakeWriteFragment>` (declared line 67). The input Slice already IS DucklakeWriteFragment JSON: insertSink is a DucklakePageSink (field line 73), and DucklakePageSink.finish() serializes each fragment via the identical codec (DucklakePageSink.java:260). So the round-trip deserializes then re-serializes the same object with the same codec — byte-equivalent output, no marker/tag/wrapper added. The comments at lines 155 and 157 ("Prefix insert fragments with a type marker", "we wrap them with a type tag") are factually wrong: nothing is prefixed, wrapped, or tagged. finishMerge (DucklakeMetadata.java:1253-1273) distinguishes fragment kinds with zero reliance on any marker: it tries deleteFragmentCodec.fromJson and checks `deleteFragment.path().startsWith("ducklake-delete-")` (lines 1257-1262), then falls back to fragmentCodec.fromJson (line 1268), which is the same DucklakeWriteFragment codec (field line 139). The suggested fix `fragments.add(insertFragment)` is behavior-preserving. This is not in the briefing's intentional-decisions or known-gaps lists. Severity correctly remains low: the overhead is one parse+alloc+reserialize per written insert file (O(files), not O(rows)), so the CPU cost is negligible; the real defect is the misleading comment plus pointless allocation.

confirmed.title: resolveDataFileId does an O(rows x files) linear scan over data-file ranges per deleted row
confirmed.detail: processDeletes calls resolveDataFileId once per deleted row position, and resolveDataFileId linearly scans the entire mergeHandle.dataFileRanges() list each call. For a DELETE touching a table with many data files and many deleted rows this is O(deletedRows * dataFiles). Since ranges are contiguous [rowIdStart, rowIdStart+recordCount) keyed by a monotonic global rowId, this can be a single sorted lookup. Not a correctness bug, but on wide deletes over large tables it is needless quadratic-ish work on the hot per-row path.
confirmed.suggestion: Hoist the ranges into a structure supporting O(log n) lookup (e.g. a TreeMap keyed by rowIdStart, floorEntry + containment check) built once in the constructor, instead of rescanning the list for every row.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The finding accurately describes the code. In DucklakeMergeSink.java, processDeletes (lines 112-116) iterates every position in the delete page and calls resolveDataFileId(rowId) once per deleted row (line 114). resolveDataFileId (lines 119-127) performs a full linear scan over mergeHandle.dataFileRanges() on every call (for-loop line 121, containsRowId check line 122). dataFileRanges is populated in DucklakeMetadata.java line 1231-1234 from catalog.getDataFiles(...) — one DataFileRange per data file active at the merge snapshot, so its size scales with the table's file count (potentially thousands for a large table). There is no batching cap on the number of ranges, so a DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE touching N rows over a table with M data files does genuine O(N*M) work on the hot per-row path. This is a real, non-correctness inefficiency. DataFileRange.containsRowId (DucklakeMergeTableHandle.java lines 54-57) confirms ranges are contiguous [rowIdStart, rowIdStart+recordCount), so the suggested TreeMap-by-rowIdStart + floorEntry + containment lookup (O(log M)) is sound — note the catalog query orders by FILE_ORDER (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java line 417), not ROW_ID_START, so the list itself isn't pre-sorted by rowId, but a TreeMap builds its own ordering so the fix still works. Severity correctly stays low: it is not a correctness issue, the magnitude is negligible for small tables, and merge is not the tightest scan path. Not listed among the briefing's intentional decisions or known gaps. The constructor-time hoisting suggestion is reasonable and cheap.

confirmed.title: getRetainedSizeInBytes double-counts every delete-file path string
confirmed.detail: deleteFilePaths is always List.copyOf(deleteFileFooterSizes.keySet()) at the only production construction site (DucklakeSplitManager.createMergedSplit lines 571/595), so the two collections hold the identical path String references. The retained-size sum counts each path twice: once in deleteFooterSizesRetained (line 208-210, estimatedSizeOf(entry.getKey())) and again in the deleteFilePaths.stream()...estimatedSizeOf(String) term (line 220). For a split with many delete files this systematically over-reports memory by the full size of all delete-path strings, skewing the engine's split-memory accounting. (Strings are shared references, so they are only retained once in reality.)
confirmed.suggestion: Count the delete-path strings exactly once — e.g. drop the per-key estimatedSizeOf from deleteFooterSizesRetained (count only SIZE_OF_LONG for the value plus the entry overhead) and keep the deleteFilePaths.stream() term, or vice-versa. Optionally also use SizeOf.estimatedSizeOf(Map,...)/estimatedSizeOf(Set,...) so the per-entry SIMPLE_ENTRY/array overhead is included consistently instead of hand-summing only payload bytes.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding reproduces independently. At the only production construction site, DucklakeSplitManager.createMergedSplit line 571 builds `List<String> deleteFilePaths = List.copyOf(deleteFileFooterSizes.keySet())`, then passes BOTH deleteFilePaths (line 588) and deleteFileFooterSizes (line 595) to the canonical DucklakeSplit constructor. The constructor's List.copyOf/Map.copyOf (DucklakeSplit.java lines 86/90) copy the collection containers but the contained path String objects remain shared references between the list and the map's key set.

In getRetainedSizeInBytes, the delete-path strings are summed twice: line 209 `estimatedSizeOf(entry.getKey())` (each map key = a delete-path string) feeds deleteFooterSizesRetained (line 224), and line 220 `deleteFilePaths.stream().mapToLong(SizeOf::estimatedSizeOf).sum()` sums the identical strings again. So each delete-path string's payload bytes are counted twice while physically retained once. The bug is real and not listed in the briefing as intentional.

Impact is small and in the conservative (over-report) direction: getRetainedSizeInBytes feeds Trino's split-memory accounting, and over-reporting can only slightly reduce split concurrency, never cause incorrect results or OOM. Most splits have zero/one delete file; only splits merging many delete files over-report by a meaningful (but still small, vs INSTANCE_SIZE and fileStatisticsDomain) amount. Genuine but low-severity nitpick. The suggested fix (drop estimatedSizeOf(key) from the footer-sizes term, keeping only SIZE_OF_LONG, and rely on the deleteFilePaths term) is correct.

confirmed.title: read_snapshot_timestamp is parsed twice (validator + getter)
confirmed.detail: parseSnapshotTimestamp runs once as the stringProperty validator at SET time (line 91) and again in getReadSnapshotTimestamp at read time (line 143). The stored property value is already the raw String the validator accepted, so the getter re-runs Instant.parse on every call that resolves a snapshot. It also means getReadSnapshotTimestamp nominally re-throws INVALID_SESSION_PROPERTY at query-execution time rather than at SET time; in practice it cannot fail (the value was already validated and Instant.parse is deterministic), so this is purely redundant work and a slightly misplaced failure surface, not a correctness bug.
confirmed.suggestion: Since the value is validated at SET time, the getter can parse without the try/catch wrapper (or share the parsed result); the duplicate validation path adds no safety. Acceptable to leave as-is given the trivial cost, but the second parse is dead defensiveness.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The observation is factually accurate. The Trino session-property framework stores the raw accepted String: stringProperty(READ_SNAPSHOT_TIMESTAMP, ...) at lines 87-92 registers parseSnapshotTimestamp only as a SET-time validator (line 91), and getReadSnapshotTimestamp at lines 137-144 retrieves session.getProperty(..., String.class) (line 139) then calls parseSnapshotTimestamp(timestamp) again (line 143). So Instant.parse does run a second time on an already-validated string, and the getter nominally re-throws INVALID_SESSION_PROPERTY (lines 151-153) at resolve time rather than SET time — though as the finding itself concedes, it cannot actually fail since the same deterministic parse already succeeded at SET.

However the consequence is negligible. The single caller is DucklakeSnapshotResolver.resolveSnapshotId (DucklakeSnapshotResolver.java:79), invoked once per query during snapshot resolution at planning time — not per-row, per-split, or in any loop. The redundant work is exactly one Instant.parse per query that pins a snapshot timestamp. This is not a hot path. The finding is correct as stated but is a trivial style nitpick (dead defensiveness / minor misplaced failure surface), already self-downgraded by the reviewer to 'acceptable to leave as-is.' Low severity is appropriate, bordering on informational.
