confirmed.title: Non-atomic Files.copy into the live extension path can expose a partially-written binary to a racing reader
confirmed.detail: The comment at 80-85 argues the lock prevents 'racing copies into the same destination' from throwing IOException, but the lock only serializes writers within this JVM. The copy writes in place to the final filename (target = tempDir.resolve(RESOURCE_FILE)) rather than to a temp file followed by an atomic move. A reader outside RESOLVE_LOCK (e.g. another JVM/process sharing the same java.io.tmpdir, or the fixture path collision noted separately) can observe a truncated/half-written ~36MB binary and fail DuckDB LOAD with a confusing corruption error rather than a clean empty Optional.
confirmed.suggestion: Copy to target + '.tmp-' + random, then Files.move(tmp, target, ATOMIC_MOVE, REPLACE_EXISTING) so readers only ever see a complete file.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The code mechanism the finding describes is real. doResolve() (lines 158-166) and the fixture variant resolveBundledExtensionPathFor() (lines 121-126) both write directly to the final filename: target = tempDir.resolve(RESOURCE_FILE), then Files.copy(in, target, REPLACE_EXISTING). On the default FS provider this truncates/replaces the live path during the write window — there is no temp-file + atomic Files.move. The RESOLVE_LOCK (line 71) and CACHED AtomicReference (line 70) only serialize/dedupe within one JVM, exactly as the comment at lines 80-85 states; they provide no cross-process exclusion. So a second JVM sharing ${java.io.tmpdir}/trino-ducklake/<platform>/ could observe a partial ~36MB binary and fail DuckDB LOAD. The suggestion (copy to tmp + ATOMIC_MOVE/REPLACE_EXISTING) is the correct standard fix.

That said, the realistic blast radius is narrow, which is why low is the right severity (not higher): (1) Within a single JVM the result is cached and the only reader — TrinoFunctionAliases.loadInProcess via DuckDbExecutorFactory line 92 — runs the LOAD strictly after the copy returns, so the in-process path is fully safe. (2) The cross-process hazard requires two distinct connector JVMs on one host sharing the OS temp dir and racing on startup/reload, which is not the default production topology (one Trino server JVM per node). (3) The failure mode is a caught IOException / clean LOAD failure that self-heals on retry once the copy completes — it does not corrupt catalog or table data. Nothing in the briefing lists this as intentional, and no framework guarantee removes the write window, so it is a genuine (if minor) robustness defect rather than a non-issue.

confirmed.title: IOException is logged with only e.getMessage(), discarding stack trace and root cause
confirmed.detail: Both catch blocks (129-133 and 171-175) log e.getMessage() only. For IOException from Files.createDirectories / Files.copy the message is frequently null or terse (e.g. just a path), so an operator chasing why pushdown silently degraded to no-extension gets no cause chain, no nested exception, and no stack. This swallows the diagnostic detail of a failure that turns into a hard CONFIGURATION_INVALID at the call site in DucklakeDuckDbExecutorFactory.
confirmed.suggestion: Pass the exception object to the logger (log.warn(e, "...", platform.get())) so the cause/stack is preserved.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Both catch blocks log only e.getMessage(): line 130-131 (warn "...%s", platform, e.getMessage()) and line 172-173 (same). They use airlift Logger's warn(String format, Object... args) overload (Logger.java:227), which discards the Throwable. The exception-preserving overload warn(Throwable exception, String format, Object... args) exists at Logger.java:204, so the suggested fix is valid. The downstream consequence is real: on extraction failure doResolve() returns Optional.empty(), which resolveBundledExtensionPath() caches permanently (CACHED.set, line 99); resolveInProcessParityPath() (DucklakeDuckDbExecutorFactory.java:92-100) then throws a generic CONFIGURATION_INVALID "trino_parity extension required" that carries none of the original IOException's cause chain or stack. So the single WARN line is the only diagnostic for why extraction failed, and it drops cause/stack — for IOException from Files.createDirectories/Files.copy (permission denied, disk full, EOF on classpath stream) the message is often terse or null. Not listed as an intentional decision or known gap in the briefing. Genuine but minor: this is diagnostic-quality only on a rare temp-dir extraction failure path; no behavioral/correctness impact, so low severity is correct.

confirmed.title: host interpolated into single-quoted ATTACH string without escapeSqlString
confirmed.detail: token and dataPath are passed through escapeSqlString() before being interpolated into the single-quoted SQL literals, but host is concatenated raw into attachUri ('ducklake:quack:' + host + ':' + port) which is then placed inside the single-quoted ATTACH '...' literal. Unlike metadataCatalog (validated to [A-Za-z0-9_]+) and token/dataPath (escaped), host gets neither treatment. In practice URI.getHost() rejects a quote-bearing server authority by returning null (caught at line 98), so direct quote injection is blocked today, but the asymmetry is a latent hazard: any future change to how host is obtained (e.g. falling back to getAuthority() or accepting registry-based authorities) would silently open an injection/quoting bug, and there is no defense-in-depth here.
confirmed.suggestion: Run host through escapeSqlString() when building attachUri, consistent with token and dataPath, so the single-quoted literal is always safe regardless of how host was derived.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is factually accurate against the code. In connectionInitSql() (QuackBackedDuckDbCatalogUrl.java): line 141 builds attachUri = "ducklake:quack:" + host + ":" + port with host concatenated raw; line 153 places attachUri inside a single-quoted ATTACH literal with NO escapeSqlString call. By contrast token (line 152) and dataPath (line 154) ARE passed through escapeSqlString (defined line 199-202 as a '->'' replace), and metadataCatalog is validated to [A-Za-z0-9_]+ via validateIdentifier (lines 106, 180-197). So host is the lone literal-embedded value that is neither escaped nor validated — the asymmetry is exactly as described.

The claimed live consequence is correctly characterized by the finding itself as NOT currently exploitable: host always originates from uri.getHost() (line 96), the only construction path being parse() (sole constructor caller at line 115; only external caller is JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:141). A single quote is not a legal server-based-authority/host character, so URI.getHost() returns null for a quote-bearing authority and line 98 throws ("Quack URL missing host"). Thus no quote can reach attachUri today. The finding acknowledges this and frames the issue strictly as a latent defense-in-depth / consistency gap that would silently become a real injection bug if host derivation ever changed (e.g. fall back to getAuthority() or registry-based authorities).

This is a legitimate low-severity consistency/hardening observation, not a reproducible bug. The Quack executor is also flagged experimental/not-production in the briefing, which further caps practical impact but does not contradict the finding. Confirmed at low severity; the suggested one-line fix (escapeSqlString(host) when building attachUri) is sound and consistent with the surrounding code.

confirmed.title: validateIdentifier accepts numeric-only / keyword names that break the generated USE statement
confirmed.detail: validateIdentifier allows any [A-Za-z0-9_]+ string, including a purely numeric value (metadata_catalog=123) or a SQL reserved word. metadataCatalog is then interpolated UNQUOTED into 'USE <metadataCatalog>.main' (line 156). A leading-digit or keyword catalog name produces invalid SQL (e.g. USE 123.main), so connectionInitSql() returns a script that fails at HikariCP connection-init time with an opaque DuckDB parse error rather than being rejected up front by parse() with a clear message. The METADATA_CATALOG '...' slot at line 155 is single-quoted so it tolerates such names, creating an inconsistency where the catalog can be successfully named but never USEd.
confirmed.suggestion: Either reject identifiers beginning with a digit in validateIdentifier, or quote the identifier in the USE statement (e.g. USE "<name>".main) — quoting also future-proofs against keyword collisions. Since the value is already restricted to [A-Za-z0-9_], double-quoting is safe.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The code mechanics in the finding are accurate. validateIdentifier (lines 189-196) accepts any non-empty [A-Za-z0-9_]+ string, so metadata_catalog=123 or a reserved word passes. connectionInitSql() then interpolates that value TWO different ways: line 155 emits METADATA_CATALOG '<value>' as a single-quoted ATTACH string literal (which tolerates "123" or keywords), while line 156 emits USE <value>.main with the value UNQUOTED as an identifier. A bare leading-digit token (USE 123.main) or a reserved word is not a valid unquoted DuckDB identifier, so the init script would fail at HikariCP connectionInitSql time (line 144 in JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java sets it via setConnectionInitSql(quack.connectionInitSql())) with an opaque DuckDB parse error rather than being rejected early by parse() with a clear message. The asymmetry the finding describes (nameable but never USE-able) is genuine. The suggested fixes (reject leading-digit, or double-quote the USE identifier — safe since the value is already restricted to [A-Za-z0-9_]) are both correct.

However, severity is correctly low and arguably could be lower: (1) the value comes from an operator-controlled connector config URL (metadata_catalog query param), not untrusted external input, so there is no injection/security angle — the [A-Za-z0-9_] restriction already blocks breakout (note line 185-188 comment frames the validator as a security guard, which it achieves); (2) this entire Quack backend is documented as experimental and gated behind -Dducklake.test.catalog-backend=DUCKDB_QUACK, explicitly not production; (3) the only consequence is a worse error message at connect time vs. parse time — no data-correctness or runtime-behavior impact for valid (default ducklake_meta or any letter-leading) names. So it is a real, minor diagnostics/robustness nit, not a functional bug for realistic usage.

confirmed.title: TRANSFORM_PATTERN is case-sensitive while BUCKET_PATTERN is case-insensitive — uppercase temporal transforms silently become identity columns
confirmed.detail: BUCKET_PATTERN (line 46) is compiled with Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE, so BUCKET(4, col) is accepted. TRANSFORM_PATTERN (line 44) has no such flag, so partitioned_by entries like YEAR(event_date), Day(d), or Month(m) do NOT match the transform branch. They fall through to the identity branch at line 201 and are treated as an identity partition on a column literally named 'YEAR(event_date)'. At CREATE TABLE time this becomes a partition on a non-existent column rather than a year transform on event_date — a silent, surprising correctness divergence between the two transform families. SQL identifiers/functions are conventionally case-insensitive, so users will reasonably write uppercase.
confirmed.suggestion: Add Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE to TRANSFORM_PATTERN so it matches the case-handling of BUCKET_PATTERN (group(1).toUpperCase at line 188 already normalizes for the enum lookup).
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The case-sensitivity asymmetry is real and verifiable in the code. DucklakeTableProperties.java line 44 compiles TRANSFORM_PATTERN = "(year|month|day|hour)\\((.+)\\)" with NO flag, while line 46 compiles BUCKET_PATTERN with Pattern.CASE_INSENSITIVE. In parsePartitionField (lines 186-198), matcher.matches() against the case-sensitive pattern fails for YEAR(event_date)/Day(d)/Month(m), and the entry falls through to the identity branch at line 201, constructing PartitionFieldSpec(trimmed, IDENTITY) with columnName equal to the literal string "YEAR(event_date)". The partitioned_by values are SQL string literals inside ARRAY[...], not unquoted identifiers, so Trino does NOT lowercase them — uppercase reaches the parser verbatim (all tests in TestDucklakePartitionedWrite.java lines 76/100/205 use lowercase only, so the uppercase path is untested). Line 188 already does group(1).toUpperCase(ENGLISH) before the enum lookup, confirming the author intended case-insensitive handling and simply omitted the flag on the pattern — so the suggested fix is correct.

However, the finding's stated consequence is overstated. It claims this "silently" becomes a partition on a non-existent column and a "silent correctness divergence." It is NOT silent: JdbcDucklakeCatalog.createTable (lines 1686-1689) resolves field.columnName() against topLevelColumnIds and throws "Partition column not found: YEAR(event_date)" when the column doesn't exist. So CREATE TABLE fails loudly (fail-closed) rather than silently mis-partitioning data. The actual user-visible impact is a confusing "column not found" error for a reasonable uppercase spelling, instead of either working or emitting a clear "Unknown partition transform" message. There is no data-corruption / wrong-partition path. Genuine bug worth fixing (trivial one-line fix), but severity is low rather than medium given the loud fail-closed behavior. (Caveat: not flagged in the PROJECT BRIEFING as intentional or a known gap.)

confirmed.title: Greedy .+ in TRANSFORM_PATTERN swallows nested parens and commas into the column name
confirmed.detail: TRANSFORM_PATTERN uses a greedy (.+) for the column-name group. Input like 'year(foo(bar))' matches with group(2)='foo(bar)', and 'day(a,b)' yields a column name 'a,b'. These malformed-but-matching entries are passed straight into PartitionFieldSpec as a column name with no further structural validation here; the failure only surfaces later (or as a bogus identity/transform column) rather than as a clear INVALID_TABLE_PROPERTY at parse time. Note BUCKET_PATTERN deliberately uses a reluctant (.+?), so the two patterns are inconsistent.
confirmed.suggestion: Tighten group(2) to disallow parentheses/commas (e.g. ([^(),]+)) so malformed transform expressions are rejected with a clear error rather than silently accepted.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The regex mechanism is real. TRANSFORM_PATTERN at DucklakeTableProperties.java:44 is `(year|month|day|hour)\((.+)\)`. Because Matcher.matches() anchors on the whole string and `.+` is greedy, `year(foo(bar))` yields group(2)="foo(bar)" and `day(a,b)` yields group(2)="a,b" (lines 186-189). No structural validation occurs in parsePartitionField (lines 186-197) nor in the PartitionFieldSpec record constructor (PartitionFieldSpec.java:30-44 only validates arity, never the column name). So a malformed column name is indeed passed downstream.

However, the finding's "silently accepted / surfaces as a bogus identity/transform column" framing overstates the consequence. Downstream in JdbcDucklakeCatalog.createTable (lines 1685-1689) the column name is resolved against topLevelColumnIds; "foo(bar)"/"a,b" match no real column, so it throws RuntimeException("Partition column not found: foo(bar)"). The "bogus column silently accepted" path is effectively unreachable since no real SQL identifier contains parens/commas. The actual defect is cosmetic: that RuntimeException is NOT translated by translateCatalogExceptions (DucklakeMetadata.java:1529-1537, which only maps TransactionConflictException), so the user gets a GENERIC_INTERNAL_ERROR-style failure instead of a clean INVALID_TABLE_PROPERTY at parse time.

The cited BUCKET_PATTERN inconsistency is largely illusory: with full-string matches() and a trailing `\s*\)` anchor, the reluctant `(.+?)` still expands to swallow nested parens to satisfy the overall match, so it would behave the same way on `bucket(4, foo(bar))`.

Net: a genuine but very minor code-quality nit about error message quality, not a correctness bug — malformed input is rejected, just with a less clean error. The suggested fix (`([^(),]+)`) is reasonable. Severity stays low.

confirmed.title: All read-path IOException/SQLException wrapped as NOT_SUPPORTED
confirmed.detail: Every IOException or SQLException thrown while attaching, opening the Arrow stream, or loading a batch is rethrown with StandardErrorCode.NOT_SUPPORTED. NOT_SUPPORTED is reserved for genuinely unsupported features/operations. A transient runtime failure here (S3/httpfs read error, dropped JDBC connection, DuckDB engine error, corrupt batch) will surface to the user and to Trino's retry/error-classification logic as 'feature not supported' rather than a query/IO failure. This both misleads operators and can defeat retry behavior keyed on error category.
confirmed.suggestion: Use a generic/IO error code (e.g. a DUCKLAKE_* GENERIC_INTERNAL_ERROR or a dedicated read-failure code) for actual read failures, and reserve NOT_SUPPORTED for the type/feature gaps the converter actually raises.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: Independently reproduced. At DuckDbFilePageSource.java:141-143 a single `catch (IOException | SQLException e)` wraps everything in `getNextSourcePage()`: `executor.execute(request)` (line 119 — ATTACH, httpfs/S3 read, Arrow stream open, declared `throws SQLException`) and `arrowReader.loadNextBatch()` (line 123 — batch read, throws IOException). All are rethrown as TrinoException(NOT_SUPPORTED). These are genuine transient/external failures (S3/httpfs errors, dropped JDBC connection, DuckDB engine error, corrupt batch), not unsupported-feature conditions. In Trino's StandardErrorCode (deps-reference io/trino/spi/StandardErrorCode.java:37) NOT_SUPPORTED is ErrorType USER_ERROR, whereas IO/read failures should be EXTERNAL/INTERNAL. The convention is confirmed by Trino's own Hive connector: RcFilePageSource catches the analogous loadNextBatch/readBlock IOException and raises HIVE_CURSOR_ERROR / HIVE_BAD_DATA / HIVE_CANNOT_OPEN_SPLIT, all declared EXTERNAL in HiveErrorCode.java. The misclassification both misleads operators and can affect error-type-keyed retry/classification. Notably the real type/feature gaps on this path are raised separately as TrinoException(NOT_SUPPORTED) inside DucklakeArrowToPageConverter.convert (lines 98/249/320/399), which is a RuntimeException not caught by this block — so the legitimate NOT_SUPPORTED uses are cleanly distinct, and this catch block only ever sees true IO/engine failures, reinforcing the finding. No briefing INTENTIONAL DECISION or KNOWN GAP covers read-path error classification; the EXPERIMENTAL duckdb-path note concerns API churn, not error codes. Severity downgraded to low: the duckdb-format read path is experimental, no custom DUCKLAKE error code enum exists yet (so this is a broader convention choice), and the retry-defeat consequence chiefly bites under fault-tolerant execution. The suggested fix (use GENERIC_INTERNAL_ERROR or a dedicated EXTERNAL read-failure code, reserving NOT_SUPPORTED for the converter's true type gaps) is correct.

confirmed.title: getCompletedBytes reports output Page size, not input bytes read
confirmed.detail: completedBytes accumulates page.getSizeInBytes() — the size of the materialized Trino Page (output of conversion) — but the SPI contract for getCompletedBytes is 'number of input bytes processed by this page source' (bytes read from the DuckDB file/stream). The reported value diverges from actual input bytes (e.g. dictionary/RLE-compressed columns inflate, count-only pages report 0), skewing scan-bytes metrics and any cost/scheduling logic that consumes them.
confirmed.suggestion: Report bytes actually read from the Arrow stream / underlying file if obtainable from the executor, or return 0 per the SPI guidance ('should return zero' when unavailable) rather than substituting output page size.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The SPI contract is explicit. ConnectorPageSource.java (deps-reference) lines 28-32 document getCompletedBytes as "the number of input bytes processed by this page source so far. If size is not available, this method should return zero." DuckDbFilePageSource.java line 138 instead does `completedBytes += page.getSizeInBytes()`, where `page` is the materialized Trino output Page (produced by converter.convert at line 135, or `new Page(rowCount)` at line 132 for the emptyProjection branch). The output Page in-memory size is not the bytes read from the DuckDB file/Arrow stream — these diverge by encoding (dictionary/RLE-compressed columns vs Trino block representation), and the divergence is extreme for the emptyProjection/count-only branch where a `new Page(rowCount)` carries no data blocks (getSizeInBytes ~0) yet real file bytes were read. I confirmed the executor cannot supply a true input-byte count: DucklakeDuckDbExecutor.ExecutionContext (line 136-143) exposes only arrowReader(), memoryUsage(), and close() — no bytesRead accessor — so the contract-correct fallback is to return 0. The value propagates verbatim through RowIdInjectingPageSource.getCompletedBytes (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:971-973) to Trino's operator/scan stats. Impact is limited to observability (scan-bytes in query stats / EXPLAIN ANALYZE); Trino split scheduling uses split-manager-provided sizes, not this value, so query correctness and scheduling are unaffected — hence low severity is appropriate. The briefing lists no intentional-decision or known-gap entry covering this metric. Finding is accurate as written.

confirmed.title: sortKeyIndex contiguity is never validated; a gap silently produces a false sort claim
confirmed.detail: toLocalProperties relies entirely on the caller having ordered the keys by sort_key_index (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.getSortKeys does ORDER BY SORT_KEY_INDEX), but the mapper never inspects DucklakeSortKey.sortKeyIndex. If the catalog ever yields a non-contiguous index set (e.g. indices 0 and 2 with 1 missing due to a buggy/partial writer or a half-applied sort-info update), the mapper treats the two surviving keys as adjacent leading prefix and emits [S(col0), S(col2)]. Unlike the dialect/unknown-column cases that correctly truncate to a safe prefix, a missing middle key is exactly the 'skipping an entry in the middle is a lie' scenario the class's own Javadoc warns about — and because SortingProperty.isOrderSensitive() is true, the planner will trust this to skip a sort operator, yielding wrong (mis-ordered) results rather than just a lost optimization. The class is otherwise rigorously conservative, so this is the one place its safe-prefix invariant depends on an unchecked external assumption.
confirmed.suggestion: Track the expected next index while iterating: start at the first key's sortKeyIndex (or assert it is the minimum) and break to the safe prefix whenever key.sortKeyIndex() != previous+1, the same way an unknown dialect or unresolved column breaks. This makes the safe-prefix guarantee self-contained instead of dependent on the caller's ORDER BY.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The finding accurately describes the code. In DucklakeSortPropertyMapper.toLocalProperties (lines 68-85) the loop reads only key.dialect(), key.expression(), key.direction(), and key.nullOrder(); it never inspects key.sortKeyIndex(). The ordering and contiguity invariant comes entirely from the caller JdbcDucklakeCatalog.getSortKeys (line 670: .orderBy(sortexpr.SORT_KEY_INDEX)), documented as a precondition in the mapper Javadoc (lines 54-55). DucklakeSortKey validates only sortKeyIndex >= 0 (lines 39-41), so a gap (e.g. indices 0,2 with 1 missing) would survive construction and be passed through. In that case the mapper would emit [S(col0), S(col2)] as if adjacent — exactly the 'skipping an entry in the middle is a lie' scenario the class Javadoc (lines 36-40) warns about. Because SortingProperty is order-sensitive, the planner could elide a real sort and return mis-ordered rows, unlike the genuinely-truncating dialect/unknown-column cases (lines 69-72, 79-83). The suggested fix (track expected next index, break on discontinuity) is correct and cheap, making the safe-prefix guarantee self-contained. Two factors keep this at low severity rather than higher: (1) the trigger requires a corrupt/partial catalog, and the project convention explicitly states 'The connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths' with intentionally minimal catalog-value validation; (2) conforming DuckLake writers populate sort_key_index contiguously from 0, so the gap is not expected in practice. It is a real but minor defensive-hardening nitpick rather than an active bug, consistent with the author's 'low' rating.

confirmed.title: Corrupt blob can escape as unchecked exception instead of IOException
confirmed.detail: bitmapCount is validated only against [0, Integer.MAX_VALUE], independently of vectorSize/blob length. A blob whose bitmap_count is larger than the bytes actually present (or a truncated bitmap body) makes buf.getInt() at line 120 throw BufferUnderflowException, or makes bitmap.deserialize(buf) throw IndexOutOfBoundsException/BufferUnderflowException. Those are unchecked and bypass the IOException catch at lines 133-135, so the method violates its declared 'throws IOException' contract and surfaces a raw RuntimeException to the page-source caller. The vectorSize==blob.length-4 check at line 102 does not bound bitmapCount, so this is reachable for any corrupt file. The convention says read paths assume a trusted catalog, which is why this is low rather than higher.
confirmed.suggestion: Cross-check bitmapCount against the remaining checksummed byte budget (each bitmap consumes at least 4 bytes for high_bits plus a minimal Roaring header), and wrap the per-bitmap getInt()/deserialize/position calls so that BufferUnderflowException/IndexOutOfBoundsException are caught and rethrown as IOException with offset context, matching the rest of the method's error contract.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is technically accurate. In decodeBlob (lines 88-159), bitmapCount is validated only against [0, Integer.MAX_VALUE] at lines 114-116. The vectorSize consistency check at lines 101-104 bounds the total byte budget (magic+bitmap_count+bitmaps+CRC == blob.length-4) but does NOT bound bitmapCount against the remaining bytes — a corrupt blob can declare a large bitmapCount with a consistent-but-short vectorSize. The per-bitmap loop (lines 119-140) then reaches:
- Line 120 buf.getInt(): on <4 bytes remaining throws BufferUnderflowException (unchecked, extends RuntimeException).
- Line 131 bitmap.deserialize(buf): only IOException is caught (lines 133-135). RoaringBitmap.deserialize on a truncated/garbage body can throw unchecked BufferUnderflowException / IllegalArgumentException (bad cookie) / IndexOutOfBoundsException / NegativeArraySizeException, which bypass the catch.
- Line 136 buf.position(rbStart + bitmap.serializedSizeInBytes()): an out-of-range index throws IllegalArgumentException (unchecked).
Any of these escapes the declared 'throws IOException' contract and surfaces a raw RuntimeException to the caller (DucklakePageSourceProvider.applyDeleteFile, line 684, which is in a 'throws IOException' path).

However the consequence is purely cosmetic: the method already turns every other corruption mode (size, magic, CRC, deserialize-declared-IOException) into a clean IOException, and this gap only changes the exception *type* on a corrupt/truncated file. Both IOException and RuntimeException fail the split the same way — no data corruption, no silent wrong results. The briefing's explicit convention ('the connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths... general catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal') directly covers this path; the delete-file path comes from the catalog. The finding itself acknowledges this and self-rates low, which is correct. Real but minor; confirmed at low severity.

confirmed.title: Downloaded file size is never verified before being moved into the cache and returned
confirmed.detail: downloadTo() copies the input stream to the partial file and the result is moved to local and returned, with no check that the number of bytes actually downloaded equals fileSize. The size guard only lives in isReady(), which runs on the NEXT materialize call for that key, not on the one that produced the file. So a truncated/short download (interrupted stream that ends without throwing, or stale catalog fileSize metadata) yields a corrupt .db that is handed straight to DuckDB ATTACH on the current call. ATTACH of a truncated DuckDB file fails opaquely or, worse, reads garbage. The atomic-write design is undermined because the integrity check is decoupled from the write that needs it.
confirmed.suggestion: After downloadTo, verify Files.size(partial) == fileSize before the move; on mismatch delete the partial and throw an IOException so the read fails loudly rather than attaching a corrupt file. transferTo returns the byte count, which can be compared directly.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The finding is accurate against the actual code. In DucklakeMaterializedFileCache.materialize() (lines 104-109): the partial is downloaded via downloadTo() (line 106), then Files.move'd to local (line 107) and returned (line 109) with no size verification on this producing call. downloadTo() (lines 132-140) calls in.transferTo(out) and discards the returned byte count entirely. The only integrity check, isReady() (lines 117-130), compares Files.size(local) != expectedSize, but it is consulted only at the top of materialize() (lines 94, 101) — i.e. on a SUBSEQUENT call for the same key, not after the download/move that produced the file. So on the call that produces the file, a short/torn download that hits EOF without throwing (transferTo reads to EOF and returns normally) yields a .db whose size != fileSize, which is moved into place and returned straight to the caller. The sole caller, DucklakePageSourceProvider.resolveDuckDbAttachTarget() (line 662-664), wraps it in DuckDbAttachTarget.LocalPath and hands it to DuckDB ATTACH — exactly as the finding describes. The fix is trivial and the integrity check already exists; it is simply applied at the wrong moment (next call instead of this call). The briefing's "Phase 1 — brutal and basic" docstring documents the atomic-write/no-eviction design but does NOT cover skipping size verification, and this is not listed among the intentional decisions or known gaps. I downgrade severity to low because the trigger is uncommon: the cache key embeds fileSize, so a complete download against correct metadata always matches; the gap only bites on a genuinely silent short read (premature EOF without exception) or stale catalog fileSize, and most filesystem/network input streams surface truncation as an exception. Real but narrow; trivial loud-fail fix (compare transferTo's count or Files.size(partial) before move).

confirmed.title: Prefetch ignores maxFooterReadSize cap, can eagerly read/allocate a huge tail a stale hint would never reach in the normal path
confirmed.detail: wrapIfHintUsable only rejects a hint when completeFooterSize > estimatedSize (must fit in the file) or > Integer.MAX_VALUE. It does not bound the prefetch by parquetReaderOptions.getMaxFooterReadSize() (default 15 MB; see ParquetReaderOptions.DEFAULT_MAX_FOOTER_READ_SIZE). A stale/corrupt ducklake_data_file.footer_size that is still smaller than a large (multi-GB) data file will pass both guards, and line 68 will do delegate.readTail((int) completeFooterSize), materializing e.g. a 1 GB Slice into memory. The path this wrapper replaces would instead read only ~48 KB (DEFAULT_FOOTER_READ_SIZE), discover the real metadataLength, and throw ParquetCorruptionException at MetadataReader line 115 once completeFooterSize exceeds maxFooterReadSize — without ever allocating the oversized buffer. So the wrapper turns a cheap, guarded rejection into a large unbounded read/allocation. The read paths feed split.footerSize() / deleteFileFooterSizes() (DucklakePageSourceProvider lines 419, 721), which come straight from the catalog.
confirmed.suggestion: Pass the configured max footer read size into wrapIfHintUsable and refuse to wrap (return delegate) when completeFooterSize > maxFooterReadSize.toBytes(), so the prefetch can never exceed the bound Trino's own MetadataReader enforces.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The mechanism is real and every checkable claim holds. In FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource.wrapIfHintUsable (lines 65-67) the only guards are completeFooterSize > estimatedSize (must fit in file) and > Integer.MAX_VALUE; there is no bound against parquetReaderOptions.getMaxFooterReadSize(). Line 68 then does delegate.readTail((int) completeFooterSize), materializing a Slice of exactly that size. By contrast, the path this wrapper replaces — Trino's MetadataReader.readFooter (deps-reference io/trino/parquet/reader/MetadataReader.java) — first reads only min(estimatedFileSize, footerReadSize) where footerReadSize defaults to 48 KB (line 99-100), computes the real completeFooterSize from the embedded metadataLength, and throws ParquetCorruptionException at lines 115-121 when completeFooterSize exceeds maxFooterReadSize (default 15 MB per ParquetReaderOptions.DEFAULT_MAX_FOOTER_READ_SIZE) BEFORE the larger re-read at line 124. So a stale/large footer_size hint that is still <= file size passes both wrapper guards and triggers an eager large allocation the normal path would have rejected after a cheap 48 KB read. The suggested fix is feasible: parquetReaderOptions is in scope at both call sites (DucklakePageSourceProvider lines 411/424 and 715/725), so getMaxFooterReadSize() can be threaded in. Severity downgraded to low: triggering requires a footer_size catalog value that is huge (hundreds of MB to GB) yet still <= file size, i.e. a corrupt catalog value. The project convention explicitly states read paths assume a trusted catalog with intentionally minimal catalog-value validation; under a trusted catalog footer_size is the true Thrift FileMetaData length (KB to low-MB) and the bound is never approached. The over-allocation is also bounded by file size, happens on a single short-lived wrapper, and the oversized footer would fail to parse immediately after — so it is a defensive-hardening gap, not a hot-path inefficiency. Real and worth a one-line fix, but medium overstates the practical impact.

confirmed.title: footerSizeHint + POST_SCRIPT_SIZE can overflow to a negative long that bypasses both guards
confirmed.detail: completeFooterSize = footerSizeHint + 8 is computed in a long with no upper check on footerSizeHint. For footerSizeHint > Long.MAX_VALUE - 8 the sum wraps negative; a negative completeFooterSize is not > estimatedSize and not > Integer.MAX_VALUE, so both guards at line 65 pass and line 68 calls delegate.readTail((int) completeFooterSize) with a negative-cast int. This is only reachable with a near-Long.MAX_VALUE catalog value (trusted-catalog assumption makes it unlikely), hence low severity, but it is an unchecked arithmetic edge in input-derived code.
confirmed.suggestion: Guard on the raw hint before adding the post-script (e.g. reject footerSizeHint > maxFooterReadSize, or footerSizeHint >= Integer.MAX_VALUE - POST_SCRIPT_SIZE) so the addition cannot overflow.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The arithmetic claim is independently reproducible. At FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource.java:62, completeFooterSize = footerSizeHint + POST_SCRIPT_SIZE (8) is computed in a long with no upper bound on footerSizeHint (only the footerSizeHint <= 0 check at line 59). For footerSizeHint in (Long.MAX_VALUE - 7 .. Long.MAX_VALUE], the sum overflows to a negative long. The line-65 guard `completeFooterSize > estimatedSize || completeFooterSize > Integer.MAX_VALUE` then both evaluate false for a negative value (getEstimatedSize() is a non-negative size per the ParquetDataSource contract; Integer.MAX_VALUE is positive). So both guards pass and line 68 calls delegate.readTail((int) completeFooterSize) with a negative-cast int. The ParquetDataSource.readTail signature (deps-reference/io/trino/parquet/ParquetDataSource.java:37) confirms it takes int, so the narrowing cast of a negative long yields a negative length.

However the consequence is overstated relative to the title's "bypasses both guards": the realistic outcome is the delegate throwing (NegativeArraySize / IllegalArgumentException / IOException) on a negative length, not silent data corruption. The triggering value is footerSizeHint within 8 of Long.MAX_VALUE; footerSize originates from the catalog (split.footerSize() / ducklake_delete_file.footer_size at DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:419 and :721), and it is the Thrift FileMetaData length of a real Parquet file — physically it cannot approach 9.2e18 bytes. The project briefing explicitly establishes "The connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths... general catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal," and this footer_size is precisely such a read-path catalog value. So the edge is real code-wise but only reachable via a corrupt/hostile catalog value the project deliberately does not defend against. The suggested one-line guard (reject footerSizeHint >= Integer.MAX_VALUE - POST_SCRIPT_SIZE) is cheap and correct, hence I confirm at low severity rather than refute, but confidence is medium because the bug is essentially unreachable under the documented trust model.

confirmed.title: Footer Thrift metadata is serialized a second time solely to measure its length
confirmed.detail: ParquetWriter.close() already fully serializes the footer (ParquetWriter.serializeFooter via Util.writeFileMetaData) and records its exact byte length in footerReadSize. This writer then re-runs the identical full Thrift serialization of the same FileMetaData object just to read footerOutput.size() and discard the bytes. For wide tables with many columns / row groups this duplicate serialization is non-trivial CPU per closed file (and every rotated file within a partition), entirely to obtain a number ParquetWriter already knows.
confirmed.suggestion: Expose the already-computed footer length from ParquetWriter (e.g. a getter over footerReadSize minus the 8-byte trailer) and use it directly, eliminating the second serialization. If an upstream getter is undesirable, this is acceptable as-is but worth a comment noting the redundancy is intentional.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The mechanical claim is accurate. ParquetWriter.close() -> writeFooter() (deps-reference ParquetWriter.java:362-376) calls serializeFooter() (line 366, which runs Util.writeFileMetaData at line 436) and records footerReadSize = footer.length() + SIZE_OF_INT + MAGIC.length() (line 375). ParquetFileWriter.finishAndBuildFragment() (ParquetFileWriter.java:92-96) then re-runs the identical Util.writeFileMetaData(fileMetaData, footerOutput) purely to read footerOutput.size(). The semantics line up with the suggestion: the connector wants the Thrift FileMetaData length EXCLUDING the 8-byte trailer (confirmed by DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java:370-372 and FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource.java:48 — "footer_size is the Thrift FileMetaData length only, excluding the 8-byte" trailer), and footerReadSize - 8 == footer.length() == footerOutput.size(). So the value ParquetWriter already computed is exactly what's being recomputed.

However the impact is negligible and the fix is out of scope: (1) footerReadSize is a private field with no getter, and serializeFooter is private static (verified by grep of ParquetWriter.java) — the only way to reuse the value is to patch upstream Trino's ParquetWriter, which the review scope explicitly excludes (deps/build/versions off-limits). (2) The redundant serialization runs once per file close (in finishAndBuildFragment), not per page or per row group; the footer is metadata only and tiny relative to the full-file encoding/compression that just completed. "Non-trivial CPU per closed file for wide tables" overstates it. The finding's own fallback ("acceptable as-is but worth a comment") is the realistic outcome. Real but cosmetic; severity stays at the floor.

confirmed.title: discreteValues branch ignores DiscreteValues.isInclusive(), inverting exclusion-set semantics
confirmed.detail: The valuesFunction callback iterates discreteValues.getValues() and returns true (keep file) only when some listed value hashes to the file's bucket, otherwise false (prune). A DiscreteValues can be an *exclusion* set (isInclusive()==false), produced for predicates like x != v / NOT IN (...), where getValues() are the values to EXCLUDE. For an exclusion set the correct conservative answer is 'never prune' (the file may hold any non-excluded value, hitting any bucket), but this code would prune every file whose bucket doesn't coincide with an excluded value's bucket — dropping valid rows. In practice this branch is currently unreachable for bucket pruning: the only types accepted by bucket(N) (TINYINT/SMALLINT/INTEGER/BIGINT/DATE/TIMESTAMP/VARCHAR/VARBINARY/UUID) are all orderable and route through SortedRangeSet, whose getValuesProcessor always calls rangesFunction, never valuesFunction (EquatableValueSet is the only set that hits valuesFunction). So this is a latent wrong-if-reached bug rather than an active one.
confirmed.suggestion: In the discreteValues callback, return true (don't prune) when !discreteValues.isInclusive(); only run the per-value hash-match loop for inclusive sets. Even though unreachable today, the silent data-loss failure mode warrants the guard.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding is accurate, including its own self-limiting "latent, not active" caveat.

Code (DucklakeBucketPartitionMatcher.java:73-80): the valuesFunction callback iterates discreteValues.getValues() and returns true on a hash match, false otherwise. It never consults isInclusive(). Per the SPI contract (DiscreteValues.java:20-23), when isInclusive()==false the getValues() are values to EXCLUDE (predicates like x != v / NOT IN). For an exclusion set the conservative answer must be "never prune," but this loop would prune any file whose bucket doesn't coincide with an excluded value's bucket — dropping valid rows. So the logic is genuinely wrong IF this branch is reached.

Reachability (the crux) is correctly analyzed as currently impossible: SortedRangeSet.getValuesProcessor().transform() always calls rangesFunction (SortedRangeSet.java:526-528), while only EquatableValueSet.getValuesProcessor().transform() calls valuesFunction (EquatableValueSet.java:224-226). EquatableValueSet's DiscreteValues.isInclusive() faithfully reflects its inclusive/exclusive flag (lines 199-202), so an exclusion set CAN reach valuesFunction — but only for types that are comparable-but-not-orderable. Every type accepted by DucklakePartitionComputer.computeBucket (TINYINT/SMALLINT/INTEGER/BIGINT/DATE/TIMESTAMP/TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE/VARCHAR/VARBINARY/UUID; computeBucket lines 240-294, REAL/DOUBLE/BOOLEAN rejected at 291-292) is orderable — I verified UUID specifically (UuidType.isOrderable()==true, lines 108-112), the least-obvious case. Orderable types yield SortedRangeSet domains, so the discreteValues branch is dead for bucket pruning. Caller path (DucklakeSplitManager.java:521-527) confirms the domain is on the bucket-source column type.

So this is a real wrong-if-reached defect with a silent data-loss failure mode, currently unreachable. Not listed in the briefing's intentional decisions or known gaps. The suggested one-line guard (return true when !isInclusive()) is correct and cheap. Severity low is right — arguably it could be even lower since it is dead today, but the future-footgun (adding a non-orderable comparable bucketable type) justifies the defensive guard at low.

confirmed.title: Boolean.parseBoolean silently returns false for '1' and '0', causing wrong partition pruning and wrong projected constants for externally-written catalogs
confirmed.detail: Boolean.parseBoolean(value) returns false for any string that is not 'true' (case-insensitive), including '1' and '0'. DuckDB's default VARCHAR cast for boolean produces 'true'/'false', and so does the JVM connector's DucklakePartitionComputer. However the C++ DuckLake extension (the spec authority) may store '1'/'0' for boolean partition values — the same ambiguity that caused JdbcDucklakeCatalog.parseBoolean to explicitly handle '1'/'0'. If such a catalog is attached, a data file whose boolean partition column is '1' (true) is parsed as false. On the pruning path this causes domain.includesNullableValue(false) to be evaluated against a domain that expects true, silently skipping or including wrong files. On the page-source projection path (buildMissingColumnBlock) the projected constant is false when the real value is true, corrupting query results. The catch block swallows the failure silently only for RuntimeExceptions; Boolean.parseBoolean never throws, so the silent wrong-value path is taken every time.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace Boolean.parseBoolean(value) with a helper that mirrors JdbcDucklakeCatalog.parseBoolean: accept 'true'/'false' (case-insensitive) and '1'/'0', and throw IllegalArgumentException for anything else (which the catch block in both callers already handles safely).
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The mechanism is real and independently verified. DucklakePartitionValueParser.java:73-74 uses Boolean.parseBoolean(value), which returns false for any string other than case-insensitive "true" and NEVER throws. Both callers wrap parseIdentity in catch(RuntimeException) (DucklakeSplitManager.java:530-532 returns true=don't-prune; DucklakePageSourceProvider.buildMissingColumnBlock at lines 1059-1064 falls back to NULL), so the catch blocks are defenses against parse exceptions — but Boolean.parseBoolean never throws on "1"/"0", so for those inputs the wrong value (false) is produced silently rather than caught. On the pruning path domain.includesNullableValue(false) is evaluated when the true value is true (wrong include/exclude); on the projection path the constant block is false when the real value is true (wrong results). The project's own JdbcDucklakeCatalog.parseBoolean (lines 2460-2469) deliberately accepts "true"/"false" AND "1"/"0" and throws IllegalArgumentException otherwise — direct internal evidence the authors consider "1"/"0" a valid catalog boolean encoding and that parseIdentity's helper is inconsistent. The suggested fix (mirror parseBoolean) is sound: it would route unexpected encodings into the existing catch blocks safely. BOOLEAN identity partitioning IS a supported live feature (DucklakePartitionComputer.computeIdentityValue lines 107-108 emit "true"/"false"); BOOLEAN is only rejected for the bucket transform (line 291), so this is not an out-of-scope type and is not listed among the briefing's intentional decisions or known gaps. Severity downgraded to low: JVM-written catalogs always emit "true"/"false" and round-trip correctly, so the bug only manifests on externally-populated catalogs (DuckDB C++ / add_files of hive directories like flag=1) that store "1"/"0". That scenario is plausible but not the primary path, and confidence is medium because I could not pin down the exact VARCHAR cast format the C++ extension/DuckDB COPY emits for boolean partition values (the map value originates from core-DuckDB COPY partition reporting outside the vendored extension tree).

confirmed.title: getRetainedSizeInBytes double-counts primitive fields already included in INSTANCE_SIZE
confirmed.detail: SizeOf.instanceSize() sums the sizes of all declared instance fields (including the 8-byte long columnId and 1-byte boolean nullable) plus the object header. Calling sizeOf(columnId) autoboxes the long to Long and returns LONG_INSTANCE_SIZE (~16 bytes heap for a boxed Long), and sizeOf(nullable) autoboxes boolean to Boolean and returns BOOLEAN_INSTANCE_SIZE (~16 bytes). Neither boxed object exists on the heap (the fields are primitive inline); the result is that the reported size is ~30 bytes higher than actual retained size every time this method is called. Trino uses these values for memory accounting — overreporting causes unnecessary memory pressure triggers and incorrect spill decisions.
confirmed.suggestion: Remove the sizeOf(columnId) and sizeOf(nullable) lines. The correct implementation is: return INSTANCE_SIZE + estimatedSizeOf(columnName); — INSTANCE_SIZE already captures the primitive fields.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The mechanics of the finding are correct, verified against the actual airlift SizeOf source.

DucklakeColumnHandle.java:30-34 is a record with primitive fields `long columnId` and `boolean nullable`. INSTANCE_SIZE (line 37) = instanceSize(DucklakeColumnHandle.class). SizeOf.instanceSize (deps-reference/io/airlift/slice/SizeOf.java:346-361) sums OBJECT_HEADER_SIZE plus every non-static declared field via sizeOfField; sizeOfField (363-379) returns 8 for `long` and 1 for `boolean`. So columnId and nullable are ALREADY counted inline inside INSTANCE_SIZE.

At lines 68-71, getRetainedSizeInBytes then adds:
- sizeOf(columnId): columnId is primitive long, so it autoboxes and binds to SizeOf.sizeOf(Long) (SizeOf.java:193-195) which returns LONG_INSTANCE_SIZE = instanceSize(Long.class) (~16 bytes) — the heap size of a boxed Long that never exists.
- sizeOf(nullable): autoboxes to SizeOf.sizeOf(Boolean) (168-170) returning BOOLEAN_INSTANCE_SIZE (~16 bytes) — a phantom boxed Boolean.
estimatedSizeOf(columnName) is correct (INSTANCE_SIZE only counted the reference, not the String payload).

Net: the method overreports by ~32 bytes per handle (the two wrapper instance sizes). The codebase's own correct idiom confirms this is a mistake: DucklakeSplit.getRetainedSizeInBytes (DucklakeSplit.java:209,212,215,217,221) accounts inline primitive longs as raw SIZE_OF_LONG (8 bytes), never as sizeOf(boxedLong). The suggested fix (drop both sizeOf() lines, keep INSTANCE_SIZE + estimatedSizeOf(columnName)) is correct.

These handles are aggregated per-column in fileStatisticsDomain for every split (DucklakeSplit.java:223), so the error is real and consumed by Trino split memory accounting. However the magnitude is ~32 bytes per column and the error is in the conservative (over-reporting) direction, so it cannot cause undercounting/OOM — only marginally pessimistic memory estimates. The finding's framing of 'unnecessary memory pressure / incorrect spill decisions' is technically possible but negligible in practice. Real bug, correct fix, but medium severity is overstated; downgraded to low.

confirmed.title: outputStream leaked when ParquetWriter.write() or close() throws
confirmed.detail: outputStream (line 177, from outputFile.create()) is not in a try-with-resources. If parquetWriter.write() (line 201) or parquetWriter.close() (line 202) throws an IOException, the method propagates the exception without closing the underlying OutputStream. The file handle leaks, and the partially-written file stays open on the remote/local store. ParquetWriter.close() is not idempotent across constructor failures — if the writer constructor threw after capturing the stream, the stream would also not be closed by the writer.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The outputStream from outputFile.create() (DucklakeMergeSink.java:177) is held in a bare local, not try-with-resources. The only stream-closing happens inside parquetWriter.close() at line 202. ParquetWriter wraps the stream in an OutputStreamSliceOutput (ParquetWriter.java:137) and its close() uses `try (outputStream)` (ParquetWriter.java:221).

The finding's three rationales hold up unevenly:

1. write() throws (line 201) -> CONFIRMED LEAK. If parquetWriter.write() throws IOException (ParquetWriter.write/writeChunk both `throws IOException`, lines 166/193), the exception propagates out of writeDeleteFile and parquetWriter.close() at line 202 is never reached, so the underlying OutputStream is never closed. Real stream/handle leak. The caller (finish(), lines 144-149) wraps this in UncheckedIOException and the merge aborts, but the stream is already orphaned.

2. close() throws (line 202) -> NOT A LEAK. ParquetWriter.close() executes `try (outputStream) { ... flush(); writeFooter(); }` (lines 221-228), so the underlying stream IS closed even if flush()/writeFooter()/writeBloomFilters() throw. The finding's claim that a throwing close() leaks the stream is incorrect.

3. Constructor-failure speculation -> IMPLAUSIBLE. The ParquetWriter constructor (lines 126-153) only wraps the stream and builds column writers; it does no I/O on the stream and nothing has been written, so a constructor throw is both unlikely and inconsequential here.

So the underlying defect is real but narrower than described: only the write()-path (and any throw between create() at 177 and close() at 202) leaks the stream. It occurs solely on an I/O error during a delete-file write — an already-failing, aborting path — not in normal operation, and causes no data corruption (orphan files are expected and swept by DuckLake maintenance per the abort() comment, lines 232-233). The fix (try-with-resources on both stream and writer) is correct and cheap. Given it triggers only on an error path with bounded impact, and two of the three stated rationales are overstated, I downgrade from high to low.

confirmed.title: resolveDataFileId does a linear scan per deleted row over all data file ranges
confirmed.detail: resolveDataFileId iterates mergeHandle.dataFileRanges() for every deleted row in processDeletes. For a MERGE or DELETE that touches N rows across a table with F files, this is O(N×F). On a large table with many data files and a large DELETE, this becomes a hot inner loop. The ranges are stored in List order and are non-overlapping, so the scan cannot terminate early when the matching file is in the middle.
confirmed.verifierReasoning: The complexity claim is accurate. DucklakeMergeSink.processDeletes (lines 112-116) loops over every position in the delete page and calls resolveDataFileId(rowId) once per deleted row. resolveDataFileId (lines 119-127) does a linear scan over mergeHandle.dataFileRanges() with no early exit. So for N deleted rows and F data files this is O(N×F). dataFileRanges is populated in DucklakeMetadata.java:1231-1234 from catalog.getDataFiles(), which returns one DataFileRange per active data file in the table at the snapshot (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.getDataFiles, line 369-417). F is therefore unbounded for a large/unmaintained table, and N can be millions on a large DELETE/MERGE, so the inner loop can reach billions of containsRowId comparisons. The proposed NavigableMap/TreeMap fix is correct: DataFileRange.containsRowId (DucklakeMergeTableHandle.java:54-57) checks [rowIdStart, rowIdStart+recordCount), the ranges are non-overlapping per row-id assignment, and floorEntry(rowId) plus a containsRowId guard resolves in O(log F). One inaccuracy in the finding's prose: the List is ordered by FILE_ORDER (line 417), not by rowIdStart, so it is not actually sorted by rowIdStart — early termination on the existing list would not be possible anyway, but this does not affect the TreeMap fix, which sorts by key independently. I downgrade severity from medium to low: each iteration is only two long comparisons, and the MERGE/DELETE is dominated by Parquet I/O, delete-file writes, and JDBC catalog round-trips, so this CPU loop is rarely the bottleneck except on pathologically large tables. It is a real, cheaply-fixable efficiency nit, not a correctness bug.

confirmed.title: Insert fragments are needlessly deserialized and reserialized inside finish()
confirmed.detail: DucklakePageSink.finish() already emits DucklakeWriteFragment as JSON bytes (Slices.wrappedBuffer(fragmentCodec.toJsonBytes(...))). DucklakeMergeSink.finish() then calls writeFragmentCodec.fromJson(insertFragment.getBytes()) to parse those bytes back into a DucklakeWriteFragment, then immediately calls writeFragmentCodec.toJsonBytes(...) to produce identical JSON bytes. The two operations cancel out — the output is the same bytes as the input — but they allocate objects and parse JSON for every insert fragment in the MERGE.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The round-trip is real and pointless. DucklakeMergeSink.finish() lines 158-159 call writeFragmentCodec.toJsonBytes(writeFragmentCodec.fromJson(insertFragment.getBytes())) on slices that insertSink already produced as DucklakeWriteFragment JSON. Verified: DucklakePageSink.finish() (DucklakePageSink.java:260) emits Slices.wrappedBuffer(fragmentCodec.toJsonBytes(fragment)) via a JsonCodec<DucklakeWriteFragment>, and DucklakeMergeSink.writeFragmentCodec is the same JsonCodec<DucklakeWriteFragment> type (DucklakeMergeSink.java:67, 82). Parsing then re-serializing with the same deterministic codec yields byte-identical output, so the two operations cancel out.

The comment at lines 155-157 ("Prefix insert fragments with a type marker", "we wrap them with a type tag") is factually false: the code adds no tag, prefix, or wrapper. The consumer, DucklakeMetadata.finishMerge() (lines 1253-1273), does NOT rely on any tag — it distinguishes delete vs insert fragments by trial-parsing (deleteFragmentCodec.fromJson + checking path().startsWith("ducklake-delete-") at line 1259, else falling back to fragmentCodec.fromJson at 1268). So the stated justification for the re-encode does not exist. The suggested replacement fragments.add(insertFragment) is correct and behavior-preserving.

Downgraded medium->low: this runs once per MERGE in finish(), iterating only over completed-file write fragments (one per output Parquet file), not per row. It is an O(files) cost on the commit path, not a hot loop, so the performance impact is negligible. The misleading comment is the more substantive defect than the wasted cycles.

confirmed.title: Delete-file footer_size=0 written unconditionally, crashing DuckDB reader
confirmed.detail: applyDeleteFragments sets r.setFooterSize(fragment.footerSize()) unconditionally (line 2244). When DucklakeMergeSink.writeDeleteFile() catches an IOException during footer serialization (lines 214-216), it sets footerSize=0 and returns it in the DucklakeDeleteFragment. That zero is then written verbatim to ducklake_delete_file.footer_size. The comment at line 1971-1973 (in the insert-fragment path) explicitly states that a literal 0 is wrong and crashes DuckDB's reader with 'Invalid footer length'. The insert-fragment path (line 1974) already guards this with `if (fragment.footerSize() > 0)`, mapping zero to SQL NULL so DuckDB falls back to a blind tail read. The delete-fragment path has no such guard, so any IOException during footer serialization silently stores 0 and will crash DuckDB whenever it subsequently reads that delete file.
confirmed.suggestion: Apply the same guard used in the insert path: `if (fragment.footerSize() > 0) { r.setFooterSize(fragment.footerSize()); }` instead of the unconditional set at line 2244.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The code asymmetry the finding describes is real and verified. JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2244 (`r.setFooterSize(fragment.footerSize())`) sets footer_size unconditionally for delete files, whereas the insert path at lines 1974-1976 guards with `if (fragment.footerSize() > 0)` and the inline comment at 1970-1973 explicitly states "A literal 0 is wrong and crashes DuckDB's reader (\"Invalid footer length\")" and maps 0 to SQL NULL. DucklakeMergeSink.writeDeleteFile() (trino-ducklake/.../DucklakeMergeSink.java:208-216, 220-225) does set footerSize=0 on IOException and returns it in the DucklakeDeleteFragment, and the catalog read-back at JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:388/434 treats delete-file footer_size as Optional.ofNullable, so 0 and NULL are genuinely distinct values that get persisted. So the delete path lacks the guard the insert path has — a legitimate defensive-symmetry/correctness inconsistency.

However, the claimed HIGH-severity consequence is substantially overstated. The footerSize=0 value originates ONLY from the catch block at DucklakeMergeSink.java:214-216, which wraps `org.apache.parquet.format.Util.writeFileMetaData(fileMetaData, footerOutput)` where footerOutput is an in-memory io.airlift.slice.DynamicSliceOutput (a growable in-memory buffer whose write* methods perform no real I/O). An IOException from Thrift serialization into an in-memory sink is effectively unreachable in practice — this is a defensive catch, and the identical pattern is used for data files in ParquetFileWriter.java:91-98. So in normal operation footerSize is always > 0 and the delete path stores a correct value. Additionally, this connector's own read path is safe regardless: DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:721 routes the delete footer hint through FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource.wrapIfHintUsable, which guards `if (footerSizeHint <= 0) return delegate;` (line 59) and falls back to a blind tail read. The crash claim depends entirely on a cross-engine DuckDB read of a delete file whose footer_size was set to 0 by the unreachable catch — a compound condition that won't occur in practice.

Net: a real but latent inconsistency worth a one-line fix (apply the same `> 0` guard for symmetry/defense-in-depth), not a high-severity crash bug. Downgraded to low.

confirmed.title: NPE on nullable snapshot_time before requireNonNull can fire
confirmed.detail: r.getSnapshotTime() is declared @Nullable in DucklakeSnapshotRecord (the jOOQ generated code), and the jOOQ schema definition for SNAPSHOT_TIME has no nullable(false) constraint, so it can legitimately return null. The call r.getSnapshotTime().toInstant() on this line dereferences the result directly, producing a NullPointerException before the value ever reaches DucklakeSnapshot's requireNonNull(snapshotTime) check in the record constructor. Any snapshot row with a null snapshot_time (e.g., from a partially-written or legacy row) will crash the metadata read path rather than surfacing a clear error.
confirmed.suggestion: Replace `r.getSnapshotTime().toInstant()` with a null-safe form, e.g. `Objects.requireNonNull(r.getSnapshotTime(), "snapshot_time is null for snapshot_id=" + r.getSnapshotId()).toInstant()`, so the error message identifies which row is corrupt.
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The finding's mechanics are factually accurate. At JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2319, r.getSnapshotTime().toInstant() is dereferenced directly. getSnapshotTime() is annotated @Nullable in the jOOQ codegen (DucklakeSnapshotRecord.java:52-54), and the column definition DucklakeSnapshotTable.java:64 uses createField(...TIMESTAMPWITHTIMEZONE(6)...) with NO .nullable(false) — unlike SNAPSHOT_ID at line 59 which does have .nullable(false). The spec authority confirms this: the vendored DuckLake C++ extension (vendor/pg_ducklake/.../ducklake_metadata_manager.cpp:196) creates the table as "snapshot_time TIMESTAMPTZ" with no NOT NULL constraint. So a null is schema-legal, and the NPE at .toInstant() would indeed precede the requireNonNull(snapshotTime, ...) guard in DucklakeSnapshot's compact constructor (DucklakeSnapshot.java:32), which can therefore never produce its intended clear message. The author even handles the other three nullable Long columns in the same method (schemaVersion/nextCatalogId/nextFileId) via orZero(), making snapshotTime the lone unguarded dereference — an internal inconsistency.

However, the severity is overstated. (1) The project briefing explicitly establishes that "the connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths... general catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal" — a null snapshot_time is exactly this class of concern. (2) No writer ever emits a null snapshot_time: this connector always sets DSL.currentOffsetDateTime() (line 1211), and the C++/pg_ducklake writers populate it too; only a hand-corrupted or partially-written legacy row triggers it. (3) The practical consequence is purely a worse error message (a raw NPE vs. a row-identifying exception) on already-corrupt data — no wrong results on valid data, no data loss. This is a low-severity defensive-coding nit, not a high-severity correctness bug. The suggested fix is a reasonable cheap improvement, but its impact is diagnostic quality only.

confirmed.title: getFallbackRecordCount materialises entire inlined dataset into memory just to call .size()
confirmed.detail: readInlinedData is called once per DucklakeInlinedDataInfo, reading all rows of the inlined table into a List<List<Object>> solely to call .size() on it. For a table with N inlined-data entries this is N full in-memory loads. The upstream note (pg_ducklake #195) explicitly warns about OOM when reading large inlined heaps. A count query against ducklake_inlined_data_{tableId}_{schemaVersion} would return the same number at negligible cost.
confirmed.suggestion: Add a countInlinedRows(long tableId, long schemaVersion, long snapshotId) method to DucklakeCatalog/JdbcDucklakeCatalog that executes SELECT COUNT(*) filtered by begin/end snapshot rather than fetching all row data, and use it here instead of readInlinedData(...).size().
confirmed.verifierReasoning:
The mechanism is real. DucklakeMetadata.java:525-531 sums `catalog.readInlinedData(info.tableId(), info.schemaVersion(), table.snapshotId(), ImmutableList.of(tableColumns.getFirst())).size()` over each DucklakeInlinedDataInfo. JdbcDucklakeCatalog.readInlinedData (lines 864-913) executes a full `dsl.select(projected).from(inlined.table).where(activeAt(snapshotId)).orderBy(row_id).fetch()` and builds a `List<List<Object>>` of every active row (lines 890-905) — even though the caller only wants the count. It even pays an unnecessary ORDER BY row_id. A `COUNT(*)` with the same `activeAt` filter returns the identical number at negligible cost, and the cheap-probe pattern already exists in the same file (hasInlinedRows uses DSL.fetchExists, lines 786-799), so adding a countInlinedRows is a natural, low-risk fix. No existing count method exists.

Severity should be downgraded from medium to low, however. getFallbackRecordCount only runs on a cold/degenerate path: getTableStatistics first checks catalog.getTableStats (line 427) and only falls back when no table-level record_count exists, AND no data files exist at the snapshot (line 510), AND inlined infos are non-empty (line 515). For a normal DuckLake table ducklake_table_stats carries record_count, so this path is rare. The data read is a single column of inlined (pre-flush) rows, not the whole dataset. The pg_ducklake #195 OOM concern is genuinely adjacent — a large accumulated inlined heap would be fully materialized during planning here — but the briefing scopes that watch to flush_inlined_data / full inlined scans, and this is a bounded fallback rather than a hot path. Real inefficiency and a correct suggested fix, but lower impact than 'medium' implies.

uncertain.title: Dedup normalizes paths but the fragment stores/opens the un-normalized path
uncertain.detail: The processed set keys on `normalized` (backslashes -> '/'), so 'a\\b.parquet' and 'a/b.parquet' are treated as the same file for dedup. But buildFragment is passed the original `filePath`, which is what Location.of(filePath) opens and what is persisted as DucklakeWriteFragment.path()/ducklake_data_file.path. Result: whichever raw spelling appears first wins and is stored verbatim (including backslashes), so the catalog can end up with a path that the dedup logic considered equivalent to a different spelling, and Location.of may mis-parse a Windows-style backslash path. The two notions of identity are inconsistent.
uncertain.suggestion: Use one canonical form throughout: either store and open `normalized`, or dedup on the raw `filePath`. Don't normalize for dedup and then persist/open the raw value.
uncertain.verifierReasoning: The mechanical observation is accurate: at lines 215-216 the dedup set keys on `normalized = filePath.replace('\\','/')`, but buildFragment is passed the raw `filePath` (lines 219-221), which is what `Location.of(filePath)` opens (line 255) and what is persisted as DucklakeWriteFragment.path() (line 331). So dedup identity and storage/open identity genuinely differ. However, the finding's two stated consequences do not hold up: (1) "Location.of may mis-parse a Windows-style backslash path" is wrong — I read deps-reference/io/trino/filesystem/Location.java: Location.of requires a URI scheme (SCHEME_SPLITTER, lines 70-72, checkArgument size==2) and treats backslash as an ordinary path character, never as a separator. add_files inputs are object-store/HDFS URIs (s3://, file://), not C:\ paths, so a backslash is preserved verbatim with no mis-parse. (2) The "two spellings collapse, raw wins, corrupt path stored" scenario requires a caller to pass BOTH `a\b.parquet` and `a/b.parquet` in the same files array referring to the same object — but in an object store those are distinct keys (backslash is a literal key char), so the normalization is the questionable part, not the storage; and that input is pathological/never-occurring. Realistic dedup (same path listed twice identically) works correctly because identical strings normalize identically. Net: a real but cosmetic inconsistency with no demonstrable mis-parse or catalog-corruption path given how Location.of works and how the procedure is invoked. Verdict uncertain rather than confirmed because the inconsistency is real code-smell, but the asserted impact is effectively non-existent; severity stays at the floor (low).

uncertain.title: Decimal read assumes Arrow vector scale equals Trino DecimalType scale, with no check
uncertain.detail: writeDecimalColumn takes vector.getObject(i).unscaledValue() and writes it directly as the Trino DecimalType's unscaled value (longValueExact for short, Int128 for long). getObject() returns a BigDecimal scaled to the Arrow DecimalVector's own scale (DecimalVector.getObject -> getBigDecimalFromArrowBuf uses the vector's scale field). If the Arrow scale ever differs from decimalType.getScale() (e.g. a future/alternate Arrow producer, or DuckDB normalizing scale), the emitted unscaled value is silently off by a power of ten, and for a long-decimal a scale that pushes the unscaled value past 18 digits would make longValueExact() throw a raw ArithmeticException rather than a TrinoException. The current writer (DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter) builds the Arrow decimal at exactly decimalType.getScale(), so the controlled round-trip is correct, but nothing here enforces that invariant.
uncertain.suggestion: Either assert vector.getScale() == decimalType.getScale() (throwing NOT_SUPPORTED on mismatch) before using unscaledValue(), or rescale via value.setScale(decimalType.getScale()) before extracting the unscaled value so a scale mismatch fails loudly instead of producing silently-wrong numbers.
uncertain.verifierReasoning:
The mechanics in the finding are accurate. writeDecimalColumn (lines 255-271) dispatches on the projected Trino DecimalType (convertColumn line 222-223, columnTypes passed to constructor line 87-89) and uses vector.getObject(i).unscaledValue() with no scale reconciliation. DecimalVector.getObject -> getObjectNotNull -> DecimalUtility.getBigDecimalFromArrowBuf(valueBuffer, index, scale, ...) (deps-reference DecimalVector.java line 162) indeed applies the vector's own scale field (line 85), so the returned BigDecimal's unscaledValue() reflects the Arrow scale, not necessarily decimalType.getScale(). If the two scales diverged, the emitted unscaled value would be off by a power of ten, and longValueExact() would throw a raw ArithmeticException rather than a TrinoException for an overflowing short decimal.

However, the claimed consequence does not arise under the actual invocation path. The only in-tree Arrow producer, DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter, builds the Arrow decimal type at exactly decimalType.getScale() (writer line 370: new ArrowType.Decimal(getPrecision(), getScale(), 128)), and DuckDB's own Arrow export of a DECIMAL(p,s) column carries the declared scale s, which equals the catalog type's scale that produces decimalType here. So the scales are bound by construction, and the finding itself concedes the controlled round-trip is correct. convert() also already throws NOT_SUPPORTED on column-count mismatch (lines 96-101).

This is therefore a missing defensive assertion against a hypothetical future/alternate producer, not a defect that manifests today. The duckdb-format read path is also explicitly EXPERIMENTAL per the briefing. I mark uncertain rather than confirmed because there is no real trigger in the current code (the 'silently wrong numbers' framing is contingent on a divergence no in-tree producer creates), yet the suggestion (assert vector.getScale() == decimalType.getScale(), and use TrinoException for overflow) is a legitimate low-value robustness improvement. Severity stays low; not a confirmed live bug.

uncertain.title: DuckLake column_id (long) silently narrowed to int Parquet field_id
uncertain.detail: columnId is a long throughout the catalog (DucklakeColumn.columnId is long, allocated from a shared monotonic catalog counter), but it is written into the Parquet field_id via (int) columnId at lines 124 and 154 (and indirectly via childColumn.columnId() at 138/178). Parquet's field_id is int-typed, so any catalog column_id exceeding Integer.MAX_VALUE (~2.1e9) silently wraps to a negative/aliased int. On a long-lived, high-churn catalog the field_id written into Parquet would no longer match ducklake_column.column_id, and DuckLake's field-id-based column mapping (the whole reason this class exists) would read the wrong column or fail. The narrowing is silent — no exception, no warning.
uncertain.suggestion: Guard the cast: if (columnId > Integer.MAX_VALUE) throw a clear TrinoException/IllegalStateException naming the column, rather than emitting Math.toIntExact-style wrap. At minimum use Math.toIntExact(columnId) so an overflow fails loudly instead of corrupting the mapping.
uncertain.verifierReasoning:
The factual premise is correct but the claimed consequence is largely refuted, and what remains is a defensive-coding nit rather than a reachable bug.

PREMISE (correct): DucklakeColumn.columnId is a long (DucklakeColumn.java:27). column_id is allocated from a shared monotonic catalog counter via tx.allocateCatalogId() / nextCatalogId++ (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1712; DucklakeWriteTransaction.java:85-87), which is also consumed by schemas/tables/partitions/views/mappings (lines 1420,1593,1647,1676,1985). The casts at DucklakeParquetSchemaBuilder.java:124 and 154 are literally `(int) columnId`, and 138/178 pass childColumn.columnId() (a long) into the same int-narrowing path. So a value > Integer.MAX_VALUE would indeed silently wrap.

WHY THE CONSEQUENCE IS REFUTED/BOUNDED:
1. Parquet field_id is a 32-bit signed int at the format level, not a code choice. Confirmed in the reference reader: deps-reference io/trino/parquet/metadata/ParquetMetadata.java:295-296 reads `element.field_id` (i32) and re-applies via `typeBuilder.id(element.field_id)` (int). The Parquet Thrift SchemaElement defines field_id as optional i32. No engine (DuckDB C++, Rust, Trino) can persist a >2^31 field_id. So Math.toIntExact would convert silent truncation into a hard error, but the inability to represent such an id is a format constraint, not this class's defect.
2. The read path narrows symmetrically: DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:479 does `fieldIdToColumnIO.get((int) column.columnId())` and 466-469 stores Parquet ids via getId().intValue(). Both write and read apply the identical (int) truncation, so within-connector matching stays self-consistent. Actual mis-attribution would require two distinct column_ids that collide to the same int (differ by a multiple of 2^32), i.e. the catalog already past ~4 billion allocations.
3. Reachability: hitting Integer.MAX_VALUE requires ~2.1 billion shared catalog-id allocations on one catalog; collision-induced corruption requires ~4 billion. Astronomically improbable for real catalogs.

Net: a fail-loud Math.toIntExact would be marginally better hygiene, but the "reads the wrong column / silent corruption on a high-churn catalog" claim is bounded by the Parquet 32-bit field_id contract and the symmetric read-side narrowing. Not a confirmed reachable correctness bug; at most a very-low-severity hardening suggestion. Marking uncertain because I could not read the Parquet Types.id() source directly from deps-reference (not vendored), though the ParquetMetadata round-trip evidence strongly supports the int-bound conclusion.

secondary.title: Stale config description claims a SQL-replay fallback that no longer exists
secondary.detail: DucklakeDuckDbExecutorFactory's Javadoc (lines 36-45) and both resolve* methods (resolveInProcessParityPath lines 86-101, resolveQuackParityPath lines 110-121) treat the trino_parity extension as REQUIRED and throw CONFIGURATION_INVALID when no path is resolvable — and the executors back this up: InProcessDuckDbExecutor/QuackDuckDbExecutor take a non-null String parityExtensionPath (requireNonNull) and TrinoFunctionAliases.loadInProcess/loadServerSide only do LOAD '<path>'. There is no in-tree SQL-replay code path. But the @ConfigDescription for ducklake.duckdb.parity-extension-path still says 'If unset, falls back to the in-tree SQL replay (current default)'. An operator reading the config docs will believe omitting the path is supported and is surprised by a hard CONFIGURATION_INVALID at first use. The two sources of truth contradict each other.
secondary.suggestion: Update the @ConfigDescription on setDuckdbParityExtensionPath to match actual behaviour: the extension is required; if unset, DUCKDB_LOCAL falls back only to the bundled/extracted binary and QUACK fails. Remove the 'falls back to in-tree SQL replay (current default)' clause.

secondary.title: toDoubleRange silently drops ranges for all timestamp types
secondary.detail: toDoubleRange handles BIGINT/INTEGER/SMALLINT/TINYINT/DOUBLE/REAL/DATE but has no branch for TIMESTAMP or TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE, so column min/max ranges are never emitted for timestamp columns even when valid file-level stats exist. DoubleRange can represent epoch-micros/millis as a double, as Iceberg's connector does. This is a stats-quality gap (cost-based optimizer gets no range for time columns), not a correctness bug.
secondary.suggestion: If desired, add timestamp branches converting the stored min/max strings to epoch micros/millis as double; otherwise document that timestamp ranges are intentionally suppressed so it is not mistaken for an oversight.

secondary.title: Dead isShort() branch in TIMESTAMP(9) population
secondary.detail: Inside the precision==9 branch the code tests type.isShort() and, if true, reads a packed micros long. Trino TimestampType is short only for precision <= 6; precision 9 is always a long timestamp, so type.isShort() is always false here and the short branch (998-1000) is unreachable. It is harmless but misleading — it implies a TIMESTAMP(9) value could arrive as a short long-packed micros, which never happens.
secondary.suggestion: Drop the isShort() branch in the precision==9 case and unconditionally read LongTimestamp via getObject, or add an assertion documenting that precision 9 is always long.

secondary.title: Dead helper methods nanosFloor / microsToLocalDateTime
