secondary.detail: Both methods are private, @SuppressWarnings("unused"), and never referenced. nanosFloor is an identity no-op (returns its argument unchanged) and microsToLocalDateTime duplicates conversion logic that is not used anywhere in the timestamp population paths. They are leftover scaffolding that adds confusion about the conversion model actually in force.
secondary.suggestion: Delete both methods.

secondary.title: Pointless catch-and-rethrow of IOException in loadNextBatch
secondary.detail: populateRoot(page) is wrapped in try { ... } catch (IOException e) { throw e; }, which catches IOException only to immediately rethrow the identical exception with no added context, logging, or cleanup. It is pure noise. (Note also that populateRoot/populateVector primarily throw TrinoException, not IOException, so this catch rarely even fires.)
secondary.suggestion: Remove the try/catch and call populateRoot(page) directly.

secondary.title: Unused Logger field and stale comment about a removed one-shot WARN
secondary.detail: The static `log` Logger (line 63) is declared and imported but never referenced anywhere in the class (verified by grep). The header comment at lines 75-78 now claims lower/upper/reverse are 'native C++ scalar functions ... fully aligned with Trino's semantics', which both contradicts the project's documented 'known-divergent placeholders that emit a one-shot WARN' decision and leaves a dead logger that the comment implies should be doing the warning. Dead field plus a comment that disagrees with the documented behaviour is a maintenance trap.
secondary.suggestion: Either remove the unused Logger import/field, or reinstate the one-shot WARN the comment/documentation describes; reconcile the lower/upper/reverse comment with the actual divergence-vs-native status so the next reader isn't misled.

secondary.title: partitionValues declared Map<Integer,Object> only to store String, then re-copied
secondary.detail: partitionValues is typed Map<Integer, Object> but the only put (line 228) stores a String, and lines 239-243 allocate a second LinkedHashMap purely to cast every value back to String. The Object typing and the copy loop are dead indirection; they add an allocation per add_files call and an unchecked (String) cast that can never fail.
secondary.suggestion: Declare the field as Map<Integer, String> partitionValues and return new LinkedHashMap<>(partitionValues) (or ImmutableMap.copyOf) directly, deleting the cast loop.

secondary.title: Map values stored but never read (hivePartitionKeyLower, tableColumnsMatched)
secondary.detail: hivePartitionKeyLower is a Map<String,Long> whose values are always null (line 159) and are never read — only containsKey is used (lines 186, 208), so it should be a Set<String>. Likewise tableColumnsMatched stores the parquet index i (line 197) but only containsKey is ever consulted (line 204); the value is dead. These obscure intent and invite a future reader to assume the values mean something.
secondary.suggestion: Make hivePartitionKeyLower a HashSet<String> of lowercased keys, and tableColumnsMatched a Set<String> of matched lowercased names.

secondary.title: parseHivePartitions Javadoc claims URL-decoding that the code does not perform
secondary.detail: The method doc states 'URL-decoded values are returned as their raw text', but the implementation does a plain substring split on '=' with no URL decoding. A path segment like name=a%20b is stored as the literal 'a%20b'. If the connector's read path URL-decodes hive segments (Hive/Trino convention typically does), the added file's partition value will not round-trip with files written normally. At minimum the comment is misleading; verify against the read-side partition value handling whether decoding is actually required.
secondary.suggestion: Either implement URL decoding to match the read path, or correct the Javadoc to state that values are taken verbatim with no decoding and confirm the read side also treats them verbatim.

secondary.title: FileMetadata fetched for schema only; num_rows recomputed via thrift round-trip
secondary.detail: fileMetadata (line 283) is obtained from parquetMetadata but only getSchema() is used. The total row count is then recomputed by summing block.rowCount() inside toThriftFileMetaData (numRows) and re-read via aggregateRecordCount(thriftMetadata.getNum_rows()). The record count is already available directly from the Trino ParquetMetadata blocks; the extra round-trip through the thrift object and the trivial aggregateRecordCount wrapper add indirection with no benefit.
secondary.suggestion: Compute recordCount directly from parquetMetadata.getBlocks() (sum of rowCount) and drop the dependence on the thrift num_rows / the one-line aggregateRecordCount helper.

secondary.title: Misleading comment and redundant toLowerCase on hex alias derivation
secondary.detail: serverAttachAlias builds the alias from HexFormat.of().formatHex(digest), which already produces lowercase [0-9a-f] with no separators. The Javadoc (lines 236-238) claims the code is 'alphanumeric only because DuckDB unquoted identifiers reject the - characters base16 produces' — but formatHex never emits '-' (that is Base64/UUID behaviour, not base16), so the rationale is wrong. The trailing .toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT) on line 249 is also a no-op since formatHex output is already lowercase. Neither causes a runtime bug, but the comment will mislead a future reader into thinking the hex format needs sanitising.
secondary.suggestion: Drop the redundant .toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT) and correct the comment to state simply that a hex SHA-256 prefix yields a bounded, SQL-safe, collision-resistant unquoted identifier.

secondary.title: Duplicated/misplaced Javadoc block on the convenience overload
secondary.detail: Lines 57-61 carry a full Javadoc comment (with @return) immediately followed by a second Javadoc comment at 62-64 on the same method. The first block describes a return contract for a method that just delegates, and two stacked /** */ comments precede one method. The first comment is effectively orphaned and the @return tag does not apply to the convenience overload it sits above.
secondary.suggestion: Collapse into a single Javadoc on the convenience overload (or move the @return-bearing doc onto the primary computePartitionValue at line 75 where it belongs).

secondary.title: Stale TODO claims unsigned validation is missing
secondary.detail: Line 124 says 'TODO: Add validation to ensure values don't exceed signed range', but per the project briefing range checking is already implemented in DucklakeUnsignedRangeChecker at page-sink time. The comment is misleading: a reader will believe out-of-range unsigned writes silently wrap when they are actually rejected, and may add duplicate/conflicting validation.
secondary.suggestion: Remove the TODO and point the comment at DucklakeUnsignedRangeChecker which enforces the range on write.

secondary.title: Footer-size IOException silently swallowed and reduced to 0 with no logging
secondary.detail: If Util.writeFileMetaData throws, footerSize is silently set to 0 and execution continues with no warn/debug log. footer_size is recorded into ducklake_delete_file (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.applyDeleteFragments:2244) and is used on read only as an optional prefetch hint (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:719-721), so 0 is a benign 'no hint' fallback rather than a correctness defect — but swallowing the exception with zero diagnostics means a systematic footer-encoding failure would be invisible. (Same pattern exists in ParquetFileWriter.java:91-99, so this is a consistency/observability nit, not a novel bug.)
secondary.suggestion: Log the swallowed IOException at warn/debug before falling back to 0 so a recurring footer-encode failure is at least observable.

secondary.title: getChanges() leaks the internal mutable change list
secondary.detail: getChanges() returns the live ArrayList backing field rather than a copy or unmodifiable view. The caller (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.attemptWriteTransaction) reads it multiple times (runConflictMatrix, LogicalConflictCheck.run, the snapshot_changes insert) and could in principle mutate it; more importantly the same list instance survives across retry attempts of the same transaction since recordChange appends to it. Today the retry loop re-creates the DucklakeWriteTransaction per attempt so this is harmless, but the unguarded shared reference is a latent hazard if a future caller retains or mutates the returned list.
secondary.suggestion: Return java.util.List.copyOf(changes) or Collections.unmodifiableList(changes) so callers cannot mutate transaction state through the accessor.

secondary.title: Visibility-window predicate hand-duplicated as raw SQL instead of reusing SnapshotRange.activeAt
secondary.detail: resolveSchemaId, resolveTableId, and hasTablesInSchema each hand-write the snapshot-visibility predicate (? >= begin_snapshot AND (? < end_snapshot OR end_snapshot IS NULL)). This is currently semantically equivalent to the canonical SnapshotRange.activeAt used by every other read in JdbcDucklakeCatalog (begin <= N AND (end IS NULL OR end > N)), but it is a third independent copy of that boundary logic. If the canonical end-snapshot inclusivity convention is ever revisited, these three raw-SQL copies will silently diverge and resolve the wrong row at the begin/end boundary. The duplication also bypasses jOOQ's column/identifier handling that the rest of the class relies on.
secondary.suggestion: Either route these resolves through the same jOOQ + SnapshotRange.activeAt path used elsewhere, or add a comment cross-referencing SnapshotRange so the equivalence is maintained deliberately.

secondary.title: Non-exhaustive instanceof chain over a sealed interface silently ignores unhandled WriteChange variants
secondary.detail: check() dispatches on WriteChange subtypes via an if/else-if chain with no terminal else. WriteChange is a sealed interface; if a new variant is added (or an existing one is renamed) it will fall through the chain and be silently skipped by the conflict matrix, with no compile-time error and no runtime signal. Given the file's stated mandate to stay in lock-step with upstream's matrix, a silently-dropped change type is exactly the kind of regression this layer is meant to prevent.
secondary.suggestion: Convert to a switch over the sealed type (pattern switch) so the compiler enforces exhaustiveness, or add a final `else { throw new IllegalStateException("unhandled WriteChange: " + change.getClass()); }`.

secondary.title: Stale comment claims delete-vs-delete overlap check is omitted, but it is implemented elsewhere
secondary.detail: The comment asserts the upstream :1259-1283 delete-vs-delete file-overlap check is 'intentionally omitted' because the state-based LogicalConflictCheck.checkDeletedFromTable subsumes it. That is no longer accurate: JdbcDucklakeCatalog.checkDeleteFileOverlap (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1295-1333) explicitly re-implements that exact upstream range with its own JDBC query against ducklake_delete_file, and its comment even cites the same :1259-1283 lines. The two comments contradict each other and a maintainer reading ConflictMatrix could wrongly conclude the overlap case is unguarded.
secondary.suggestion: Update the comment to point to JdbcDucklakeCatalog.checkDeleteFileOverlap as the place the overlap check actually lives, rather than claiming it is omitted.

secondary.title: Two distinct compaction kinds report the identical 'compacted it' reason
secondary.detail: tablesMergeAdjacent and tablesRewriteDelete produce the byte-identical conflict reason 'compacted it'. When a delete conflicts, the operator cannot tell from the message whether the intervening commit was a merge_adjacent or a rewrite_delete, which are different operations with different remediation. This degrades the diagnosability of an already-rare concurrency failure.
secondary.suggestion: Differentiate the theirAction strings, e.g. "merged adjacent files" vs "rewrote a delete file".

secondary.title: Unreachable catch block for DucklakePartitionTransform.valueOf
secondary.detail: TRANSFORM_PATTERN's group(1) alternation is restricted to exactly (year|month|day|hour), each of which uppercases to a valid DucklakePartitionTransform constant (YEAR/MONTH/DAY/HOUR). DucklakePartitionTransform.valueOf(transformName) at line 192 can therefore never throw IllegalArgumentException, so the catch block at lines 194-196 ('Unknown partition transform') is dead code that can never execute. It gives a false impression that arbitrary transform names reach this point.
secondary.suggestion: Drop the try/catch and call valueOf directly, or replace the regex group with a broader pattern if the intent was to surface 'unknown transform' errors for things like week()/quarter().

secondary.title: childrenByParent map-building duplicated verbatim in DucklakeStatsLeafProjector
secondary.detail: The exact parentColumnId -> childName -> DucklakeColumn index construction here is duplicated in DucklakeStatsLeafProjector.buildChildrenByParent (lines 63-73 of that file), down to the same structure and getOrDefault(Map.of()) usage at call sites. Two copies of the same field-id traversal scaffolding can drift (e.g. one adds the active-column filter suggested above and the other doesn't), producing inconsistent field-id resolution between written schema and emitted stats.
secondary.suggestion: Extract a shared helper (e.g. a small DucklakeColumnTree utility) used by both the schema builder and the stats leaf projector so the traversal stays in lockstep.

secondary.title: Redundant null check in transformBlockToInteger
secondary.detail: The loop checks sourceBlock.isNull(position) and appends null, then in the else branch calls computePartitionValue which itself starts with `if (block.isNull(position)) return null;` and the code then re-checks `if (valueStr == null) appendNull()`. The outer isNull branch makes the inner valueStr==null path dead for the only null source (a non-null position can never yield null from computePartitionValue here). Two null paths obscure intent.
secondary.suggestion: Drop the outer isNull guard and rely on the valueStr==null check, or vice versa — keep a single null path.

secondary.title: completedPositions tracked but never exposed (dead field)
secondary.detail: The field completedPositions is incremented on every page (line 137) but getCompletedPositions() from ConnectorPageSource is not overridden, so the SPI default (OptionalLong.empty()) is used and the accumulated value is never read. The work to maintain it is wasted and the presence of the field implies a metric is exposed when it is not.
secondary.suggestion: Either override getCompletedPositions() to return OptionalLong.of(completedPositions), or remove the field and its increment.

secondary.title: cacheKey uses platform-default charset for getBytes()
secondary.detail: remotePath.toString().getBytes() and Long.toString(fileSize).getBytes() use the JVM default charset. The cache key is only consumed within the same JVM so this does not currently break correctness, but it makes the hash depend on file.encoding and is an unnecessary determinism hazard if the key is ever persisted, logged for cross-process comparison, or the default charset differs across deployments.
secondary.suggestion: Pass StandardCharsets.UTF_8 explicitly to getBytes().

secondary.title: ExecutionRequest compact constructor null-guards only two of five fields, leaving projectedColumns un-normalised
secondary.detail: The compact constructor defensively copies/null-guards pushedExpressions and duckDbTimeZone, but target, projectedColumns, and pushedPredicate are stored as-is with no null check and no defensive copy. isEmptyProjection() (line 124) and DuckDbFilePageSource's constructor (line 89, which calls isEmptyProjection() during page-source construction) dereference projectedColumns.isEmpty(); a null projectedColumns therefore constructs successfully but NPEs later at an unrelated call site rather than failing fast at construction. Production callers happen to wrap arguments in requireNonNull/List.copyOf, but the record's own contract does not guarantee it, and the inconsistent treatment (two fields hardened, three not) is a trap for future callers and the test constructors that build ExecutionRequest directly. projectedColumns is also exposed as a live mutable reference rather than List.copyOf like pushedExpressions.
secondary.suggestion: In the compact constructor add requireNonNull for target, projectedColumns, and pushedPredicate, and wrap projectedColumns = List.copyOf(projectedColumns) for the same immutability guarantee already applied to pushedExpressions.

secondary.title: Dead branches: discreteValues callback and Integer instanceof never execute for bucket-supported types
secondary.detail: As established above, bucket(N) only applies to orderable types backed by SortedRangeSet, which never invokes the valuesFunction (lines 73-80) — that callback is dead. Relatedly, Trino's native container for INTEGER/TINYINT/SMALLINT/DATE single values is Long, so range.getSingleValue() yields Long and hits line 105; the 'value instanceof Integer' branch at lines 108-110 in nativeValueBlock is also dead for these types. Not a behavioral bug, but dead code that obscures which paths are actually exercised and which one (the discreteValues path) is silently incorrect.
secondary.suggestion: Either drop the unreachable Integer branch and replace the discreteValues callback with a conservative 'return true', or add a comment documenting that only the ranges branch is reachable for bucket pruning so a future reader doesn't trust the discreteValues path.

secondary.title: SWANLAKE engine fails only at first-query split time, not at catalog startup
secondary.detail: create() is invoked per page-source/per-split (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:617). Because DucklakeExecutionEngine.SWANLAKE is an accepted config value with no @AssertTrue guard in DucklakeConfig (unlike QUACK's isQuackEngineConfigComplete at line 379-387), a catalog configured with ducklake.execution-engine=swanlake binds and starts successfully, then throws NOT_SUPPORTED on every read split at query time. The failure is deferred from startup to first use, so an operator only discovers the misconfiguration when a query runs rather than when the catalog is loaded.
secondary.suggestion: Add an @AssertTrue (or reject in a config-validation method) that fails catalog startup when executionEngine == SWANLAKE, so the unsupported-engine error surfaces at config-binding time rather than per-split at query time.

secondary.title: Invariant-violation exceptions surface as generic internal errors on the write path
secondary.detail: collect() throws raw IllegalArgumentException (line 85-86, anonymous row field) and IllegalStateException (lines 89-90, 99-100, 111-112) for catalog-tree/type mismatches. This code runs inside ParquetFileWriter's constructor, which is invoked per output file from DucklakePageSink.openParquetWriter() during INSERT/CTAS/MERGE. These unchecked exceptions are not translated to a TrinoException, so a malformed catalog tree (e.g. a ROW/ARRAY/MAP child missing from ducklake_column) aborts the write with an opaque generic engine error instead of a clean error code. Note the inconsistency with the parallel CREATE path in DucklakeMetadata.toColumnSpec() (line 931), which throws TrinoException(NOT_SUPPORTED) for the same anonymous-row condition. These are 'should never happen' invariants, hence low severity, but the failure mode is worse than necessary.
secondary.suggestion: Throw TrinoException with a DuckLake-specific error code (e.g. the connector's catalog/metadata error code) instead of IllegalState/IllegalArgument, mirroring toColumnSpec(), so a corrupt catalog tree fails the write with a diagnosable code rather than an internal error.

secondary.title: Compact constructor silently substitutes null fileFormat with "parquet" while calling requireNonNull on other fields
secondary.detail: The compact constructor calls `requireNonNull` for `path`, `columnStats`, and handles other nulls defensively, but `fileFormat = null` is silently promoted to `"parquet"` (lines 43-44). This inconsistency means a caller that accidentally omits `fileFormat` via the canonical constructor gets a wrong-but-silent default rather than a loud failure, making it easy to commit files with the wrong format tag. The JSON path (missing `"fileFormat"` key) also silently defaults rather than erroring.
secondary.suggestion: Either add `requireNonNull(fileFormat, "fileFormat is null")` and require all callers to be explicit, or at least document in a comment that null→parquet substitution is the intentional JSON-backwards-compat policy. The convenience constructors that hard-code `"parquet"` are the preferred API for non-DuckDB formats; the canonical constructor should not silently paper over a missing value.

secondary.title: Inconsistent trim: bucket path trims, simple-transform path does not
secondary.detail: Line 60 applies `value.trim()` before the bucket-pattern match. Line 65 passes the original `value` (un-trimmed) to `fromString(value)`, which calls `value.toUpperCase(Locale.ENGLISH)` then `Enum.valueOf()`. If `value` has leading/trailing whitespace (e.g. `" identity"`) the bucket branch would succeed while the simple-transform branch would throw `IllegalArgumentException` with an opaque message. The divergence is purely accidental.
secondary.suggestion: Extract `String trimmed = value.trim()` before line 60 and use `trimmed` in both branches: `BUCKET_PATTERN.matcher(trimmed)` and `fromString(trimmed)`.

secondary.title: Dead branch: type.equals(VARCHAR) is always subsumed by type instanceof VarcharType
secondary.detail: VarcharType.equals compares by length, and VARCHAR (unbounded) is itself a VarcharType instance. The instanceof clause already catches every VarcharType including unbounded VARCHAR, so the type.equals(VARCHAR) branch is never the deciding factor. It is dead code — slightly misleading and inconsistent with every other branch in the method which uses type.equals.
secondary.suggestion: Remove the type.equals(VARCHAR) sub-clause; keep only `type instanceof VarcharType`.

secondary.title: Null-defaulting a record field in the compact constructor is fragile
secondary.detail: Records are meant to be transparent data carriers; mutating a parameter in the compact constructor to supply a default (if (duckDbWriterMode == null) { duckDbWriterMode = ...; }) undermines that contract. The field can arrive null only from Jackson deserialization of old JSON that predates the field (a backward-compat concern). However, this silently accepts null at any other call site too — a programmatic null passes through without the requireNonNull guard that all other fields get. If a future refactor adds a call site that forgets to supply the value, the default silently masks it rather than failing fast.
secondary.suggestion: Either add @JsonSetter(nulls = Nulls.SKIP) with a @JsonProperty defaultValue, or keep the compact-constructor null-guard but also add it to the requireNonNull chain to document that null is only tolerated as a JSON-deserialization artifact. At minimum, invert the condition: requireNonNull first, then use a @JsonDeserialize default.

secondary.title: IOException from footer-size computation is silently swallowed, storing 0 for delete file footer_size
secondary.detail: If Util.writeFileMetaData throws, footerSize falls back to 0 with no log output, and 0 is then stored in the DucklakeDeleteFragment. JdbcDucklakeCatalog.applyDeleteFragments (line 2244) calls r.setFooterSize(fragment.footerSize()) unconditionally, persisting the literal 0. The catalog's data-file write path (lines 1970-1976) explicitly documents that 0 is wrong ('Invalid footer length') and only sets footerSize when > 0. The delete-file path lacks the same guard. At read time the leak is absorbed because FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource.wrapIfHintUsable guards on footerSizeHint <= 0, so 0 becomes a no-op hint. However the silent exception drop means the failure is invisible and the asymmetry with the data-file path is surprising.
secondary.suggestion: Log the exception at WARN level before falling back, or rethrow it since footer computation on an in-memory DynamicSliceOutput is extremely unlikely to fail. Separately, apply the same > 0 guard used in the data-file path before calling r.setFooterSize() in applyDeleteFragments.

secondary.title: parquetReaderConfig called without requireNonNull, inconsistent with every other parameter
secondary.detail: All other constructor parameters (fileSystemFactory, fileFormatDataSourceStats, catalog, duckDbReadCache, duckDbS3Config, ducklakeConfig, executorFactory) are null-checked via requireNonNull before use. parquetReaderConfig is dereferenced directly via parquetReaderConfig.toParquetReaderOptions() without a prior requireNonNull. An injection failure would produce a NullPointerException with no message instead of the standard 'parquetReaderConfig is null'.
secondary.suggestion: Add requireNonNull(parquetReaderConfig, "parquetReaderConfig is null") before calling toParquetReaderOptions(), or chain: this.parquetReaderOptions = requireNonNull(parquetReaderConfig, "parquetReaderConfig is null").toParquetReaderOptions();

secondary.title: Unchecked narrowing cast of BIGINT partition_key_index to int
secondary.detail: The JOOQ-generated DUCKLAKE_PARTITION_COLUMN.PARTITION_KEY_INDEX field is declared as TableField<..., Long> (SQLDataType.BIGINT). orZero() returns a long, and the cast (int) orZero(...) silently truncates. Values in practice are tiny, but the compiler gives no warning and the truncation is invisible. The DucklakePartitionField record declares the field as int, so the type mismatch is baked in.
secondary.suggestion: Use Math.toIntExact(orZero(r.get(partcol.PARTITION_KEY_INDEX))) to get a clear ArithmeticException if overflow ever occurs, rather than silent bit-truncation.

secondary.title: Missing message-only constructor forces subclass to hard-code null cause
secondary.detail: TransactionConflictException only exposes a two-argument constructor (String, Throwable). Its immediate subclass LogicalConflictException consequently has to call super(message, null) to construct a no-cause instance. Passing an explicit null where a meaningful cause doesn't exist is the right semantic, but the parent class DucklakeException already provides both the one-arg and two-arg forms. The missing (String) overload in TransactionConflictException is what forces the awkward null literal to appear in LogicalConflictException line 42, and would force the same pattern on any future subclass that has no underlying cause to attach.
secondary.suggestion: Add a single-argument constructor: public TransactionConflictException(String message) { super(message); }. Then update LogicalConflictException(String message) to call super(message) instead of super(message, null).

secondary.title: Redundant null check that can never fire
secondary.detail: requireNonNull(config.getCatalogDatabaseUrl(), ...) at line 37 cannot trigger: getCatalogDatabaseUrl() is annotated @NotNull and airlift's config validation rejects a null value at startup before any provider get() call. The check adds noise without protecting anything.
secondary.suggestion: Remove the requireNonNull call; the @NotNull constraint on the config field already covers it.

secondary.title: fromCatalog() duplicates Enum.valueOf() with an explicit switch
secondary.detail: fromCatalog() toUpperCases the input then switches on the result, producing exactly the same behaviour as Enum.valueOf(DucklakeNullOrder.class, value.trim().toUpperCase(ROOT)) — including a thrown exception on unknown values. DucklakeSortDirection.fromCatalog() (lines 25-35 of DucklakeSortDirection.java) has the identical structure. The explicit switches add maintenance surface: any future enum value must be added in two places (the enum body and the switch), and the two files must stay in lockstep on the same pattern.
secondary.suggestion: Replace both fromCatalog() implementations with Enum.valueOf(DucklakeNullOrder.class, value.trim().toUpperCase(Locale.ROOT)) and Enum.valueOf(DucklakeSortDirection.class, value.trim().toUpperCase(Locale.ROOT)) respectively; or, if the friendlier error message matters, at least route through valueOf() and catch IllegalArgumentException to rethrow with the custom message.

secondary.title: fromCatalog() duplicates Enum.valueOf() with an explicit switch (same as DucklakeNullOrder)
secondary.detail: See DucklakeNullOrder finding above — both files are structurally identical and carry the same maintenance burden.
secondary.suggestion: Replace with Enum.valueOf(DucklakeSortDirection.class, value.trim().toUpperCase(Locale.ROOT)), mirroring the fix in DucklakeNullOrder.

secondary.title: DucklakeSchema record has no compact constructor — schemaName, schemaUuid, and all three Optional fields are unguarded against null
secondary.detail: DucklakeColumnStats (same package) guards its Optional fields with requireNonNull. DucklakeTable and DucklakeInlinedDataInfo also have compact constructors with requireNonNull. DucklakeSchema has no compact constructor at all, so a null schemaName (possible if the DB row is corrupt or a test passes null), null schemaUuid, or null Optional reference for endSnapshot/path/pathIsRelative will propagate silently until it NullPointerExceptions at the call site, which is far from construction.
secondary.suggestion: Add a compact constructor with requireNonNull for schemaName, schemaUuid, endSnapshot, path, and pathIsRelative, matching the pattern in DucklakeColumnStats and DucklakeTable.

secondary.title: DucklakeAddFilesException cause dropped when wrapping into TrinoException
secondary.detail: The catch block does new TrinoException(INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT, e.getMessage()) rather than new TrinoException(INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT, e.getMessage(), e). The original stack trace is discarded, so any runtime exception that propagated through DucklakeAddFilesNameMapper and wrapped into a DucklakeAddFilesException loses its origin in server logs.
secondary.suggestion: Pass e as the third argument: new TrinoException(INVALID_PROCEDURE_ARGUMENT, e.getMessage(), e).

secondary.title: tableId and fileSizeBytes fields fetched but never read by any caller
secondary.detail: DucklakeMetadata.java (line 430) accesses only recordCount() from this record. Neither tableId nor fileSizeBytes is ever accessed by any caller via the DucklakeTableStats accessor — both fields are mapped from the DB row in toDucklakeTableStats (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2362-2365) and then silently discarded. The record carries two dead fields selected from the database on every getTableStats() call.
secondary.suggestion: Either remove tableId and fileSizeBytes from the record (making it a single-value wrapper, at which point a plain OptionalLong works), or add the fileSizeBytes to the TableStatistics.Builder as an Estimate if the spec warrants exposing physical data size.

secondary.title: partitionKeyIndex declared as int but the underlying DB column is BIGINT
secondary.detail: The generated schema (DucklakeFilePartitionValueTable.java) declares PARTITION_KEY_INDEX as SQLDataType.BIGINT (Long). When the record is constructed in JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:704 the value is retrieved as a long via orZero() and then silently narrowed with (int). The write path at JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2002 casts the Map<Integer,String> key up to long before storing. The round-trip works because no table will ever have 2^31 partition fields, but the declared type int on partitionKeyIndex is inconsistent with the column's BIGINT type and the silent narrowing cast masks any future schema widening.
secondary.suggestion: Change partitionKeyIndex from int to long in the record to match the DB column type and eliminate the silent cast at JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:704.

refuted.title: Conflict rethrow casts the outer exception, not the matched TransactionConflictException
refuted.detail: The catch detects a conflict with hasTransactionConflict(e), which walks the entire cause chain, but then rethrows via (RuntimeException) e -- casting the OUTER exception. If a TransactionConflictException were ever wrapped as the cause of a non-RuntimeException (the catch is on Exception, and action.execute is declared to throw SQLException), hasTransactionConflict returns true while the cast throws ClassCastException, masking the real conflict. Today the conflict checks throw the exception directly so e is the conflict, so this is latent rather than active.
refuted.suggestion: Locate the actual TransactionConflictException in the chain (a small helper returning the matched instance) and rethrow that, or guard the rethrow with `if (e instanceof TransactionConflictException tce) throw tce;` before the chain-walk fallback.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The cast (RuntimeException) e at JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1183 cannot throw ClassCastException in any actual code path. Type chain: LogicalConflictException extends TransactionConflictException (TransactionConflictException.java:27-28) extends DucklakeException (DucklakeException.java:19-20) extends RuntimeException. Every path that makes hasTransactionConflict(e) true throws the conflict exception DIRECTLY as e, never wrapped: ensureSnapshotLineageUnchanged (line 1130 -> 1201) throws transactionConflictException(...) which returns a TransactionConflictException; LogicalConflictCheck.run (1140) and runConflictMatrix/checkDeleteFileOverlap (1151, 1326) throw LogicalConflictException directly; insertSnapshotRow (1156 -> 1221) throws transactionConflictException directly. In all cases e itself instanceof TransactionConflictException, so the hasTransactionConflict chain-walk (1347-1357) matches at depth 0 and the cast to RuntimeException is valid. The finding's failure mode requires a TransactionConflictException wrapped as the cause of a non-RuntimeException e. The action callback is declared throws SQLException (1039-1040), but the action lambdas (jOOQ/JDBC mutations at 1418-2211) do not catch and re-wrap a TransactionConflictException into a checked SQLException; conflict exceptions are produced by framework code after action.execute() returns, not inside it. The finding itself concedes 'Today the conflict checks throw the exception directly so e is the conflict, so this is latent rather than active.' So there is no reachable bug. The detector-walks-chain vs cast-assumes-depth-0 mismatch is a mild robustness smell and the suggested instanceof-pattern rethrow is marginally cleaner, but as filed the claimed consequence is unreachable. Refuted as an actual defect; remains a low/cosmetic nitpick at most.

refuted.title: typedCompare/getColumnStats does string comparison for timestamp columns
refuted.detail: Beyond the numeric mismatch above, timestamp/timestamptz/timestamp_s/ms/ns columns are not handled by typedCompare and fall to lexicographic string comparison of the stored stat text. Whether this is correct depends entirely on the textual encoding DuckLake writes for timestamp stats (zero-padded ISO is order-preserving; offsets, varying fractional digits, or 'T' vs space separators are not). Combined with the numeric bug this means typedMin/typedMax is only reliably correct for date columns.
refuted.suggestion: Handle timestamp* by parsing to the appropriate temporal type for comparison, or document and test the exact stat text encoding to confirm lexicographic order matches chronological order.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's literal premise is correct: in typedCompare (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:587-595) timestamp/timestamptz/timestamp_s/_ms/_ns are not switch cases and fall through to `default -> a.compareTo(b)` (line 594), i.e. lexicographic string comparison, used by typedMin/typedMax (576/581) to pick the surviving min/max string when merging stats across multiple data files (550/553). But the claimed consequence is unobservable, so the practical impact does not hold.

The ONLY production consumer of getColumnStats' returned min/max is DucklakeMetadata.getTableStatistics → toDoubleRange (DucklakeMetadata.java:463, 492, 535-561). toDoubleRange only emits a range for BIGINT/INTEGER/SMALLINT/TINYINT (545), DOUBLE/REAL (548), and DATE (551); for every timestamp type it falls through and returns Optional.empty() (560). So the timestamp min/max string typedCompare picks — right or wrong, lexicographic or not — is discarded before reaching Trino. I confirmed via grep that getColumnStats has exactly one non-test caller (DucklakeMetadata) and that DucklakeColumnStats.minValue/maxValue is not exposed through any metadata table; the other minValue/maxValue references (JdbcDucklakeCatalog 2015-2016, 2277-2284) are the write path building a different stats record, and 471-472 is the separate file-pruning path using parseStatValue, not typedCompare. So 'typedMin/typedMax only reliably correct for date columns' has no user-visible effect for timestamps.

Separately, while reading I confirmed the real (but distinct) issue the finding alludes to as 'the numeric mismatch above': the DuckLake catalog stores canonical type names (int8/int16/int32/int64, float32/float64, timestamp, decimal(p,s)) per DucklakeTypeConverter.java:118-150, but typedCompare switches on SQL/DuckDB display names (bigint, integer, double, real, decimal — line 588-591), so numeric columns never match and also fall to string compare. That numeric case IS observable because toDoubleRange does consume numeric ranges and re-parses the (possibly lexicographically-wrong-survivor) string — but that is the separate numeric finding, not this timestamp finding. As scoped to timestamps, the consequence is dead code at the consumption site; severity stays low at most.

refuted.title: Views with corrupt/missing metadata are listed but cannot be opened
refuted.detail: isViewAccessible (line 1493) only checks the dialect string, so a trino/brikk view whose column_aliases JSON is missing or fails to decode is still returned by listViews/getViews-iteration. But getView -> decodeTrinoView returns Optional.empty() for that same view (line 1521-1522), so SHOW TABLES / listViews advertises the view while any attempt to query or describe it reports the relation as not found. getViews (line 1356) silently drops it via ifPresent, so listViews and getViews disagree on the view set.
refuted.suggestion: Either keep such views out of listViews (treat undecodable metadata as inaccessible) or, if they should be surfaced, fail loudly with a clear TrinoException instead of returning empty so the list and the resolve paths agree.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The mechanical claim is accurate: listViews/getViews filter only on dialect via isViewAccessible (DucklakeMetadata.java:1300, 1308, 1354), while getView (line 1331) and getViews-via-ifPresent (line 1356) drop a view whose metadata fails to decode (decodeTrinoView returns Optional.empty at 1517-1522). So in the abstract, a Trino-dialect view with NULL/corrupt column_aliases would be advertised by SHOW TABLES but unopenable, and listViews vs getViews would disagree.

But this scenario is not reachable through normal connector operation. The accessible dialect is the custom, namespaced literal "trino/brikk" (line 134), not a generic "trino". Only this connector writes that dialect, and every writer co-writes valid JSON in the same transaction: createView (1378-1381), replaceViewMetadata via setViewComment/setViewColumnComment (1408-1423, 1447-1462) all pass VIEW_CODEC.toJson(definition), which is never null/blank and is always structurally decodable by the same VIEW_CODEC. renameView (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1467) only carries forward whatever metadata already exists. DuckDB/pg_ducklake or any spec-standard engine would never emit dialect="trino/brikk", so their views are filtered out by isViewAccessible regardless of their column_aliases format.

Therefore the only way to hit the listViews/getView divergence is genuine catalog corruption or an external engine impersonating the proprietary "trino/brikk" dialect with bad metadata. The PROJECT BRIEFING explicitly declares this out of scope: "The connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths ... general catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal." dialect and viewMetadata come straight off the catalog row (toDucklakeView, JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2368-2383) with no validation by design. Under the documented trusted-catalog contract a trino/brikk view always round-trips. The fail-soft behavior (warn + treat as not-found, lines 1517/1521) is a defensible response to corruption rather than a correctness bug. Downgrading to low; the inconsistency is real only in an explicitly out-of-scope, untrusted-catalog state.

refuted.title: Merge fragment classification relies on a lenient codec parse plus a path-prefix string check
refuted.detail: finishMerge decides delete-vs-insert by first attempting deleteFragmentCodec.fromJson on every fragment and accepting it only if path() starts with the literal "ducklake-delete-". This depends on (a) the delete codec being strict enough that a write fragment does not parse into a DucklakeDeleteFragment with a delete-prefixed path, and (b) the magic prefix never changing in the writer that produces fragments. If a write fragment happens to deserialize successfully under the delete codec and its path coincidentally begins with that prefix, an insert would be mis-routed as a delete (data loss / wrong row counts). The classification is also order-dependent and fragile. The first catch (line 1264) silently swallows the parse failure, which is the normal path but masks genuinely malformed delete fragments.
refuted.suggestion: Tag fragments with an explicit discriminator field (e.g. a type enum serialized in the JSON) rather than inferring intent from a parse-success + path-prefix heuristic, so insert/delete routing is unambiguous and a malformed fragment fails loudly.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The claimed data-loss consequence (an insert fragment mis-routed as a delete) cannot actually occur given how fragments are produced and routed, even though the finding correctly identifies that the codec-parse is not a real discriminator.

(1) Parse-success is indeed worthless as a discriminator: airlift's JsonCodec mapper disables FAIL_ON_UNKNOWN_PROPERTIES (build/deps-reference/io/airlift/json/BaseJacksonProvider.java:85), so the extra DucklakeWriteFragment fields (pathIsRelative/fileFormat/recordCount/columnStats/...) are silently ignored, and the missing primitive long fields (dataFileId/deleteCount) default to 0. So a write-fragment JSON DOES deserialize cleanly into a DucklakeDeleteFragment. The comment at DucklakeMetadata.java:1258 ("has dataFileId > 0") is misleading — that check is never performed; only path().startsWith("ducklake-delete-") (line 1259) is.

(2) The path-prefix check is collision-free for every fragment that actually reaches finishMerge. The only producer is DucklakeMergeSink. Delete files are named "ducklake-delete-"+UUID (DucklakeMergeSink.java:172). Insert files come from DucklakePageSink: "ducklake-"+UUID+".parquet"/".db" (DucklakePageSink.java:307,333), optionally prefixed by "colName=value/" partition dirs (buildRelativePath, lines 367-382). A UUID's first segment is 8 hex chars; "delete" is 6 chars and contains non-hex letters, so "ducklake-"+UUID can never begin with "ducklake-delete-". Partitioned paths start with the partition column name, further from collision.

(3) The only arbitrary/user-supplied path source, add_files, commits via catalog.commitAddFiles (DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java:233) and never routes through finishMerge or the delete codec, so a user-registered file literally named "ducklake-delete-foo.parquet" cannot be mis-classified here either.

Therefore the asserted insert-routed-as-delete data-loss path is unreachable. The finding's observation that the design is brittle/order-dependent, that the comment is misleading, and that the first catch silently swallows malformed-delete-fragment parse errors is a fair maintainability nit, but it is not the medium-severity correctness/data-loss bug claimed. Downgraded to low.

refuted.title: Redundant getTableColumns round trips within a single metadata call
refuted.detail: getTableProperties calls catalog.getSortKeys then catalog.getTableColumns for the same (tableId, snapshotId) (lines 310, 315). getTableStatistics calls getColumnHandles(session, tableHandle) (line 447) which itself issues catalog.getTableColumns (line 371); the same statistics method also issues getDataFiles, getTableStats, getColumnStats, hasInlinedDeletes, getInlinedDataInfos. These are separate JDBC round trips per planner call on the same snapshot and could be batched/hoisted. Not incorrect, but it is repeated catalog work on the hot planning path.
refuted.suggestion: Fetch the column list once and pass it down, and consider coalescing the per-call stats lookups; at minimum avoid calling getColumnHandles (which re-queries columns) when the column list is already available.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The finding's "redundant ... within a single metadata call" thesis does not hold against the code. getTableProperties (DucklakeMetadata.java:305-334) calls getSortKeys(310) and getTableColumns(315), but those fetch DIFFERENT data (sort keys vs columns), not the same thing twice — so not redundant — and getTableColumns is gated behind a non-empty sort-keys early return (line 311-313), so it is skipped entirely for the common unsorted-table case. getTableStatistics (404-498) calls getColumnHandles(447), which issues getTableColumns(JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:371) exactly ONCE; the other calls in that method — getDataFiles(411), hasInlinedDeletes(418), getInlinedDataInfos(502), getTableStats(427), getColumnStats(463) — each fetch distinct datasets. There is no point where a single metadata callback fetches the same column list twice on the normal path. Crucially, getTableProperties and getTableStatistics are two SEPARATE Trino SPI @Override methods invoked independently by the planner, not "a single metadata call"; the reviewer conflated cross-callback round trips with intra-call redundancy. The suggestion to "avoid calling getColumnHandles when the column list is already available" is unfounded — getTableStatistics has no other column fetch, so getColumnHandles IS the single fetch, not a duplicate. Coalescing across getTableProperties/getTableStatistics would require a request-scoped cache that doesn't exist (JdbcDucklakeCatalog at line 96+ has no caching layer; every method is a fresh jOOQ query), i.e. an architectural change, not a local batch/hoist. The one genuinely duplicated query — getDataFiles at line 411 and again at line 510 in getFallbackRecordCount on the same (tableId, snapshotId), but only on the tableStats-empty fallback path — is not what the finding cites. The underlying fact (several uncached JDBC round trips per planner callback) is true but is normal lakehouse-connector planning cost, not the "redundant repeated catalog work" the finding describes.

refuted.title: TIMESTAMP precisions 1,2,4,5,7,8 build a writer that then throws at first page
refuted.detail: toDuckDbSqlType()/toArrowType() map any non-{0,3,6,9} TIMESTAMP precision to the micros default (TIMESTAMP / MICROSECOND), so CREATE TABLE and the Arrow schema succeed for e.g. TIMESTAMP(2) or TIMESTAMP(8). But populateTimestampVector() (952-1013) only handles precisions 0/3/6/9 and throws NOT_SUPPORTED for everything else. The result is that the writer is constructed successfully and the consumer thread starts, then the first write(page) fails with an exception surfaced from the consumer — after the file/connection/thread machinery is already live — instead of failing fast and cleanly at construction. Trino permits TIMESTAMP(p) for p=0..12, so these precisions are representable types.
refuted.suggestion: Reject unsupported TIMESTAMP precisions up front in toDuckDbSqlType()/toArrowType() (throw NOT_SUPPORTED for p not in {0,3,6,9}) so construction fails before the worker thread and DuckDB attach are set up, matching where the populate path actually draws the line.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's described architecture is real but its claimed consequence cannot occur. The Arrow writer's lazy throw does live on the consumer thread: populateTimestampVector (DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter.java:952-1013) runs inside loadNextBatch (line 703-779) on the runInsert consumer thread, and its else-branch throws NOT_SUPPORTED for precision not in {0,3,6,9} (lines 1009-1011); that error is captured in consumerError (line 246) and only surfaces on a subsequent write() (line 409-412) or finishAndBuildFragment (line 457), after ATTACH + CREATE TABLE + the daemon thread are already live (lines 200-234). So the "throws after machinery is live instead of failing fast" mechanic is accurate IN ISOLATION.

However, a TIMESTAMP(p) column with p not in {0,3,6,9} never reaches the writer with that precision. The catalog spec layer normalizes it first. In both CREATE TABLE (DucklakeMetadata.java:904-906) and CTAS beginCreateTable (lines 1052-1054), the column type is converted via DucklakeTypeConverter.toDucklakeType, whose TimestampType switch collapses every non-{0,3,6,9} precision to the literal "timestamp" (DucklakeTypeConverter.java:221-228, default -> "timestamp"). The page-sink/writer column handles are then rebuilt by reading the type back out of the catalog: beginCreateTable does typeConverter.toTrinoType(col.columnType()) at lines 1072-1080, and "timestamp" maps to TimestampType.TIMESTAMP_MICROS = precision 6 (DucklakeTypeConverter.java:146). Plain INSERT likewise derives handles from the catalog-stored "timestamp" type. DucklakePageSink.columnTypes comes straight from those handles (DucklakePageSink.java:115-116).

Net: by the time DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter is constructed, every TimestampType column handle has precision 6 (or 0/3/9 for the *_s/_ms/_ns spellings), all of which populateTimestampVector handles. The default -> "timestamp" arm in toDuckDbSqlType (lines 304-311), the matching arm in toArrowType, and the else-throw in populateTimestampVector are unreachable defensive guards for an input the upstream pipeline does not produce; they are never exercised, so no live writer ever throws lazily for a representable type. The real (separate) behavior here is silent precision normalization of TIMESTAMP(2)/(8) to TIMESTAMP(6) at the catalog layer — a different, non-lazy issue and arguably an intentional micros-default for the DuckLake spec. The suggested fix (reject in toDuckDbSqlType/toArrowType) would target dead code. Downgrading to low; the finding describes a latent inconsistency in where the guards are drawn, not an observable bug.

refuted.title: Discrete-value min/max computed with lexicographic String.compareTo, not typed order
refuted.detail: extractDiscreteValueBounds derives the pruning min/max by comparing the stringified values with String.compareTo (lines 397, 400). For multi-element sets this yields the lexicographic extremes, not the typed extremes: e.g. values {9,10,100} normalize to "9","10","100" giving minValue="10", maxValue="9" — an inverted range. The catalog then performs typed comparison on these strings in findDataFileIdsInRange, so an inverted/wrong [min,max] can prune files that actually match (wrong results). This branch is reached only for EquatableValueSet (non-orderable types), which limits real-world exposure, but a range predicate over a non-orderable type is itself meaningless and should not be pushed.
refuted.suggestion: Either skip pruning entirely for the discrete (non-orderable) branch (return Optional.empty()), or compute min/max using the type's native comparator before stringifying, so the bounds reflect typed order.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The claimed "inverted range / wrong results" consequence is unreachable. extractDiscreteValueBounds (DucklakeSplitManager.java:387-405) is invoked ONLY through the valuesFunction callback of getValuesProcessor().transform() (line 383). In Trino's SPI only EquatableValueSet dispatches to valuesFunction — verified in EquatableValueSet.getValuesProcessor().transform (deps-reference .../predicate/EquatableValueSet.java:224-227, which calls valuesFunction.apply(getDiscreteValues())). All orderable types use SortedRangeSet, which routes to rangesFunction; that branch (lines 364-382) uses ranges.getSpan() and therefore the correct typed extremes. EquatableValueSet's constructor (EquatableValueSet.java:77-79) hard-throws if type.isOrderable(), so the discreteValues branch handles exclusively NON-orderable types.

The finding's motivating example {9,10,100} requires a numeric column, but all numeric types (int8/16/32/64, float, decimal) and boolean are orderable in Trino and go through SortedRangeSet, never reaching extractDiscreteValueBounds. The example is impossible.

For the non-orderable types that actually reach this branch, the catalog side is consistent: JdbcDucklakeCatalog.parseStatValue (lines 2423-2443) only returns a typed Comparable for isNumericType (lines 2445-2458: int/float/decimal — all orderable, never here) or boolean (also orderable, never here); for everything else it returns the raw String. compareValues (lines 2486-2499) then does String.compareTo. So the catalog prunes with the SAME lexicographic order the split manager used to compute min/max — there is no order mismatch across the boundary, hence no false pruning. The finding even concedes a range predicate over a non-orderable type is meaningless. At worst this is a cosmetic naming/clarity point (min/max are lexicographic for non-orderable types), not a correctness defect.

refuted.title: findRowFieldType silently picks first case-insensitive match on duplicate field names
refuted.detail: findRowFieldType returns the first RowType field whose name equalsIgnoreCase the catalog child name. If a struct has two fields differing only in case (legal in some parquet/Trino RowTypes), the wrong field's Trino type can be selected for type-checking and stats decoding, mis-decoding min/max bytes for that leaf. The catalog-side lookup (childrenByParent, line 150) is also lowercased so it cannot distinguish them either, but here the failure is silent rather than a clear error.
refuted.suggestion: If two row fields collide case-insensitively for the same struct, throw a DucklakeAddFilesException rather than returning the first match, or match exact-case first and only fall back to case-insensitive when unique.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The code at lines 416-424 does pick the first case-insensitive match, and the catalog-side map at line 150 (childrenByParent ... .put(column.columnName().toLowerCase(Locale.ROOT), ...)) is also lowercased, as the finding states. But the claimed consequence (silent min/max byte mis-decoding) does not hold up:

1. Same-source consistency. In mapStruct (line 304/320), the RowType passed to findRowFieldType is built from match.columnType() via typeConverter.toTrinoType (line 193 -> DucklakeTypeConverter line 84-97, which parses the catalog "struct<...>" string), while childTarget comes from childrenByParent (the catalog child-column rows). Both describe the SAME struct from the SAME catalog. They are two encodings of one schema, so they cannot independently disagree on field set. For two RowType fields to collide case-insensitively, the catalog struct string itself must contain e.g. struct<a:int,A:varchar> — and in that case the lowercase-keyed Map.put at line 150 would have already silently dropped one child BEFORE findRowFieldType is reached. The real (and intentional) case-folding point is line 150, not findRowFieldType; the cited lines are merely downstream of it.

2. Not silently wrong in practice. For a primitive leaf, mapField calls DucklakeAddFilesTypeChecker.checkCompatible(targetType, source, ...) at line 267, which throws loudly if the (possibly wrong) selected Trino type is incompatible with the parquet primitive. A silent mis-decode therefore additionally requires the two case-colliding siblings to be type-compatible — an extremely narrow corner.

3. Intentional design. The briefing states catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal on read paths (trusted catalog) and column identity is field_id-based with add_files writing ducklake_name_mapping rows for case differences. Case-insensitive matching is used consistently across the whole mapper (topByName line 155, hivePartitionKeyLower line 159, childrenByParent line 150). Test testAddFilesReadConsultsNameMapForCaseDifferingColumns (line 540) confirms case-differing names are a supported, name-map-mediated scenario — siblings differing only in case is not a representable normal catalog state.

The observation is real as a defensive-coding nit, but the asserted impact requires a catalog state the system's own design assumes cannot occur, and the decisive case-fold lives at line 150, not at the cited lines. Severity stays low at most; effectively refuted as a bug.

refuted.title: Encrypted-footer ('PARE') files yield an encrypted-footer length as the footer_size hint
refuted.detail: readFooterLengthFromPostScript accepts both 'PAR1' and 'PARE' magic and returns tail.getInt(0) as footer_size. For 'PARE' (encrypted footer) the 4-byte slot is the encrypted footer length, not the plaintext Thrift FileMetaData length that FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource expects. If such a value is stored and later used as the prefetch hint, the reader may prefetch the wrong number of tail bytes. Impact is limited (the prefetch wrapper tolerates a hint that is merely the wrong size and can fall back), but accepting 'PARE' here records a semantically different number under the footer_size column.
refuted.suggestion: Only accept 'PAR1' for the footer_size hint; return 0 for 'PARE' so the read path falls back to a blind tail read rather than trusting an encrypted-footer length.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's parquet-spec claim is technically accurate (in PARE/encrypted-footer mode the 4-byte LE slot is not the plaintext Thrift FileMetaData length), but the claimed consequence cannot occur because the code path is unreachable for PARE files.

In DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.java the footer-length read happens only at line 318 (readFooterLengthFromPostScript), which is the sole call site (grep confirmed) and sits in the same try block AFTER line 278: MetadataReader.readFooter(dataSource, parquetReaderOptions, Optional.empty(), Optional.empty()). The two trailing Optional.empty() are the fileDecryptionProperties.

In the vendored Trino MetadataReader.java (build/deps-reference/io/trino/parquet/reader/MetadataReader.java) lines 105-133: when encryptedFooterMode (EMAGIC "PARE") is detected, line 132-133 does validateParquetCrypto(fileDecryptionProperties.isPresent(), ... "fileDecryptionProperties cannot be null when encryptedFooterMode is true"). Since add_files passes Optional.empty(), readFooter throws on any PARE file at line 278 — before line 318 ever runs and before any DucklakeWriteFragment (with footerSize at line 335) is constructed. Therefore an encrypted-footer length can never be recorded into ducklake_data_file.footer_size via this procedure; the b7=='E' branch in readFooterLengthFromPostScript (line 396) is dead with respect to the stored hint.

Additionally, even if such a value were somehow stored, FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource is explicitly built to tolerate a wrong-sized hint: wrapIfHintUsable (lines 59-69) returns the bare delegate when the hint+8 exceeds file size, and readTail (lines 106-124) is single-shot — a too-small cache triggers MetadataReader's normal delegate re-read. So the reader cannot be made to read the wrong footer; worst case is one slightly-off prefetch that falls back. The finding itself concedes impact is limited. Net: not a real bug, an unreachable defensive branch at most. Severity correctly low (arguably none).

refuted.title: Quoted list-element parser silently drops literal backslashes
refuted.detail: The quoted-string branch treats '\\' as a generic escape introducer (line 189): on seeing a backslash it appends the NEXT character and advances by two, discarding the backslash itself. DuckDB's varchar serialization for inlined lists escapes embedded apostrophes (either as '' doubling, handled at line 195, or via the engine's ToString form) but does NOT escape literal backslashes — a backslash in the data is emitted verbatim. Consequently a list<varchar> element containing a literal '\' (e.g. the string 'a\b') is parsed as 'ab' (backslash dropped, following char merged), and a trailing backslash before the closing quote would consume the quote. The varchar list audit (TestDucklakeCrossEngineTypeAudit line 364) never includes a backslash, so this is untested. Silent data corruption on read of inlined string arrays.
refuted.suggestion: Decide on the exact escaping scheme DuckDB emits for the inlined-list ToString form and parse it faithfully. If DuckDB only doubles apostrophes ('') and does not backslash-escape, remove the '\\'-as-escape branch entirely and rely solely on the '' doubling logic at line 195; otherwise treat '\\' -> '\' as a literal and only honor recognized escapes. Add a cross-engine fixture with a backslash-bearing string element.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's core premise — that "DuckDB does NOT escape literal backslashes; a backslash in the data is emitted verbatim" — is contradicted by the vendored DuckDB source. The nested-to-varchar cast that produces the inlined list text escapes BOTH apostrophes and backslashes with a leading backslash.

Evidence in /Users/jminard/DEV/brikk/repos/ducklake-integrations/vendor/duckdb/src/include/duckdb/function/cast/vector_cast_helpers.hpp:
- WriteEscapedString (lines 244-249): for each char, `const bool needs_quote = string_data[i] == '\\' || string_data[i] == '\''; destination[offset] = '\\'; destination[offset + needs_quote] = string_data[i];` — i.e. a literal `\` is written as `\\` and a literal `'` is written as `\'`.
- CalculateEscapedStringLength (line 212) reserves the extra byte for exactly `'\''` or `'\\'`, confirming both are backslash-escaped.
- The quote-trigger LOOKUP_TABLE in nested_to_varchar_cast.cpp marks `'`, `,`, `[`, `]`, `{`, `}`, `(`, `)`, `:`, `=`, `"` as requiring quoting; even when a backslash appears (not itself a quote trigger, but typically accompanied by one or producing quotes), the writer doubles it.

So for the data string `a\b`, DuckDB emits `'a\\b'`. The Java parser at DucklakeInlinedValueConverter.java line 189-192 sees the first `\`, appends the next char (`\`), advances by 2, producing the literal `\` — correct round-trip to `a\b`. The claimed corruption (`a\b` -> `ab`) does not occur. Likewise embedded apostrophes are emitted as `\'` (matching the test comment at line 358-359, "`\'` backslash escape for an embedded apostrophe"), handled by the same branch; the `''`-doubling branch at line 195 is a tolerant fallback for the alternate SQL-literal form.

The `\\`-as-escape branch is therefore exactly correct and faithful to DuckDB's serialization, not a corruption bug. The same fact is independently documented in decodeBlobText's Javadoc (lines 230-233): DuckDB's Blob::ToString emits `\\` for a literal backslash, so a backslash always introduces an escape. The "untested backslash" observation is a minor test-coverage gap at most, not silent data corruption; severity downgraded to low.

refuted.title: Numeric-timestamp fallback assumes microseconds with no unit validation
refuted.detail: When a timestamp value arrives as a Number, toTimestamp treats n.longValue() directly as epochMicros (lines 349-351), and toTimestampWithTimeZone does likewise (lines 371-372). There is no check that the driver actually returned microseconds; a driver returning epoch millis or nanos for an inlined TIMESTAMP would be silently mis-scaled by 1000x. This branch is a fallback (jOOQ normally returns LocalDateTime/OffsetDateTime), but if it is ever hit the failure is a silent multi-order-of-magnitude temporal error rather than an exception.
refuted.suggestion: Since the documented inlined contract is text/temporal objects (not raw Number epochs), either remove the Number branch and fail loudly on an unexpected numeric timestamp, or assert the expected unit explicitly with a comment justifying the microsecond assumption.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The Number branch at lines 349-351 (toTimestamp) and 371-372 (toTimestampWithTimeZone) treats n.longValue() as epochMicros. The finding's claimed consequence (a driver returning millis/nanos silently mis-scaled by 1000x) is not grounded in how this code is actually invoked.

Invocation path: DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:283 -> JdbcDucklakeCatalog.readInlinedData (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:864-905) builds the row values via jOOQ rec.get(i) against the inlined data table in the catalog DB (PostgreSQL or DuckDB). For SQL TIMESTAMP / TIMESTAMP WITH TIME ZONE columns, both the Postgres and DuckDB JDBC drivers (via jOOQ) deliver java.time.LocalDateTime / OffsetDateTime (or java.sql.Timestamp, whose toString() is handled by the text else-branch through parseLocalDateTimeValue/parseOffsetDateTimeValue at lines 392-434). They do NOT return a bare numeric epoch for timestamp columns, so the Number branch is a defensive fallback that is effectively unreachable on the real read path.

Even if it were hit: DuckDB's native internal timestamp unit IS microseconds since epoch, so interpreting a raw integer timestamp as epochMicros is the correct DuckDB-aligned interpretation, not an arbitrary guess. This is consistent with the sibling numeric branches (toEpochDays at 335-342 assumes epoch-days, toLong at 311-317 raw long) which all follow the same 'numeric == native DuckDB unit' contract.

So the premise (some driver returns millis/nanos as a Number) is speculative, and the assumed unit (micros) is actually the correct one for the DuckDB backend. The suggestion to fail loudly or add an asserting comment is a reasonable defensive-coding nicety, but the asserted low-severity correctness bug does not materialize given the drivers in play and the correct unit choice. Downgrade to a cosmetic/clarity note rather than a real correctness issue.

refuted.title: Change-kind matching is case-sensitive; upstream uses CIEquals
refuted.detail: applyEntry() and the upstream-mirroring switch on `kind` use a Java string switch, which is exact-case. Upstream ParseChangeType (ducklake_transaction_changes.cpp:42-85) compares every change-type token with StringUtil::CIEquals, i.e. case-insensitively. The changes_made text is read straight out of the shared DuckLake catalog (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.runConflictMatrix -> parseAll over ducklake_snapshot_changes.CHANGES_MADE), so it can be authored by DuckDB/pg_ducklake. If any writer ever emits a token in a different case (e.g. `CREATED_TABLE`, `Inlined_Insert`), this parser throws IllegalArgumentException "Unsupported change type" where DuckDB parses it fine. That aborts the conflict-detection read path of an otherwise-valid commit rather than detecting/ignoring the change. The file's own Javadoc claims it round-trips catalogs written by DuckDB/pg_ducklake, but this case mismatch breaks that claim.
refuted.suggestion: Lower-case (or use equalsIgnoreCase via an if/else or a normalized key) the parsed `kind` before the switch, matching upstream CIEquals semantics for all tokens including the macro tokens.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding correctly states a factual asymmetry: upstream ParseChangeType (vendor/ducklake/src/storage/ducklake_transaction_changes.cpp:42-85) matches tokens with StringUtil::CIEquals (case-insensitive), while the Java port (InterveningChanges.java:133-168) uses an exact-case `switch (kind)` with a `default ->` that throws IllegalArgumentException. So the read-side case handling genuinely differs.

However, the claimed consequence — that a DuckDB/pg_ducklake writer "may emit a token in a different case (CREATED_TABLE, Inlined_Insert)" and thereby break the read path — is not realizable. The spec-authoritative producer of changes_made is the same vendored ducklake extension's WriteSnapshotChanges/AddChangeInfo (vendor/ducklake/src/storage/ducklake_transaction.cpp:1025-1130). Every token is emitted as a hardcoded lowercase string literal: the generic AddChangeInfo template appends `change_type` then `":"` (lines 1029-1035) with all call sites passing lowercase literals ("inserted_into_table", "deleted_from_table", "altered_table", "altered_view", "inlined_insert", "inlined_delete", "inline_flush", "dropped_table", "dropped_view"), and the inline blocks emit "dropped_schema:", "created_schema:", "created_view:"/"created_table:", "created_scalar_macro:", "created_table_macro:", "dropped_scalar_macro:", "dropped_table_macro:" — all lowercase. pg_ducklake vendors the byte-identical ducklake source (the two transaction_changes.cpp files diff as IDENTICAL), so it produces lowercase too. The upstream CIEquals is therefore defensive symmetric tolerance, not evidence that any real writer emits mixed case.

Consequently the Javadoc's "round-trips cleanly through the matrix" claim holds for every catalog those engines actually write — the exact-case switch matches all canonically-emitted tokens, including the macro tokens and both inline_flush/flushed_inlined spellings (line 159). The only way to hit the default-throw is a hand-corrupted catalog value, which the conventions explicitly place out of scope ("connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths"). The hardening suggestion (lower-case before switching) is a reasonable defensive nicety but addresses a non-occurring input; this is at most a low-severity robustness nit, not a medium correctness bug, since it cannot abort a commit produced by any spec-conformant writer.

refuted.title: parseUnsignedLong rejects valid uint64 IDs above Long.MAX_VALUE that upstream accepts
refuted.detail: Upstream parses every ID change value with StringUtil::ToUnsigned (ducklake_transaction_changes.cpp:166+), which yields an unsigned 64-bit value and accepts the full uint64 range. The Java side uses Long.parseLong, which throws NumberFormatException for any value in (Long.MAX_VALUE, 2^64-1] and for values with a leading '+'. Any such ID (table/schema/snapshot index) in a catalog written by DuckDB makes parse() throw IllegalArgumentException, aborting the conflict-detection read for a legitimate commit instead of detecting the intervening change. The negative-check at line 267 is unreachable for normal Long.parseLong output and only exists to reject an explicit '-' literal, but the real divergence is the upper bound.
refuted.suggestion: Use Long.parseUnsignedLong(value) to accept the full uint64 range (the values are only used as Set<Long> identity keys, so the sign bit being set is harmless for equality), and adjust/remove the now-misleading negative check.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding correctly identifies a literal divergence: parseUnsignedLong (InterveningChanges.java:263-277) uses Long.parseLong, whereas upstream (ducklake_transaction_changes.cpp:166+) uses StringUtil::ToUnsigned wrapped in idx_t/uint64 (TableIndex/SchemaIndex). However, the claimed *consequence* — that a legitimate DuckDB-written catalog produces an ID in (Long.MAX_VALUE, 2^64-1] that makes parse() throw and abort conflict detection — cannot occur with real data, so this is not a medium correctness bug.

Evidence: The change_value strings are catalog entry IDs (dropped/altered/inserted table/schema/view ids, lines 147-160). These IDs are allocated as a monotonically incrementing counter: commit_state.commit_snapshot.next_catalog_id++ (ducklake_transaction.cpp:1366, 1394, 1443, 1490, 1789, 1810). The counter is persisted in the catalog schema as a SIGNED 64-bit column: "next_catalog_id BIGINT" (ducklake_metadata_manager.cpp:175), and read back as such (generated DucklakeSnapshotTable.java:74 NEXT_CATALOG_ID = SQLDataType.BIGINT). Although the C++ in-memory type is idx_t (uint64), the actual stored/serialized values are sequential counters bounded by BIGINT storage; reaching Long.MAX_VALUE (~9.2e18) would require ~9 quintillion catalog mutations, which is physically unreachable. C++ to_string() of these counters never produces a leading '+', so that sub-claim is moot too. The negative-check at line 267 is indeed effectively dead for Long.parseLong output (only catches an explicit '-'), which is a minor cosmetic point.

Net: switching to Long.parseUnsignedLong is a reasonable parity-hardening nit (the values are only used as Set<Long> identity keys, so a set sign bit is harmless), but it guards against an input that cannot exist given signed-BIGINT-backed sequential ID allocation. Downgrade to low; not a real abort-on-legitimate-commit hazard.

refuted.title: computeBucket has no guard against arity == 0 (ArithmeticException)
refuted.detail: computeBucket performs `(hash & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % arity` with no check that arity > 0. If a bucket(0) transform string ever reaches here (the BUCKET_PATTERN regex `bucket\((\d+)\)` accepts "bucket(0)"), the modulo throws an unguarded ArithmeticException rather than a clear validation error. The class javadoc advertises a 'defensive runtime guard' posture for bucket types, but the most likely arithmetic failure mode is unguarded.
refuted.suggestion: Add an explicit `if (arity <= 0) throw new IllegalArgumentException("bucket arity must be positive, got " + arity)` at the top of computeBucket so the failure is diagnosable and not a bare ArithmeticException.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding is built on a misquoted regex. It claims BUCKET_PATTERN is `bucket\((\d+)\)` and accepts "bucket(0)". The actual pattern (DucklakeTableProperties.java:46) is `bucket\(\s*(\d+)\s*,\s*(.+?)\s*\)` — it requires a comma and a column name, so a bare "bucket(0)" does not match at all (it falls through to TRANSFORM_PATTERN and is rejected as an unknown transform).

More importantly, an explicit positive-arity guard already exists upstream at the only place a bucket arity is parsed: DucklakeTableProperties.parsePartitionField, lines 179-181: `if (arity <= 0) throw new TrinoException(INVALID_TABLE_PROPERTY, "bucket(N, col) requires a positive arity, got " + arity);`. This is exactly the diagnosable validation error the finding's suggestion asks for, and it fires before any PartitionFieldSpec carrying that arity is ever constructed.

The arity reaching computeBucket (DucklakePartitionComputer.java:235, `(hash & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % arity`) flows only from that validated PartitionFieldSpec — confirmed via both call sites: DucklakePartitionComputer.java:91-95 (which additionally guards arity.isEmpty()) and DucklakeBucketPartitionMatcher.java:95. There is no path by which arity==0 reaches the modulo. The claimed unguarded ArithmeticException is not reachable given how the code is actually invoked.

refuted.title: REAL/DOUBLE identity partition value uses Java float/double formatting which can diverge from the DuckDB-stored representation
refuted.detail: computeIdentityValue renders REAL via `String.valueOf(REAL.getFloat(...))` and DOUBLE via `String.valueOf(DOUBLE.getDouble(...))`. Java's float/double toString produces values like "1.0", "NaN", "Infinity", "-0.0", or scientific notation "1.0E10". These partition_value strings are compared/round-tripped against the values DuckDB writes for the same identity-partitioned floating column; if DuckDB renders floats differently (e.g. different precision, no trailing ".0", different infinity/NaN spelling), the same logical value can land in a different partition string than a DuckDB-side write, breaking cross-engine partition matching for float identity partitions.
refuted.suggestion: Confirm against the DuckDB ducklake extension how it formats REAL/DOUBLE identity partition_value strings, and align the rendering (or document that float identity partitioning is unsupported/discouraged) rather than relying on Java's default float formatting.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The finding assumes partition_value strings are matched by string equality across engines, but they are not. computeIdentityValue (DucklakePartitionComputer.java:122-127) renders REAL/DOUBLE via String.valueOf and stores that per-file in ducklake_file_partition_value.partition_value. On the read/prune path, DucklakeSplitManager.partitionValueMatchesDomain (line 500-535) calls parsePartitionValue -> DucklakePartitionValueParser.parseIdentity, which for DOUBLE does Double.parseDouble(value) and for REAL Float.floatToIntBits(Float.parseFloat(value)) (DucklakePartitionValueParser.java:64-69). Matching is therefore NUMERIC, not lexical: "1.0", "1", and "1.0E10" all parse to the same IEEE-754 value and prune identically, regardless of which engine wrote the string. Java guarantees Double.parseDouble(String.valueOf(x)) == x (shortest round-trip), so a Trino-written file never lands in a "different partition" — partition_value is per-file catalog metadata, not a directory path requiring byte-identical strings. The page-indexer grouping key within a single Trino write is also internally consistent because String.valueOf is deterministic. The codebase already adopts String.valueOf(float/double) as its canonical float-text convention and explicitly special-cases NaN/Infinity at the sites that matter (DucklakeStatsExtractor.java:145-152, DuckDbWhereClauseTranslator.java:218-225, DuckDbExpressionTranslator.java:450 rejects NaN/Inf literals), so the partition computer is consistent with that. The only residual is the degenerate NaN/Infinity/-0.0 spelling corner where DuckDB's text parser might differ — but partitioning on a NaN float key is pathological, and a parse mismatch there at worst disables pruning (safe, still correct results) rather than dropping/keeping wrong rows. The finding's stated consequence ("same logical value lands in a different partition string, breaking cross-engine matching") does not hold for finite values, which refutes the core claim; severity remains low at most.

refuted.title: REAL literal emitted as bare decimal causes over-restrictive pushdown (dropped rows)
refuted.detail: For a REAL column the literal is emitted as `Float.toString(f)` (e.g. `0.1`), with no type annotation. DuckDB parses a bare decimal literal as DOUBLE/DECIMAL, so `real_col = 0.1` is evaluated by widening the REAL column to DOUBLE: (double)0.1f = 0.10000000149... which is NOT equal to the double literal 0.1. Trino, by contrast, compares REAL = REAL and matches. This predicate reaches DuckDB via createDuckDbPageSource->effectivePredicate (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:603-617), which is the file-statistics/dynamic-filter domain pushed AS additional engine-side filtering; Trino's filter operator above the scan can only remove more rows, never re-admit rows DuckDB already dropped. So an equality dynamic filter on a REAL join key (or any single-value REAL range) can silently drop matching rows. DOUBLE (line 224) is safe because Double.toString round-trips exactly to the same double DuckDB parses.
refuted.suggestion: Emit the REAL literal with an explicit cast so DuckDB compares in REAL space, e.g. `CAST(<Float.toString(f)> AS FLOAT)` (or append `::FLOAT`). Float.toString still yields a value that round-trips to the same float, and the CAST forces DuckDB to compare float-to-float rather than widening the column to double.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
