The finding's core technical premise is empirically false. I verified the actual DuckDB behavior with the DuckDB CLI (the same engine version family this connector embeds).

The translator at DuckDbWhereClauseTranslator.java:215-222 emits a bare decimal literal for REAL columns via Float.toString(f), e.g. `"col" = 0.1`. This becomes a real server-side WHERE filter (confirmed via DuckDbSelectSqlBuilder.java:62-80, which appends the domain clause into `... FROM <table> WHERE <clause>`), so the row-dropping concern is at least on the right code path.

But the claimed mechanism — "DuckDB parses a bare decimal literal as DOUBLE/DECIMAL, so real_col = 0.1 is evaluated by widening the REAL column to DOUBLE" — is wrong:
1. typeof(0.1) in DuckDB is DECIMAL(2,1), NOT DOUBLE.
2. For a FLOAT column, `CAST(0.1 AS FLOAT) = 0.1` returns TRUE, whereas the finding's claimed evaluation `CAST(0.1 AS FLOAT)::DOUBLE = 0.1::DOUBLE` returns FALSE. DuckDB does NOT widen the FLOAT column to DOUBLE; it resolves the FLOAT = DECIMAL comparison in float space.
3. Tested every form the translator can emit against a table containing CAST(0.1 AS FLOAT): bare `=` (matched 1), `IN (0.1, 0.2)` (matched 1), and range `>= 0.1 AND <= 0.1` (matched 1). Also tested f=0.2, 0.3, 1.1, 3.14 — all matched exactly one row each.

So `real_col = 0.1` matches the same rows DuckDB-side as Trino's REAL=REAL comparison; no rows are silently dropped. The suggested CAST-to-FLOAT fix is unnecessary (and the empirical test shows `CAST(0.1 AS FLOAT) = 0.1` and `f = 0.1::FLOAT` produce identical results to the bare form).

There is genuinely no REAL test in TestDuckDbWhereClauseTranslator.java, so a cheap belt-and-suspenders `::FLOAT` annotation plus a fixture would be defensible hardening against future DuckDB type-resolution changes — hence low (not zero) severity — but the alleged correctness bug (dropped rows) does not exist in current behavior.

refuted.title: OutputStream / ParquetWriter leaked if write or close throws in writeDeleteFile
refuted.detail: outputFile.create() opens an OutputStream and a ParquetWriter is constructed over it, but neither is wrapped in try/finally or try-with-resources. If the ParquetWriter constructor, parquetWriter.write(...), or parquetWriter.close() throws an IOException (or the BlockBuilder/write step throws any RuntimeException), the OutputStream is never closed. This leaks an open filesystem/output handle on every failed delete-file write, and the IOException then propagates up through finish() (where it is rewrapped) without any cleanup. The sibling ParquetFileWriter.abort() (ParquetFileWriter.java:115-130) deliberately closes both parquetWriter and outputStream best-effort; this path has no equivalent.
refuted.suggestion: Wrap the writer/stream in try-with-resources or a try/finally that best-effort closes parquetWriter and outputStream (closing the stream) on any failure before rethrowing, mirroring ParquetFileWriter.abort().
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's central claim is technically wrong. It asserts that if parquetWriter.close() throws, the OutputStream "is never closed." But io.trino.parquet.writer.ParquetWriter.close() (deps-reference io/trino/parquet/writer/ParquetWriter.java:213-230) wraps its entire body in try-with-resources on the stream: line 221 `try (outputStream) { columnWriters.forEach(...); flush(); ... writeFooter(); }`. The constructor (line 137) wraps the passed OutputStream in an OutputStreamSliceOutput, so closing that closes the caller's outputStream. Therefore if close() throws (column writers, flush, or footer write fail), the underlying OutputStream is guaranteed to be closed by the try-with-resources. The close()-throws and write-step-throws-then-close paths cited as leaks do NOT leak.

The only genuine residual exposure in DucklakeMergeSink.writeDeleteFile (lines 176-202): if parquetWriter.write() at line 201 throws (or the constructor at 185-193 throws, which does no I/O), close() is never reached and the stream is not closed. That is a real but narrow handle leak — one OutputStream per failed delete-file write, on an already-aborting MERGE/DELETE query, with the orphaned on-disk file already covered by abort()'s documented DuckLake-maintenance cleanup (lines 228-234). It is not the "leaks on every failed write including close()" high-severity bug described.

The sibling ParquetFileWriter.abort() (lines 115-130) closes both best-effort, but that class keeps both as long-lived fields across multiple calls; the merge-sink path is a single local scope. Adding try/finally here would be reasonable defensive hygiene, but the finding materially overstates both the failure modes (close() does not leak) and the severity. Downgrading from high to low.

refuted.title: Delete files written in finish() are orphaned on partial failure; abort() does not delete them
refuted.detail: finish() iterates deletesByDataFile writing one Parquet delete file per entry. If write #1 succeeds but write #2 throws IOException, the exception propagates and the already-written file #1 is never removed. Trino does not call abort() after finish() throws, and abort() (lines 228-234) only forwards to insertSink.abort() with a comment deferring cleanup to DuckLake maintenance — it never tracks or deletes the delete files this sink created. DucklakePageSink, by contrast, records writtenFilePaths and best-effort deletes them in abort() (DucklakePageSink.java:281-289). Result: every failed/aborted MERGE/DELETE leaves orphan delete Parquet files on storage that are never referenced by the catalog (so DuckLake's orphan cleanup, which scans catalog references, is the only recourse) and are silently dropped from any cleanup the connector itself performs.
refuted.suggestion: Track each written delete-file Location in a field, delete them best-effort inside abort(), and on a mid-loop failure in finish() delete the files already written this call before rethrowing — matching DucklakePageSink's writtenFilePaths handling.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The code description is accurate but the severity and "bug" framing are refuted by a documented intentional decision. Facts confirmed: DucklakeMergeSink.finish() (lines 135-150) writes one delete file per entry with no per-call tracking, so a mid-loop IOException leaves earlier files (closed at line 202) orphaned; abort() (lines 228-234) only forwards to insertSink.abort() and its own comment states "Delete files written during this operation will become orphans — cleaned up by DuckLake's maintenance procedures"; and DucklakePageSink.abort() (lines 281-289) does best-effort delete its writtenFilePaths, so the inconsistency the finding cites is real.

However, this is an explicitly documented design decision, not a bug. dev-docs/TODO-WRITE-MODE.md §"Commit-Failure File Cleanup" (lines 764-769) states: "Files written before a failed commit become orphans. Today this is delegated to DuckLake's ducklake_delete_orphaned_files() maintenance procedure rather than handled inline. Once the M8 maintenance verbs land, this becomes self-served from the connector." The PROJECT BRIEFING also lists maintenance/orphan cleanup (remove_orphan_files) as a documented KNOWN GAP delegated to DuckLake. The abort() comment directly encodes this policy.

There is no correctness impact: orphaned delete files are never referenced by the catalog (commitMerge/commitDelete in DucklakeMetadata.finishMerge only commit fragments returned by a successful finish()), so reads and writes remain correct — the only effect is storage leakage on failure paths, which the project deliberately defers to DuckLake's orphan sweep. The "high / correctness" classification is therefore overstated. The legitimate residual point is the abort() inconsistency vs DucklakePageSink (the connector cleans up its own insert files but not merge delete files), but that is a low-severity robustness/consistency nit already covered by the documented "self-served from M8" plan, not a correctness defect.

refuted.title: checkDeletedFromTable consults merge_adjacent and rewrite_delete but never compacted_table
refuted.detail: InterveningChanges parses three distinct compaction-related change kinds (compacted_table -> tablesCompacted, merge_adjacent -> tablesMergeAdjacent, rewrite_delete -> tablesRewriteDelete; see InterveningChanges.java:154-156). checkDeletedFromTable only conflicts against tablesMergeAdjacent and tablesRewriteDelete. The generic compacted_table set (tablesCompacted) is parsed and merged (InterveningChanges.java:123) but consulted by NOTHING in the matrix or anywhere else in the module. A concurrent OPTIMIZE/compaction emitting compacted_table for the same table as my DELETE will not be flagged by the change-vs-change matrix. In practice it is still caught downstream by LogicalConflictCheck.checkDeletedFromTable (compaction end-snapshots the old data_file_ids, so the activeAt query fails), so this is defense-in-depth rather than a silent data-loss hole — but the matrix is documented as the spec lock-step layer, and it silently diverges from the three compaction kinds upstream emits.
refuted.suggestion: Either add `conflictIfMember(c.tableId(), other.tablesCompacted, "delete from table", "compacted it");` to checkDeletedFromTable so all three compaction kinds are handled symmetrically, or document at the tablesCompacted field why it is intentionally never read.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's core claim — that the JVM matrix "silently diverges from the three compaction kinds upstream emits" — is factually wrong. The JVM checkDeletedFromTable (ConflictMatrix.java:178-188) is an exact port of upstream's tables_deleted_from loop. Upstream ducklake_transaction.cpp:1253-1257 consults exactly four sets for delete-from-table: dropped_tables, altered_tables, tables_merge_adjacent, tables_rewrite_delete. It does NOT consult tables_compacted. The JVM code mirrors this precisely (lines 180-187: droppedTables, alteredTables, tablesMergeAdjacent, tablesRewriteDelete).

Crucially, upstream ALSO parses compacted_table into tables_compacted (ducklake_transaction_changes.cpp:70,193) and declares the set (ducklake_transaction.cpp:748), but grep confirms tables_compacted appears nowhere in CheckForConflicts — it is parsed-but-never-consulted in the conflict matrix upstream, exactly as in the JVM. So the JVM "tablesCompacted parsed but read by nothing" behavior is a faithful reproduction of upstream, not a divergence.

The file's class Javadoc (lines 28-31) mandates "Keep this file in lock-step with upstream — the matrix entries are the spec." Applying the suggested fix (adding a tablesCompacted conflict check) would DIVERGE from upstream and break the documented lockstep contract. InterveningChanges.java:38-40 already documents that the full ChangeType enum is parsed for clean round-trip even where the matrix doesn't reference a kind.

The finding itself concedes there is no data-loss hole: LogicalConflictCheck.checkDeletedFromTable (verified lines 117-149) re-validates that each referenced data_file_id is still activeAt the current snapshot; compaction end-snapshots old data files, so a racing compaction is caught there regardless. The matrix omission is therefore both intentional-by-spec and harmless. At most this is a documentation nit (a one-line comment on tablesCompacted explaining it is intentionally unread for lockstep), hence low severity, not a medium correctness issue.

refuted.title: resolveBundledExtensionPathFor re-extracts (REPLACE_EXISTING) on every call into a path another thread/executor may have already LOADed
refuted.detail: Unlike resolveBundledExtensionPath, the per-platform variant is intentionally uncached and runs Files.copy(..., REPLACE_EXISTING) on every invocation. For a platform equal to the host platform, its target (tmpdir/trino-ducklake/<platform>/trino_parity.duckdb_extension) is the EXACT same file doResolve writes and that InProcessDuckDbExecutor passes to DuckDB LOAD. Overwriting a binary that DuckDB has already mmap'd/loaded is undefined: on Windows REPLACE_EXISTING against an open native module fails with a sharing violation (returns empty, breaking the fixture), and on POSIX the in-place truncate+rewrite corrupts the bytes of any concurrent reader that opened the path before the copy completes. The shared RESOLVE_LOCK serializes the two copies but does not protect a DuckDB process that already holds the file open.
refuted.suggestion: Extract per-platform fixtures to a distinct directory keyed away from the host extraction path (e.g. a 'fixtures/<platform>' subdir), or copy to a unique temp file and atomically move; skip the re-copy if the target already exists with matching size.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
resolveBundledExtensionPathFor (TrinoParityExtensionResolver.java:110-135) has exactly two callers, both test-only fixtures: DucklakeTestCatalogEnvironment.java:175 and TestDucklakeDuckDbExecutorBackends.java:106. Both pass a hard-coded containerArch of "linux-arm64"/"linux-amd64" (DucklakeTestCatalogEnvironment.java:168-169, TestDucklakeDuckDbExecutorBackends.java:99-100), never the host platform string directly.

The finding's central premise — that the per-platform target collides with the file the host InProcessDuckDbExecutor has already LOADed and DuckDB mmap'd — only holds on a Linux amd64/arm64 host (where doResolve()'s detectPlatform() returns the same "linux-*" string and thus the same tmpdir/trino-ducklake/<platform>/ path). On the documented common case (macOS host), doResolve() uses darwin-* while the fixture uses linux-*, so the paths are in different directories and never collide.

Even in the Linux-host case the claimed corruption cannot occur given the actual call ordering. The per-platform copy runs once in @BeforeAll (TestDucklakeDuckDbExecutorBackends.java:75-107, before any test body), strictly BEFORE any new InProcessDuckDbExecutor() which only appears in @Test methods (lines 147, 185, 237, 266, 283, 301). So the re-copy happens before — not after — the in-process LOAD. There is no overwrite of an already-loaded file. Across test classes, each InProcessDuckDbExecutor is created and closed via try-with-resources, so no DuckDB process holds the file open when a later @BeforeAll re-copies.

The "re-extracts on EVERY call" framing is also overstated: each fixture calls the method exactly once during one-time setup (guarded by @BeforeAll or a synchronized lazy-init under LOCK), not in a hot loop. And the host extracted path is never LOADed by a host DuckDB at all — TestingDucklakeQuackEngineServer.java:64-67 only feeds it to MountableFile.forHostPath/withCopyFileToContainer, which reads the bytes to copy them into the Linux container; the actual LOAD happens in-container against IN_CONTAINER_PARITY_EXTENSION_PATH. The Windows sharing-violation scenario additionally requires a Windows host running these Linux-container Quack tests, which the fixtures never target.

So the consequence is not real in the actual call graph. At most this is a latent code-smell (an uncached REPLACE_EXISTING copy that could in principle collide if a future non-test caller invoked it with the host platform), which is why I'd downgrade to low rather than refute the smell entirely. The suggested fixtures/<platform> subdir is a reasonable defensive cleanup but not a correctness bug today.

refuted.title: containsNan is always false even when NaN min/max is observed
refuted.detail: convertStatValue returns Optional.empty() for NaN float/double (lines 145-147, 153-155), but the containsNan flag declared at line 78 is never set true and is passed as the constant false at line 119. So a column whose only float values are NaN (or that contains NaN) reports containsNan=false. JdbcDucklakeCatalog merges this flag (lines 2107,2121) and the DuckDB path also hardcodes false, so the catalog's contains_nan column is effectively never set from the parquet path. Low impact today (the flag appears unused for pruning), but the suppressed-min/max plus false NaN flag means a NaN-only column looks like 'no stats' rather than 'has NaN'.
refuted.suggestion: When convertStatValue suppresses a value due to NaN, set containsNan=true (it needs to return that signal, e.g. via a small result holder), or document that NaN detection is intentionally not derived from parquet stats.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The factual observations are accurate: DucklakeStatsExtractor declares containsNan=false (line 78), never sets it true, and passes it as the constant false at line 119; convertStatValue returns Optional.empty() for NaN (lines 145-147, 153-155); both writer paths also hardcode false (DuckDbFileWriter.java:484, DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter similar). But the claimed consequence is overstated and the issue is not a correctness bug.

(1) No read-side consumer: grep for getContainsNan/CONTAINS_NAN read access finds none in trino-ducklake — the connector never uses contains_nan for pruning, so there is zero impact on Trino query results.

(2) Cross-engine reads are safe because of the NULL convention. JdbcDucklakeCatalog writes SQL NULL (not literal false) for the false case — r.setContainsNan(agg.containsNan ? Boolean.TRUE : null) at line 2121, with an explicit comment (2017, 2119-2120) that this mirrors the upstream 'TRUE when set, NULL otherwise' convention. The reference DuckLake C++ engine's pruning wraps every referenced stat in '(stat IS NULL OR <filter>)' (ducklake_metadata_manager.cpp:1283-1284). So a NULL contains_nan is treated as 'unknown' and the file is KEPT. For x = NaN the filter is 'contains_nan' (1149-1150); with NULL it becomes (contains_nan IS NULL OR contains_nan) -> file kept. For x > c it appends 'OR contains_nan' (1168-1169), again null-guarded. No NaN rows are ever incorrectly pruned. The only effect is a forgone positive-pruning optimization on NaN-free float columns.

(3) The 'NaN-only column looks like no-stats' behavior matches the reference exactly: ducklake_stats.cpp:268-275 creates numeric float/double stats only when 'has_contains_nan && !contains_nan' and otherwise returns nullptr (no stats) — identical in effect to convertStatValue returning empty for NaN.

So the suggestion to suppress min/max is already done correctly per spec, and writing NULL instead of false is the spec-aligned safe choice. The genuine residual — never recording contains_nan=true even when NaN is observed, permanently forgoing the positive-pruning hint for cross-engine consumers — is a real but optimization-only gap, not a correctness defect. Severity stays low; the 'correctness' category is mislabeled (it's a missed-optimization, not a wrong-result, issue).

refuted.title: Possible NPE if a column chunk has no inlined meta_data
refuted.detail: rowGroup.getColumns().get(parquetColumnIndex).getMeta_data() is dereferenced directly. In the parquet Thrift model ColumnChunk.meta_data is optional (a chunk can carry only file_path/file_offset with metadata stored out-of-line). For Trino/DuckDB-written self-contained files it is always present, but an add_files-registered foreign file with external column metadata would yield null here and NPE on line 88. The catch in convertStatValue does not cover this loop.
refuted.suggestion: Guard with `if (!columnChunk.isSetMeta_data()) continue;` (or null-check) before reading total_compressed_size/num_values, consistent with the existing defensive `parquetColumnIndex >= columns.size()` skip just above.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The NPE scenario the finding describes — an add_files-registered foreign file whose ColumnChunk carries only file_path/file_offset with out-of-line metadata, yielding null from getMeta_data() at DucklakeStatsExtractor.java:87 — cannot occur, because the extractor never receives raw foreign-file thrift. Both of its only two callers feed it a FileMetaData object where meta_data is guaranteed set:

1) add_files path (DucklakeAddFilesProcedure.toThriftFileMetaData, lines 489-532): the thrift FileMetaData is *synthesized in-process* from Trino's parsed ParquetMetadata. Every chunk is created fresh and gets chunk.setMeta_data(meta) called unconditionally at line 522, with meta always a freshly-constructed ColumnMetaData (lines 501-522). The foreign file's raw bytes never reach the extractor; Trino's MetadataReader.readFooter (line 278) already parsed/validated them into ColumnChunkMetadata before re-synthesis. So getMeta_data() is never null on this path.

2) write path (ParquetFileWriter.finishAndBuildFragment, line 87/102): fileMetaData comes from parquetWriter.getFileMetaData() — a self-contained file produced by Trino's own writer (meta_data always present, as the briefing confirms), and the same object is serialized via Util.writeFileMetaData at line 94, which would itself fail if meta_data were unset.

Given both callers guarantee meta_data is present, the unconditional dereference at line 87 is safe. The suggested isSetMeta_data() guard would be harmless but defends against an input shape that the code's own construction makes unreachable. Not a real bug.

refuted.title: Top-level column with no matching column_id is silently written without a field_id
refuted.detail: When a top-level field name from sourceMessageType is not found in topLevelColumnIds, the field is kept verbatim with no field_id (lines 81-84). The comment says this 'shouldn't happen', but if it ever does (e.g. a name/case mismatch between the converter's names and the handle names), the resulting Parquet column carries no field_id while every sibling does. Because column identity in this connector is field_id-based, DuckLake would then mis-map or drop that column on read with no diagnostic. A silent partial-annotation is worse than failing the write.
refuted.suggestion: Treat a missing top-level columnId as an invariant violation: throw an IllegalStateException naming the offending field instead of silently emitting an un-annotated column.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The asserted consequence (a top-level Parquet column silently written without a field_id, causing mis-map/drop on read) is unreachable in the actual call path. There is exactly one caller: DucklakePageSink lines 128-135. It constructs ParquetSchemaConverter with columnNames = handle.columns().stream().map(DucklakeColumnHandle::columnName) (DucklakePageSink lines 118-120) and then passes the SAME handle.columns() list to buildMessageType (line 133). Inside the builder, topLevelColumnIds is keyed off that same list via the identical columnName() accessor (DucklakeParquetSchemaBuilder lines 70-72). In ParquetSchemaConverter.convert (deps-reference lines 104-107), each top-level field's name is set directly from columnNames.get(i) with no case folding, normalization, or renaming applied to top-level names. DucklakeColumnHandle is a record (DucklakeColumnHandle.java line 30) with a stable columnName accessor, so two calls cannot return different strings. Therefore sourceMessageType.getFields() field names are byte-identical to the topLevelColumnIds keys, and the else branch at lines 81-84 cannot be taken for any well-formed handle list. The 'name/case mismatch between converter names and handle names' the finding hypothesizes has no mechanism to occur. The finding's suggestion (throw instead of silently falling through) is a reasonable hardening nitpick for a dead/defensive branch, but it does not describe a reachable bug, so the correctness claim is refuted. Severity stays low at most, but it is really a defensiveness preference rather than a defect.

refuted.title: childrenByParent uses last-wins HashMap put on (parentId, childName) with no collision check
refuted.detail: The children index is built with .put(column.columnName(), column), so if allColumns ever contains two columns sharing the same parentColumn and columnName (e.g. a historical/end-snapshotted column co-existing with its live replacement after a rename/re-add, depending on what allCatalogColumns() yields), the later list entry silently overwrites the earlier one and an arbitrary column_id is propagated to the Parquet leaf. The order is whatever the input list happens to be, so the chosen id is nondeterministic. The 2360-region catalog code that builds the analogous list uses computeIfAbsent into a List and explicitly validates child cardinality (resolveColumnType throws on wrong counts); this builder has no such guard.
refuted.suggestion: Filter allColumns to active (endSnapshot empty) columns before indexing, or assert no duplicate (parentId, name) pair, so a stale historical column cannot win the lookup.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's premise — that `allColumns` could contain a historical/end-snapshotted column co-existing with its live replacement after a rename/re-add — does not hold given how the list is sourced.

In DucklakePageSink.java:132-135, allColumns comes from handle.allCatalogColumns(), which is populated in DucklakeMetadata.java:995 by `catalog.getAllColumnsWithParentage(handle.tableId(), handle.snapshotId())`. That method (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.fetchTableColumns, lines 358-366) filters with `.and(activeAt(col, snapshotId))`. activeAt (SnapshotRange.java:50-53) is `begin_snapshot <= snapshotId AND (end_snapshot IS NULL OR end_snapshot > snapshotId)`. So the query returns ONLY columns active at exactly one snapshot. A renamed/re-added column gets its old row's end_snapshot set (per the field-id-based identity model in the briefing), so the stale historical row is excluded by the SQL itself — it can never reach the HashMap.put at DucklakeParquetSchemaBuilder.java:65. Within a single snapshot a well-formed table schema has unique sibling names under any given parent, so two active columns sharing (parentId, columnName) cannot occur; the last-wins put has at most one candidate per key. Hence the claimed nondeterministic column_id propagation cannot be triggered.

The finding also mischaracterizes the comparison code. The sibling builder DucklakeStatsLeafProjector.buildChildrenByParent (lines 63-66) uses the identical `.put` last-wins on the same allCatalogColumns list — it does NOT use computeIfAbsent-into-a-List. The catalog's resolveColumnType cardinality validation is about matching Parquet group child counts against the type tree, a different concern from deduplicating same-named active columns, so it is not an analogous "guard" that this builder is missing.

The code is fine as written because the upstream query enforces the single-active-version-per-snapshot invariant. A defensive assert could be added but is not required for correctness. Severity at most low; effectively a non-issue.

refuted.title: IDENTITY routing on REAL/DOUBLE can split one partition directory across two writers
refuted.detail: For IDENTITY transforms buildPartitionPage hands the raw source Block to the PageIndexer, which routes by the block's raw value bits, whereas getPartitionValues derives the directory name via DucklakePartitionComputer.computeIdentityValue -> String.valueOf(float/double). For REAL/DOUBLE columns the two views disagree on NaN and signed zero: two distinct NaN bit patterns (or +0.0 vs -0.0) get different writer indexes from the indexer but both render to the same directory token ("NaN", or "0.0"). The result is two separate Parquet files written under the same partition path/value within a single partitioned write, i.e. a duplicated partition value with split data. Only matters if a floating-point column is used as an IDENTITY partition key.
refuted.suggestion: Either reject REAL/DOUBLE (and other non-deterministically-stringifiable types) as IDENTITY partition columns at table-property validation, or canonicalize NaN/-0.0 before both indexing and naming so the indexer and directory token agree.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's core mechanism — that the PageIndexer routes two distinct NaN bit patterns (or +0.0/-0.0) to DIFFERENT writer indexes while getPartitionValues renders them to the SAME directory token — does not hold for this Trino version, so the claimed split-partition-directory bug does not occur.

Code paths verified:
- DucklakePagePartitioner.buildPartitionPage (lines 146-148) hands the raw source Block to the indexer for IDENTITY; getPartitionValues -> DucklakePartitionComputer.computeIdentityValue (DucklakePartitionComputer.java:122-127) renders REAL/DOUBLE via String.valueOf(float/double). DucklakePageSink (lines 203-240) groups positions by writer index and names each directory from getPartitionValues(page, posArray[0]) of one representative row per writer. So the indexer's grouping decides which rows share a writer/directory.
- The indexer groups by the type's IDENTICAL operator. This Trino has the DISTINCT_FROM->IDENTICAL rework (TypeOperators.java:130,286,413 reference OperatorType.IDENTICAL), and GroupByHash uses IDENTICAL for collision equality plus HASH_CODE.
- DoubleType.identical (DoubleType.java:218-229): Double.isNaN(left)&&Double.isNaN(right) returns true; +0.0==-0.0 returns true. DoubleType.hashCodeOperator (199-206) uses doubleToLongBits (canonicalizes all NaN patterns to one value) and normalizes signed zero (if value==0 value=0). RealType.identical (RealType.java:172-184) and hash (151-158, floatToIntBits) are identical in behavior.

Consequence: both distinct NaN bit patterns and +0.0/-0.0 hash to the same bucket AND are IDENTICAL -> same writer index -> a single writer and single partition directory. No split. The premise 'get different writer indexes from the indexer' is false.

There is indeed no type validation rejecting REAL/DOUBLE as IDENTITY partition keys (DucklakeTableProperties.parsePartitionField lines 166-201 only records the column name; JdbcDucklakeCatalog.createTable lines 1675-1697 only checks the column exists). So float IDENTITY partitions are allowed, but the data-splitting harm the finding describes does not materialize. The only residual is cosmetic directory-name non-determinism: a +0.0/-0.0 group could be named "0.0" or "-0.0" depending on posArray[0], but it remains one directory, not split data.

Residual uncertainty: GroupByHashPageIndexer/GroupByHash live in trino-main (outside deps-reference) so I could not read the grouping implementation directly; the conclusion rests on the well-established fact that this Trino era's grouping uses the IDENTICAL operator, whose presence is confirmed in the SPI. High confidence given that.

refuted.title: Non-identity transform value computed twice per row (build block, then re-stringified, then re-parsed)
refuted.detail: transformBlockToInteger calls DucklakePartitionComputer.computePartitionValue to get a decimal String, then immediately Long.parseLong(valueStr) and INTEGER.writeLong. The computer formats an int as a String solely so this method can parse it back to a long. Besides the wasted String allocation per non-null row, it routes the value through decimal text and Integer-range validation; an EPOCH HOUR value (epochDay*24+hour) for an extreme future/past timestamp is a long that would throw inside INTEGER.writeLong's range check rather than being handled, but the same value is what feeds directory naming, so the failure mode is at least consistent.
refuted.suggestion: Add an int-returning variant in DucklakePartitionComputer (e.g. computePartitionIntValue) returning the computed bucket/temporal integer directly, and have transformBlockToInteger call INTEGER.writeLong on it without the String round-trip.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
Read DucklakePagePartitioner.java:139-184 and DucklakePartitionComputer.java in full. The finding is categorized as "correctness" but the only correctness claim is self-refuting and the efficiency claim is a negligible micro-nit with a dubious fix.

Correctness claim (EPOCH HOUR overflow): computePartitionValue's non-identity outputs are BUCKET (computeBucket returns (hash & INT_MAX) % arity, always in [0,arity) — fits INTEGER), CALENDAR temporal (year/month 1-12/day 1-31/hour 0-23 — trivially fit), and EPOCH temporal. Only EPOCH HOUR (computeEpochValue line 215-217: epochDay*24+hour) can overflow INTEGER, and only for epochDay > Integer.MAX_VALUE/24 ≈ 89M days ≈ year ~246000 — a physically unrealizable timestamp. EPOCH is also the deprecated legacy encoding per the briefing. The finding itself concedes "the failure mode is at least consistent" since the same value feeds directory naming. INTEGER.writeLong (AbstractIntType.java:96-115) range-checks and throws GENERIC_INTERNAL_ERROR; this is no worse than the suggested int variant, which would either (int)-truncate silently or need the identical guard. So the proposed fix does not improve the overflow behavior — it potentially worsens it.

Efficiency claim (String round-trip): real but trivial. transformBlockToInteger (line 173-179) calls computePartitionValue which formats via Integer.toString/String.valueOf (lines 95, 188-191), then Long.parseLong's it back. One small String allocation per non-null row, only for the PageIndexer block build (indexerType=INTEGER, line 74). This is not a hot path relative to Parquet encoding. Critically, getPartitionValues (line 104-119) legitimately needs the String form for directory naming, so computePartitionValue must keep returning String. The suggested computePartitionIntValue would duplicate the transform-dispatch logic across two methods that must produce mutually-consistent values — a real maintenance/divergence risk traded for a negligible allocation. Single-source-of-truth via String is a defensible design choice, not sloppiness. Downgrading from a correctness finding to at most a low-severity style note.

refuted.title: getPartitionColumnName does a linear scan per partition key on every new-writer path
refuted.detail: getPartitionColumnName iterates partitionColumnMappings looking for a matching partitionKeyIndex. It is called from DucklakePageSink.buildRelativePath once per partition key for every newly opened writer. partitionKeyIndex is a stable per-field key known at construction, so the lookup is recomputable once.
refuted.suggestion: Precompute a Map<Integer,String> (partitionKeyIndex -> columnName) in the constructor and look up directly.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The factual mechanics are accurate: getPartitionColumnName (DucklakePagePartitioner.java:124-132) linearly scans partitionColumnMappings for a matching partitionKeyIndex, and buildRelativePath (DucklakePageSink.java:367-382, line 376) calls it once per partition key. But the claimed consequence — a meaningful per-new-writer inefficiency — does not hold up.

1. Cold path, not hot. buildRelativePath is reached only via openNewWriter, which appendPartitioned (DucklakePageSink.java:225-228, 239-240) invokes solely when writer == null (first time a partition value is seen) or on file rotation past targetMaxFileSize. It is NOT called per row or per page. The genuine hot path is partitionPage -> pageIndexer.indexPage (lines 90-94), which never touches getPartitionColumnName. Each call sits behind opening an actual Parquet/DuckDB output file (UUID generation, fileSystem.newOutputFile, OutputStream create, ParquetWriter construction at lines 307-327) — I/O that dwarfs any in-memory scan by orders of magnitude.

2. The scan is over a tiny constant set. partitionColumnMappings has one entry per partition field (built from partitionSpec.fields() at lines 57-77). Real partition specs have ~1-4 columns. The total cost per writer is O(partition_fields^2) with a single-digit constant — effectively free.

So the suggested Map<Integer,String> precompute is a legitimate but purely cosmetic micro-optimization with no measurable runtime benefit. The described impact ('linear scan ... on every new-writer path') overstates a constant-bounded scan on a file-open cold path. Not a real efficiency issue; correctedSeverity stays at the floor (low) at most, and it is better characterized as refuted as a substantive finding.

refuted.title: Decimal compatibility allows scale widening, which loses integer-digit capacity and silently corrupts/overflows values
refuted.detail: The rule accepts a source decimal into the target iff sourcePrecision <= targetPrecision AND sourceScale <= targetScale. This is wrong for representability. add_files registers the parquet file as-is (no data rewrite); the file's values are unscaled integers stored with the file's own scale, and on read the column is interpreted as the catalog (target) type. Two concrete failures: (1) Integer-range loss: source DECIMAL(5,0) (5 integer digits, range up to 99999) is accepted into target DECIMAL(5,2) (only 3 integer digits). 5<=5 and 0<=2 both pass, but the target cannot represent the source's values at all. (2) Even when precision headroom exists (e.g. DECIMAL(5,0) -> DECIMAL(7,2)), the file stores the value with scale 0 while the catalog type declares scale 2, so a stored unscaled integer 123 is read as 1.23 (off by 100x) unless the read path rescales by the scale delta — which the standard parquet decode path does not do. Either way the emitted data is wrong. The acknowledged stats-decoding caveat at DucklakeAddFilesNameMapper.java:268-275 ("values can be wrong if the parquet primitive's byte width disagrees with the catalog widening") is the same class of hazard, here applied to actual row data via decimal scale.
refuted.suggestion: Require exact scale and only allow precision growth: sourceD.getScale() == targetD.getScale() && sourceD.getPrecision() <= targetD.getPrecision(). If scale widening is intended to be supported, it must additionally guarantee integer-digit capacity (targetPrecision - targetScale >= sourcePrecision - sourceScale) AND the read path must rescale by (targetScale - sourceScale); absent a rescaling read path, restrict to equal scale.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The JVM rule at DucklakeAddFilesTypeChecker.java:98-101 (accept iff sourcePrecision<=targetPrecision AND sourceScale<=targetScale) is a faithful, byte-for-byte port of the spec-authority C++ reference DuckLakeParquetTypeChecker::CheckDecimal() in vendor/ducklake/src/functions/ducklake_add_data_files.cpp:759-775, whose condition `source_scale > target_scale || source_precision > target_precision -> Fail` is the exact logical negation of the JVM accept condition. The class Javadoc explicitly states it 'implements the same widening rules as upstream's DuckLakeParquetTypeChecker', and the convention 'evaluate correctness at the right layer' / 'the vendored DuckDB ducklake C++ extension is the spec authority' applies. The reviewer's 'off by 100x' / integer-range-loss reasoning is mistaken about the read path: the parquet DECIMAL logical type carries its own per-column scale/precision (reference reads them at line 602 from the schema element), so the parquet reader decodes values using the file's own scale and rescales by the scale delta during the standard widening to the catalog type — this is ordinary decimal widening, not a raw-unscaled-integer reinterpretation. The reviewer also wrongly conflates this with the stats caveat at DucklakeAddFilesNameMapper.java:268-275; that caveat is specifically about min/max stats bytes being decoded with the target type ignoring the parquet primitive byte width (a documented pre-existing add_files stats limitation), whereas row data flows through the normal reader that honors file metadata. If any representability concern remains, it lives in the upstream DuckLake spec, not in this code, which intentionally matches the reference exactly.

refuted.title: REAL source accepted into DOUBLE target, but add_files does no rewrite so 4-byte floats are read as 8-byte doubles
refuted.detail: isCompatible permits source RealType into target DoubleType. add_files does not rewrite the data file; on read, the column's physical storage in the parquet file is 4-byte IEEE FLOAT while the catalog/Trino type is DOUBLE. Trino's parquet reader decodes according to the requested (target) type, and float->double promotion is not a free physical reinterpretation — the file's column carries FLOAT physical type and 4-byte values. Unless the reader explicitly coerces FLOAT physical -> DOUBLE logical (which add_files schema mapping bypasses by handing the catalog type to the reader), this yields garbage or a decode error at scan time rather than at add_files registration. The same physical-width concern applies to signed-int widening (lines 72-77/113-118), where an INT32 file column is accepted into a BIGINT catalog column.
refuted.suggestion: For a no-rewrite add_files path, restrict numeric compatibility to exact physical-type matches (REAL==REAL, DOUBLE==DOUBLE, and each integer width to itself) unless you have verified the scan path performs the physical widening. If upstream allows int/float widening only because it rewrites, document and gate that here; otherwise tighten the rule to exact match.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's premise — that Trino's parquet reader naively reinterprets the file's bytes using the catalog/target type, so a 4-byte FLOAT column read as DOUBLE yields garbage — is incorrect. The connector's read path passes the file's REAL physical descriptor (not a synthesized one) to the reader.

In DucklakeParquetTypeUtils.constructField (lines 102-106), the PrimitiveField is built from trinoType (the catalog/target type) AND primitiveColumnIO.getColumnDescriptor() — the file's actual physical descriptor. The page source builds these from fileSchema = fileMetadata.getSchema() (DucklakePageSourceProvider.java line 428), i.e. the file's own parquet schema.

Trino's ColumnReaderFactory.create() (deps-reference/io/trino/parquet/reader/ColumnReaderFactory.java) then dispatches on BOTH field.getType() (target) and field.getDescriptor().getPrimitiveType().getPrimitiveTypeName() (the file's physical primitive):
- Lines 175-182: target DOUBLE with physical DOUBLE -> getDoubleDecoder; with physical FLOAT -> getFloatToDoubleDecoder. This is an explicit FLOAT->DOUBLE numeric widening, exactly the case the finding claims is unhandled.
- Lines 158-170: target AbstractLongType (BIGINT) with physical INT32 -> getInt32ToLongDecoder; INT64 -> getLongDecoder. Explicit INT32->BIGINT widening — the int-widening concern the finding also raises (lines 72-77/113-118).
- Lines 133-140: AbstractIntType handles narrower signed-int physical primitives similarly.

So the widening rules in DucklakeAddFilesTypeChecker (RealType->DoubleType at lines 81-83; narrower-int->wider-int at 72-77/113-118) align precisely with widenings the scan path already performs via dedicated coercing decoders, with no rewrite required and no garbage/decode error. The class Javadoc (lines 38-43) explicitly states it mirrors upstream DuckLakeParquetTypeChecker widening rules. The finding's own suggestion concedes the rule is fine 'if you have verified the scan path performs the physical widening' — which it does.

refuted.title: isReady deletes the cached file on size mismatch, including from the pre-lock check, while other readers may hold it open
refuted.detail: isReady() calls Files.deleteIfExists(local) whenever the on-disk size != expectedSize. It is invoked at line 94 BEFORE the per-key lock is taken, and at line 101 under the lock. The class Javadoc explicitly states the cache never deletes files because READ_ONLY ATTACHes may still hold them open across split lifetimes. But a size mismatch (e.g. the same remotePath rewritten to a new size, then an old split with the old size still scheduled, or two splits with different fileSizeBytes for keys that hash-collide in the 32-hex-char truncation) will delete the file out from under an active DuckDB ATTACH. On POSIX the inode survives an open handle, but on the rename/move path and on Windows this is unsafe, and the unsynchronized line-94 delete races a concurrent thread's Files.move into the same local path, which can throw or leave the cache inconsistent.
refuted.suggestion: Make isReady() a pure predicate (no deletion); never delete an existing local file. On size mismatch, re-fetch to the partial file and atomically REPLACE_EXISTING via move (which is the existing flow anyway), and only perform any deletion under the per-key lock. Move the stale-file handling entirely inside the synchronized block.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The cache key embeds fileSize (DucklakeMaterializedFileCache.java line 92 + cacheKey lines 142-149: SHA-256(remotePath + ":" + fileSize) truncated to 128 bits). A file that lands at `local` = cacheDir.resolve(key + ".db") was always fully downloaded to `.partial` then atomically moved (lines 106-107), so it has size == expectedSize by construction. Therefore for any legitimately cached file that an active READ_ONLY ATTACH holds open, isReady() returns true at line 124 and the delete at line 126 is never reached.

The size-mismatch delete fires only against a file whose on-disk size differs from the size encoded in its own key — i.e. a torn/corrupt/foreign leftover, never a healthy file in use. The finding's "same remotePath rewritten to a new size" scenario is self-refuting: a different size produces a different key and thus a different .db filename, so an old reader's file and the new file are distinct paths — nothing is deleted out from under the reader. The "two splits with different fileSizeBytes that hash-collide" scenario requires both a 128-bit SHA-256 collision (negligible) and differing sizes; fileSizeBytes is the catalog's recorded file_size_bytes (one value per data-file path, confirmed via DucklakeSplit fileSizeBytes plumbed from catalog and used at DucklakePageSourceProvider.java:662-663), so two splits for the same file carry the same size.

The line-94-vs-move race is benign: Files.deleteIfExists returns false rather than throwing if the file vanished, and Files.move(REPLACE_EXISTING) tolerates concurrent absence; the only thing line 94 deletes is a wrong-size (corrupt) file, which the locked writer then re-creates correctly. The Javadoc "never deletes files" contract (lines 47-54) is about having no eviction policy for valid entries, not about declining to drop a corrupt/torn file; removing a size-mismatched file is consistent with that intent. No realistic path deletes an in-use file. Residual concern exists only under pathological external tampering, which the briefing places out of scope (trusted-catalog read-path assumption). High severity is unjustified; this is at most a low-severity defensiveness note.

refuted.title: execute() guards zero rows but not zero columns; get(0) can throw instead of returning 0
refuted.detail: The method's own contract (and the interface Javadoc) says execute() returns "0 for DDL or when a wrapped backend can't surface a count." The guard only covers the no-rows case (rows.isEmpty()). If quack_query_by_name surfaces a row with zero columns (e.g. a DDL/statement that does not produce the assumed `Count BIGINT` single-column shape), rows.get(0).get(0) calls Record.get(0) on a fieldless record, which throws (IllegalArgumentException/IndexOutOfBounds) rather than degrading to 0. The intended fallback to 0 is only reached for non-Number values, not for the missing-column case. The whole premise rests on the unverified assumption that every wrapped mutation yields exactly one row with at least one column.
refuted.suggestion: Before indexing, also check column arity: `Record first = rows.get(0); if (first.size() == 0) { return 0; } Object value = first.get(0); return value instanceof Number n ? n.intValue() : 0;` This keeps the documented 0-fallback for shapes that don't carry a count.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The finding's premise — that quack_query_by_name could surface a row with zero columns — is not a realizable shape. Per the class Javadoc (QuackWrappedMetadataQuery.java:121-123), the Quack server runs inner DML via DuckDB's SendQuery and the table function surfaces DuckDB's standard `Count BIGINT` shape: one row, one column carrying the affected-row count (or 0 for DDL). DuckDB statement results, including DDL, always materialize a count column; there is no path through this table function that yields a fieldless record, so rows.get(0).get(0) at line 131 cannot hit the IndexOutOfBounds/IllegalArgumentException the finding describes. The two realizable cases are covered: no rows (line 128 → return 0) and a one-column result whose value is coerced via `value instanceof Number n ? n.intValue() : 0` (line 132), which already degrades any non-numeric shape to 0. Additionally, per the project briefing the Quack executor is experimental, gated behind a test flag, and blocked upstream on UPDATE/DELETE RPC support — it is not production-active, and the existing test (TestQuackWrappedMetadataQuery) only exercises the read/fetchOne paths, not execute() mutations. The suggested size()==0 guard is a harmless robustness nicety against a shape the wrapped function cannot emit, not a real defect.

refuted.title: Typed fetch() double-materializes the result set
refuted.detail: dsl.fetch(wrapped) eagerly materializes the full Result<Record> into memory, then .stream().map(...).collect(toList()) builds a second full List. For large metadata reads (e.g. getDataFiles-style scans routed through this wrapper) every row is held twice and an intermediate Stream pipeline is spun up, where the sibling mapper overload (line 103) and DirectMetadataQuery both use jOOQ's in-place Result.map(...). This is the read path for the experimental Quack backend, so it is low impact, but it is gratuitously heavier than the other two methods in the same class.
refuted.suggestion: Mirror the mapper path: `return dsl.fetch(wrapped).map(row -> row.into(recordType));` which maps in place over the already-materialized Result without the extra Stream/collect allocation.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding's central claim — that fetch() at lines 83-87 "double-materializes the result set" so "every row is held twice" — is inaccurate. dsl.fetch(wrapped) materializes one Result<Record> into memory; then row.into(recordType) produces a second list of mapped R records. The suggested fix dsl.fetch(wrapped).map(row -> row.into(recordType)) does the EXACT same thing: jOOQ's Result.map(RecordMapper) iterates the already-materialized Result and returns a fresh List<R> of newly-created records via the same row.into() call. The fix eliminates only the intermediate Stream/Collectors.toList() machinery — not any second materialization. So the headline consequence does not hold; the proposed change saves nothing beyond Stream-pipeline allocation overhead.

The finding also mischaracterizes the siblings. DirectMetadataQuery's typed fetch (DirectMetadataQuery.java:38-41) does NOT use in-place Result.map — it returns query.fetch() directly because the query is already typed ResultQuery<R>. The Quack path must remap precisely because it executes a raw inlined SQL string, so dsl.fetch(wrapped) returns an untyped Result<Record> that has to be coerced via row.into(recordType). The only sibling that uses Result.map is the RecordMapper overload at line 103, which is a genuinely different code path (positional coerce + caller-supplied mapper).

Net: the real delta is avoiding one Stream pipeline on the experimental, non-production Quack read backend (gated behind -Dducklake.test.catalog-backend=DUCKDB_QUACK per the briefing's KNOWN GAPS, "blocked on upstream RPC ... not production"). That is at most a cosmetic style-consistency nit, not the memory/double-copy efficiency problem described. Severity stays low and the substantive claim is refuted.

refuted.title: Footer-size serialization failure is silently swallowed to 0, disabling the read-side footer prefetch hint
refuted.detail: finishAndBuildFragment recomputes footer_size by re-serializing fileMetaData; if Util.writeFileMetaData throws IOException the catch block sets footerSize = 0 and continues. A 0 is persisted as 'no hint' (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1974 only sets the hint when fragment.footerSize() > 0), so the file silently loses the FooterPrefetchingParquetDataSource optimization and falls back to a blind tail read on every subsequent scan. The failure is not logged anywhere, so a systematic serialization problem (e.g. a metadata shape Util can't encode) would degrade read performance fleet-wide with no signal. Note the value itself is correct when serialization succeeds: it matches the Thrift FileMetaData length written on disk (same DynamicSliceOutput(40) + Util.writeFileMetaData path as ParquetWriter.serializeFooter, and bloom-filter offsets are already mutated into fileMetaData before getFileMetaData() returns).
refuted.suggestion: At minimum log a one-shot WARN when serialization fails instead of silently swallowing; ideally surface the length ParquetWriter already computed (it stores footer.length() in footerReadSize during writeFooter) rather than recomputing, which also removes the failure mode entirely.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The finding's described consequence (silent perf degradation fleet-wide) rests on the catch block at ParquetFileWriter.java:97-99 being reachable, but it effectively is not. Line 85 calls parquetWriter.close() BEFORE the footer-size re-serialization. During close(), ParquetWriter.writeFooter() (ParquetWriter.java:362-376) already serializes this exact fileMetaData object via serializeFooter() (lines 432-438), whose body is byte-for-byte identical to the connector code at lines 92-95 (new DynamicSliceOutput(40) + Util.writeFileMetaData). If that serialization could throw IOException, close() would throw first and finishAndBuildFragment would never reach line 91. getFileMetaData() (line 87) returns the stored reference unmodified, so the object re-serialized at line 94 is the same one that already serialized successfully on disk. Thus a 'metadata shape Util can't encode' would surface as a hard failure at close(), not as a silent 0/no-hint. The catch is essentially unreachable defensive code. The caller behavior is confirmed correct: JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:1974 sets the hint only when footerSize()>0, and a literal 0 is documented (lines 1970-1973) to crash DuckDB's reader, so mapping 0->NULL is deliberate and correct. The suggestion is also flawed: footerReadSize is private OptionalInt with no getter (lines 118, 375) and equals footer.length()+SIZE_OF_INT+MAGIC.length() (the full tail), not the Thrift-metadata length (footer.length()) that the hint column expects; reusing it would write a wrong value. A one-shot WARN in the dead catch is a harmless cosmetic nicety at most, not a real correctness/perf defect.

refuted.title: resolveSnapshotIdById issues a full DB round-trip to return the same ID it was given
refuted.detail: `resolveSnapshotIdById` calls `catalog.getSnapshot(snapshotId)` (a SELECT on `ducklake_snapshot`) and then returns `snapshot.snapshotId()` — which is identical to the `snapshotId` argument. The only effect of the round-trip is the existence check (throws if not found). This method is invoked once per read query when a snapshot ID is pinned at the session, query, or catalog-default level. In high-concurrency workloads (many simultaneous time-travel queries) this adds a needless catalog-DB query per plan.
refuted.suggestion: Validate the ID with a dedicated `catalog.snapshotExists(long)` check (or inline a count query), or validate once at session/plan time and cache the result. At minimum, eliminate the full `DucklakeSnapshot` row fetch and `toDucklakeSnapshot` mapping when the caller only needs the ID echoed back.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The round-trip is NOT needless. resolveSnapshotIdById (lines 100-108) fetches the snapshot specifically to validate that a user-pinned snapshot ID exists, throwing INVALID_ARGUMENTS ("DuckLake snapshot ID does not exist") at line 106 if not. This is a real, necessary validation: the resolved snapshotId flows into catalog.getTable(..., snapshotId) (DucklakeMetadata.java:214) and activeAt() predicates; without the existence check, an invalid pinned ID would silently produce an empty/incorrect result instead of a clear error. The finding itself concedes "the only effect is the existence check" — but that check is the point.

The performance framing is overstated. (1) resolveSnapshotIdById only fires on the PINNED-snapshot path (query override / session property / catalog default — lines 72, 84, 91). The common, unpinned read path returns catalog.getCurrentSnapshotId() (line 97), which is itself a DB query (SELECT max(snapshot_id), JdbcDucklakeCatalog:215-217), so resolveSnapshotId always costs exactly one catalog query regardless — the pinned path is not adding a query on top of a free path. (2) getSnapshot (JdbcDucklakeCatalog:225-232) is a single-row primary-key lookup on a tiny table; this runs once at plan time, not per-split or per-row, dwarfed by the rest of Trino planning. (3) "high-concurrency time-travel workloads" is a niche, not a hot path.

The only legitimate kernel is a micro-nit: selectFrom (SELECT *) + toDucklakeSnapshot mapping materializes a full DucklakeSnapshot record when only existence is needed; a SELECT 1 / count would shave a negligible amount. That is a cosmetic efficiency tweak, not a medium-severity issue. Downgraded to low; the "needless round-trip" premise is refuted.

refuted.title: InterruptedException is wrapped in RuntimeException, losing the Trino error code boundary
refuted.detail: When `sleeper.sleep(waitMs)` is interrupted (line 76), the code sets the interrupt flag and throws a plain `RuntimeException` (line 80). This escapes through `executeWriteTransaction` in `JdbcDucklakeCatalog` without any Trino error code, so Trino surfaces it as an unclassified internal error rather than a query-cancellation signal. Trino cancels queries by interrupting worker threads; swallowing the interrupt into a `RuntimeException` means the query appears to fail with an obscure message instead of being correctly identified as cancelled.
refuted.suggestion: Throw a `TrinoException` with `QUERY_REJECTED` or re-throw the `InterruptedException` wrapped in the existing `DucklakeException` hierarchy, or check `Thread.currentThread().isInterrupted()` and translate to `TrinoException(GENERIC_INTERNAL_ERROR, ...)` with a clear message. The interrupt flag is correctly reset but the exception type should signal cancellation.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The finding overstates the consequence and its suggested fix is architecturally wrong for this layer.

1. The interrupt flag IS correctly restored before throwing (WriteTransactionRetry.java:79 `Thread.currentThread().interrupt()`). The finding itself concedes this. In Trino, query cancellation is engine-driven: the engine interrupts worker threads and tracks cancellation via the query/task state machine and the thread interrupt status — NOT by inspecting the exception type of an escaping RuntimeException. Since the flag is preserved here, the cancellation signal is NOT lost. The claim that the query "appears to fail with an obscure message instead of being correctly identified as cancelled" is not borne out: cancellation classification proceeds from the engine's own state, independent of this exception's type.

2. This layer (jvm/ducklake-catalog) is intentionally engine-agnostic and reusable by non-Trino engines (per the briefing convention "Engine-agnostic write/conflict/type/partition logic lives in jvm/ducklake-catalog"). It has no dependency on Trino's SPI, so it cannot and must not throw a TrinoException — the suggestion to throw `TrinoException(QUERY_REJECTED)` here would couple the reusable library to Trino and is architecturally incorrect. Translation to TrinoException happens deliberately in the plugin layer (DucklakeMetadata.translateCatalogExceptions, lines 1529-1537), which only maps TransactionConflictException → TRANSACTION_CONFLICT.

3. The suggested error code QUERY_REJECTED is itself semantically wrong: StandardErrorCode.java:55 classifies it as a USER_ERROR meaning admission-time rejection, not a mid-execution interrupt. Trino has no connector-thrown "interrupted/cancelled" code that applies here.

4. The wrap-into-generic-exception-after-restoring-the-flag pattern is the author's consistent convention elsewhere: DuckDbArrowStreamFileWriter.java:417-419 and 452-455 do exactly the same (set interrupt flag, wrap InterruptedException into a plain IOException, no special Trino cancellation code).

5. The path is extremely rare (interrupt landing precisely during the exponential-backoff sleep between conflict retries) and not hot. The only legitimate residual nit is stylistic: a plain RuntimeException could be a DucklakeException for consistency with the library's own hierarchy (DucklakeException extends RuntimeException) — but that is cosmetic, not a correctness or cancellation-signal defect.

refuted.title: Integer.parseInt on unbounded regex group overflows for bucket arities > Integer.MAX_VALUE
refuted.detail: BUCKET_PATTERN uses `\d+` with no length cap. If the catalog stores a transform string like `bucket(2147483648)` (or any value whose digit string exceeds 10 digits / INT_MAX), `Integer.parseInt(matcher.group(1))` throws an unchecked `NumberFormatException`. Because `parseCatalogTransform` has no try/catch and its caller (JdbcDucklakeCatalog line 631) is inside a jOOQ record-mapper lambda, the exception propagates as an unhandled runtime failure and aborts the entire `getDataFiles` query for that table.
refuted.suggestion: Either cap the regex to at most 10 digits (`\d{1,10}`) or wrap the parseInt in a try/catch and rethrow as a descriptive `TrinoException` / `IllegalStateException` with the bad catalog value in the message.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The Java mechanics are real (BUCKET_PATTERN `\d+` at line 30 is uncapped; `Integer.parseInt(matcher.group(1))` at line 62 throws NumberFormatException on >INT_MAX; parseCatalogTransform has no try/catch), but the finding's impact analysis and severity do not hold up.

1) Caller misidentified: line 631 is inside getPartitionSpecs, NOT getDataFiles as the finding claims. The "aborts the entire getDataFiles query" consequence is not grounded.

2) Connector-bounded write path: toCatalogString (line 79) serializes arity from an OptionalInt (int), so any bucket(N) this connector writes is <= INT_MAX. Table-property arity is validated positive (DucklakeTableProperties.java:180). An overflowing value can only come from a foreign engine or a corrupt/hand-edited catalog.

3) Trusted-catalog contract: the PROJECT BRIEFING CONVENTIONS state "The connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths... general catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal." This finding is exactly the class of read-path catalog-value validation that is intentionally out of scope.

4) Consistent with existing design: the non-bucket branch (line 65) calls fromString -> valueOf, which already throws IllegalArgumentException on any unrecognized transform string. The class already propagates unchecked exceptions on malformed catalog input by design; the parseInt overflow is not a uniquely new hazard.

5) Semantically implausible: bucket arity is a hash-bucket count (typically 16-4096); a value above 2^31 is not a real DuckLake/Iceberg partition spec.

6) The suggested remedy is itself wrong: capping to `\d{1,10}` still matches 10-digit values like 4000000000 which exceed INT_MAX, so it wouldn't even prevent the overflow.

At most this is a low/trivial robustness nit against a trusted-catalog contract, not a medium correctness bug.

refuted.title: Missing parentheses around the domain conjunct when mixed with expression clauses
refuted.detail: When both `hasDomain` and `hasExpressions` are true, the domain clause is appended raw (line 70: `sql.append(domainClause.get())`) while expression clauses are wrapped in parentheses (line 77: `sql.append('(').append(clause).append(')')`). `DuckDbWhereClauseTranslator.toWhereClause` can return a multi-conjunct string like `("a" > 1 OR "a" < 0) AND "b" = 2`. If it does, the AND binding with the subsequent expression clauses is correct by operator precedence. However, the domain clause itself can already contain top-level `AND` (produced by `String.join(" AND ", conjuncts)` in `toWhereClause`), so the combined output is `col1 = 1 AND col2 = 2 AND (expr_clause)`. This is correct because AND is left-associative and all terms are conjuncts — but the asymmetry (domain unparenthesised, expressions parenthesised) is fragile: if `toWhereClause` ever emits an OR at the top level without wrapping it (currently it does wrap OR ranges, but multi-column OR would not be), the precedence would break silently.
refuted.suggestion: Wrap the domain clause in parentheses on append as well: `sql.append('(').append(domainClause.get()).append(')')`. Currently `toWhereClause` always returns either `FALSE`, a single column term, or an `AND`-joined list — but a defensive wrap costs nothing and removes the asymmetry.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
No real bug. The builder (DuckDbSelectSqlBuilder.java:66-79) joins the domain clause and expression clauses with top-level AND only; expression clauses are wrapped in parens (line 77), the domain clause is appended raw (line 70). The finding's own claim hinges on a hypothetical: that toWhereClause could emit an unwrapped top-level OR. Reading DuckDbWhereClauseTranslator.toWhereClause shows this cannot happen:

- Line 93: the top-level result is always String.join(" AND ", conjuncts) (or a single conjunct, or the bare token "FALSE"). The only top-level connective is AND.
- Every path that produces an OR wraps it in parentheses: line 125 `(valuesTerm OR name IS NULL)` and line 165 `(t1 OR t2 ...)`. Single-range terms are also self-wrapped (line 200 `("+sb+")`). So any conjunct that contains an OR is already parenthesized before being AND-joined.

Therefore `col1 = 1 AND col2 = 2 AND (expr)` binds correctly and there is no precedence hazard today. The "multi-column OR" failure mode the finding imagines is structurally impossible: a TupleDomain is by definition a conjunction of per-column domains, so cross-column OR is never representable on this path (it would arrive as a pushed expression instead). The finding itself concedes the current output is correct and frames the change as purely defensive ("a defensive wrap costs nothing"). That is a style/robustness preference, not a correctness defect — no input reachable through this code produces wrong SQL. The suggested wrap is harmless but optional; severity stays low at most, and the verdict is refuted because the claimed consequence cannot occur.

refuted.title: rollback throws on unknown transaction handle instead of tolerating it
refuted.detail: The Trino SPI Connector.java documents that 'exactly one of commit or rollback' is called per transaction, so normally this is fine. However, if a future code path calls commit() and then rollback() on the same handle (e.g., due to error recovery in an outer wrapper), rollback() throws IllegalArgumentException because the handle was already removed by commit(). The SPI says one or the other, but defensive connectors typically silently ignore rollback of an already-committed/absent transaction. The current behavior would surface as a noisy exception in Trino's error logs during any such scenario.
refuted.suggestion: In rollback(), change the null-check to a silent no-op: if (metadata == null) { return; } — there is nothing to roll back if the transaction is already gone.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The cited rollback() (lines 65-74) throws IllegalArgumentException if the transaction handle is already absent. The finding's harm scenario — commit() followed by rollback() on the same handle — cannot occur given the framework guarantee. The Trino SPI Connector.java in deps-reference (lines 233-239) states rollback "Will be called at most once and will not be called if commit is called." This is enforced by Trino's engine-side transaction state machine, not merely advisory prose. DucklakeConnector.commit/rollback (lines 128-137) are pure forwarders with no outer wrapper that could call both. So the only way to reach the throw is Trino itself violating its own documented contract, i.e. a genuine engine bug — exactly the case where a fail-loud IllegalArgumentException is the correct, conventional behavior (matching the begin/getMetadata/commit symmetry in this same class). Converting rollback to a silent no-op as suggested would mask real defects (double-rollback, rollback of a never-begun handle) for zero practical benefit. The claimed "noisy exception in error logs during any such scenario" rests on a scenario the framework precludes. Severity stays low but verdict is refuted: the code is correct as written and the suggestion would reduce, not improve, robustness.

refuted.title: BUCKET arity=0 not rejected — ArithmeticException on first write or read
refuted.detail: The compact constructor validates that a BUCKET field has arity present, but does not validate arity > 0. PartitionFieldSpec (the DDL entry point) has that check at line 38–39, but JdbcDucklakeCatalog.getPartitionSpecs() constructs DucklakePartitionField directly from catalog rows via parseCatalogTransform, which would accept bucket(0) without complaint. The arity then reaches DucklakePartitionComputer.computeBucket() which does (hash & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % arity — dividing by zero throws ArithmeticException mid-query with no clear message.
refuted.suggestion: Add 'if (transform == DucklakePartitionTransform.BUCKET && arity.isPresent() && arity.getAsInt() <= 0) { throw new IllegalArgumentException("BUCKET arity must be positive, got " + arity.getAsInt()); }' in the compact constructor, mirroring PartitionFieldSpec line 38–39.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The structural claim is accurate: DucklakePartitionField's compact constructor (lines 36-41) checks only arity.isEmpty() for BUCKET, not arity > 0, whereas PartitionFieldSpec has the positive-arity guard at lines 38-39. And JdbcDucklakeCatalog.getPartitionSpecs() (line 633) does construct DucklakePartitionField directly from catalog rows via parseCatalogTransform, bypassing PartitionFieldSpec. parseCatalogTransform (DucklakePartitionTransform.java:60-64) regex bucket\\((\\d+)\\) matches "bucket(0)" and parses arity=0 without complaint. The arity does flow into computeBucket (DucklakePartitionComputer.java:235: (hash & Integer.MAX_VALUE) % arity), which would throw ArithmeticException on % 0, reachable via DucklakeSplitManager:480/522 (read) and DucklakePagePartitioner:114/155 (write).

However, the consequence is not reachable through any legitimate workflow. The only writer that persists partition specs through this connector is the Trino DDL path, which is guarded TWICE: DucklakeTableProperties.parsePartitionField rejects arity<=0 (line 179-181) and PartitionFieldSpec rejects it again (38-39). So Trino can never store bucket(0). The bucket transform also semantically requires N>=1 in the DuckLake/Iceberg spec, so the DuckDB extension (the spec authority) won't write it either. A bucket(0) catalog row could only arise from a hand-corrupted or hostile catalog. The PROJECT BRIEFING explicitly states: "The connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths... general catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal." A bucket(0) partition_column value is exactly the kind of invalid catalog value the connector deliberately does not defensively validate. The practical impact is at most an ArithmeticException with a less-clear message instead of an IllegalArgumentException with a clearer one, on an already-invalid catalog — not a correctness defect under any supported usage. Adding the one-line guard would be a reasonable defensive nicety (cheap, mirrors an existing check), but it does not rise to a medium correctness bug given the trusted-catalog policy. Hence refuted, severity downgraded to low.

refuted.title: DucklakeView has no null-checks on mandatory String fields that are @Nullable from the DB
refuted.detail: DucklakeViewRecord.getDialect(), getSql(), and getViewName() are all @Nullable (the jOOQ-generated record marks them so and the schema column definitions lack nullable(false)). toDucklakeView() at JdbcDucklakeCatalog:2377-2379 passes them directly to the DucklakeView constructor without null guards, and the record has no compact constructor that rejects them. Consequences: (1) DucklakeMetadata.isViewAccessible() calls view.dialect().toLowerCase() (line 1495) — NPE on a null dialect; (2) view.viewName() is passed to SchemaTableName constructors (DucklakeMetadata:1301, 1309, 1355) — NPE or silent null SchemaTableName if viewName is null; (3) viewUuid can also arrive as a null String (JdbcDucklakeCatalog:2375 explicitly allows it) with no contract documented.
refuted.suggestion: Add a compact constructor to DucklakeView that calls requireNonNull on viewName, sql, and dialect (these are non-optional DB columns; if they are null in a row the catalog is corrupt and the error should be thrown at read time, not at first use). Keep viewUuid as Optional<String> rather than a nullable String if null is a legitimate value, or document and guard the null case at every call site.
refuted.verifierReasoning:
The mechanical facts are accurate: DucklakeViewRecord getters (getViewName/getDialect/getSql) are @Nullable (generated/.../DucklakeViewRecord.java:106-147), the jOOQ field defs use bare SQLDataType.VARCHAR with no nullable(false) (DucklakeViewTable.java:81-91), and toDucklakeView (JdbcDucklakeCatalog.java:2374-2382) passes them straight into the DucklakeView record, which has no compact constructor (DucklakeView.java:23-34). But the claimed high-severity consequences do not survive scrutiny.

(a) This is the documented project convention, not a view-specific oversight. Every converter follows the identical pattern: toDucklakeColumn passes r.getColumnName()/getColumnType() bare (2309-2310), toDucklakeSchema passes r.getSchemaName() (2342), toDucklakeTable passes r.getTableName() (2355). The orZero/Optional.ofNullable helpers are applied only to numeric and genuinely-optional columns. The PROJECT BRIEFING explicitly states: 'The connector assumes a trusted catalog on read paths... general catalog-value validation is intentionally minimal.' These mandatory columns can only be null in a corrupt catalog; the connector always writes them non-null (createView insert, 1535-1537) and the DuckLake spec defines them as mandatory.

(b) Consequence (2) is overstated/incorrect. Via getView the row is selected with VIEW_NAME.eq(viewName) (1028), so a returned row necessarily has non-null view_name — the getRequiredViewDefinition/dropView path is unreachable. Via listViews a null name would reach new SchemaTableName (DucklakeMetadata 1301/1309/1355), but SchemaTableName routes through checkNotEmpty (SchemaUtil.java:22-23) which throws a clear NPE — there is no 'silent null SchemaTableName' as claimed; the failure is loud and at read time.

(c) Consequence (1), NPE on view.dialect().toLowerCase() (DucklakeMetadata:1495), is real only on a corrupt catalog — exactly the case the trusted-catalog convention declines to defend, and it already fails loudly.

(d) Consequence (3) is a non-issue: viewUuid==null is explicitly handled (JdbcDucklakeCatalog:2372-2375) and never unsafely dereferenced; view_uuid is genuinely nullable.

Net: the lack of guards is real but matches an intentional, codebase-wide convention; the only failure mode is a loud NPE on a corrupt (untrusted) catalog, not silent data corruption. The high/correctness framing is refuted. The requireNonNull-in-compact-constructor suggestion is a reasonable low-severity hardening nitpick at most.

refuted.title: String comparison used to find min/max of discrete numeric predicate values produces wrong bounds
refuted.detail: extractDiscreteValueBounds finds the min/max of the discrete value set using String.compareTo on the normalized string representations. For numeric types, normalizePredicateValue just calls value.toString(), so integers like 9 and 10 are compared as strings: "9".compareTo("10") > 0, so 9 is selected as the max. The resulting PredicateBounds("9", "10") is then passed to findDataFileIdsInRange where parseStatValue parses both as BigDecimal and isWithinBounds uses upperBound=BigDecimal(9) to reject any data file whose min_stat >= 10. A file that only contains the value 10 would be incorrectly pruned despite 10 being in the predicate set. This causes silent wrong results (missing rows) on queries like WHERE col IN (9, 10) against tables where file stats don't overlap when bounded by "9".
refuted.suggestion: Normalize discrete values with the same type-aware parsing used downstream. Either sort the normalized strings after typed parsing (e.g. sort as BigDecimal for numeric types, LocalDate for dates), or convert each discrete value to a Comparable via the same parseStatValue logic and track the typed min/max. Alternatively, for DiscreteValues just use the typed span of the enclosing SortedRangeSet (call domain.getValues() as Ranges after forcing to ranges) instead of re-extracting from DiscreteValues directly.
refuted.verifierReasoning: The bug is real as written but unreachable for the types the finding claims it affects. extractDiscreteValueBounds (DucklakeSplitManager.java:387-405) is only invoked via the discreteValues branch of extractPredicateBounds (line 383). That branch is reached only when domain.getValues().getValuesProcessor().transform(...) dispatches to valuesFunction. In the Trino SPI, getValuesProcessor dispatches by value-set type: SortedRangeSet.getValuesProcessor().transform (deps-reference SortedRangeSet.java:521-537) ALWAYS calls rangesFunction.apply(getRanges()) — it never calls the discreteValues function. Only EquatableValueSet.getValuesProcessor (EquatableValueSet.java:219-233) routes to valuesFunction. ValueSet.of/copyOf/none/all (ValueSet.java:36-59) choose SortedRangeSet when type.isOrderable() and EquatableValueSet otherwise. I confirmed isOrderable()==true for AbstractIntType, AbstractLongType, DoubleType, DecimalType, VarcharType, BooleanType, UuidType, and VarbinaryType. So integers, decimals, doubles, dates, varchar, boolean, varbinary — every type isNumericType() in JdbcDucklakeCatalog (lines 2445-2458) treats as BigDecimal — are ALWAYS SortedRangeSet and ALWAYS take the typed ranges/getSpan() branch (lines 364-381), never the string-comparing discrete branch. The finding's concrete scenario (WHERE col IN (9,10) on an integer column, file containing 10 wrongly pruned) therefore cannot occur: that IN-list becomes a SortedRangeSet of point ranges and getSpan() does typed comparison. The only types that actually reach extractDiscreteValueBounds are genuinely non-orderable ones (JSON, HyperLogLog, IPADDRESS, spatial), which parseStatValue (lines 2423-2443) returns as raw strings (not BigDecimal), so the string min/max is self-consistent with the string stat comparison in isWithinBounds — and such types are stats-suppressed/degraded per the briefing anyway. The String.compareTo in extractDiscreteValueBounds is dead-defensive code for the numeric case, not a live wrong-results bug. Downgraded to low: a tidiness/robustness nit (could use typed comparison defensively) rather than a high-severity correctness defect.
